results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Every tove chortled at a rath. A borogove chortled at a borogove that chortled at a tove. Bungo chortled at a borogove. Bungo didn't snicker-snacked Zindle but it is not the case that Rafin chortled at Zindle. Every rath snicker-snacked Bungo just in case every borogove snicker-snacked Bungo.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. No toves chortled at a rath.
2. There is a tove that every rath chortled at.
3. No toves chortled at every rath.
4. A tove chortled at every rath.
5. Every tove chortled at every rath.
6. A tove chortled at a rath.
results.model_response:
Let  

T(x)  = “x is a tove”  
B(x)  = “x is a borogove”  
R(x)  = “x is a rath”  
C(x,y)= “x chortled at y”  
S(x,y)= “x snicker-snacked y”  

Premises

P1  Every object is exclusively a tove, a borogove, or a rath and each kind is non-empty.  
P2  ∀x (T(x) → ∃y (R(y) ∧ C(x,y)))  (Every tove chortled at a rath)  
P3  ∃x ( B(x) ∧ ∃y ( B(y) ∧ C(x,y) ∧ ∃z ( T(z) ∧ C(y,z) ) ) )  
     (A borogove chortled at a borogove that chortled at a tove)  
P4  ∃y ( B(y) ∧ C(Bungo , y) )  
P5  ¬S(Bungo , Zindle) and ¬C(Rafin , Zindle)  
P6  (∀x ( R(x) → S(x , Bungo) )) ↔ (∀x ( B(x) → S(x , Bungo) ))

What must follow from these?

Observe:

• By P1 there exists at least one tove, say a.  
• By P2 that tove a chortled at some rath b (R(b) ∧ C(a , b)).  

Therefore there certainly exists at least one pair (tove, rath) such that the tove chortled at the rath:

  ∃x∃y ( T(x) ∧ R(y) ∧ C(x , y) ).

That sentence is exactly statement 6.

Need any of the other candidate statements be true?

• 1 contradicts P2 outright, so it is not forced (indeed it is impossible).  
• 2, 3, 4 and 5 concern how many raths each tove chortled at.  
  P2 only guarantees “at least one” rath per tove; it does not rule out “all” raths, nor does it
  require it.  
  One can build a model of the premises in which no tove is chortled at by every rath, and a
  different model in which some (or even all) toves chortle at every rath.  
  Hence none of 2–5 is forced by the premises.

Conclusion

The only statement that must be true in every situation satisfying the given information is

6. A tove chortled at a rath.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. A rath snicker-snacked a rath that snicker-snacked a tove. Bungo chortled at every rath. Zindle snicker-snacked a rath.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. A rath chortled at Bungo.
2. Not all raths chortled at Bungo.
3. Bungo chortled at a rath.
4. No raths chortled at Bungo.
5. Zindle is a tove.
6. Every rath chortled at Bungo.
results.model_response:
Must-true statements are those that hold in every interpretation that satisfies the given facts.  
The facts tell us:

• There is at least one rath, one tove and one borogove.  
• Bungo chortled at every rath (∀rath r : Bungo chortled-at r).

Because at least one rath exists, Bungo necessarily chortled at (that) rath.  
Hence statement 3

3. Bungo chortled at a rath.

is true in every possible situation.

For the other statements we can build consistent counter-examples, so none of them is forced:

1. A rath chortled at Bungo – can be made true or false at will.  
2. Not all raths chortled at Bungo – false if we let every rath chortle at Bungo.  
4. No raths chortled at Bungo – false if we let one rath chortle at Bungo.  
5. Zindle is a tove – Zindle can be a rath, a borogove or a tove.  
6. Every rath chortled at Bungo – false if we let at least one rath stay silent.

Therefore the only statement that must be true is:

Statement 3.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Bungo is a borogove. Bungo chortled at Rafin. Rafin galumphed over Zindle. Not all toves chortled at Rafin only if not all toves are beamish. If every rath is mimsy, then Rafin is a tove only if no raths galumphed over Bungo.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Rafin chortled at every borogove.
2. Every borogove chortled at Rafin.
3. Rafin chortled at a borogove.
4. Not all borogoves chortled at Rafin.
5. No borogoves chortled at Rafin.
6. A borogove chortled at Rafin.
results.model_response:
Facts that are definitely given

1. Bungo is a borogove.
2. Bungo chortled at Rafin.

From 1 and 2 we can immediately infer

• There exists at least one borogove (namely Bungo) that chortled at Rafin.

That is exactly statement 6:

6. A borogove chortled at Rafin.

Nothing else in the information supplied lets us conclude anything about whether Rafin chortled at borogoves, or whether every / no / not-all borogoves chortled at Rafin; the two conditional sentences mention toves, raths, mimsy and beamish, but they do not add any unconditional fact about borogoves chortling. Therefore none of the other listed statements is forced to be true.

Hence the only statement that must be true is:

Statement 6.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Not all borogoves will whiffle. Bungo isn't a borogove just in case not all borogoves chortled at Bungo. Every rath gimbled and Bungo chortled at Bungo.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Every rath gimbled and not all borogoves will whiffle.
2. Every rath gimbled and no borogoves will whiffle.
3. Bungo will whiffle.
4. Bungo won't whiffle.
5. Not all raths will whiffle and only borogoves gimbled.
6. Bungo gimbled.
results.model_response:
The facts we are given are:

F1  Everything is (exclusively) a tove, a borogove or a rath, and there is at least one of each.

F2  Not all borogoves will whiffle.  
  (∃x)(Borogove(x) ∧ ¬Whiffle(x))

F3  Bungo isn’t a borogove iff not all borogoves chortled at Bungo.  
  (¬Borogove(Bungo)) ↔ ¬(∀x)(Borogove(x) → Chortle(x,Bungo))

F4  Every rath gimbled.  
  (∀x)(Rath(x) → Gimble(x))

F5  Bungo chortled at Bungo.  
  Chortle(Bungo,Bungo)

We now check each candidate statement.

1.  Every rath gimbled and not all borogoves will whiffle.  
    • “Every rath gimbled’’ is exactly F4.  
    • “Not all borogoves will whiffle’’ is exactly F2.  
    Both conjuncts are stipulated facts, so their conjunction is true in every
    admissible situation.  
    ⇒ Statement 1 must be true.

2.  Every rath gimbled and no borogoves will whiffle.  
    The first conjunct is true (F4), but F2 allows some borogoves to whiffle, so
    the second conjunct need not hold.  Hence the whole sentence need not be
    true.  
    ⇒ Not forced.

3.  Bungo will whiffle.  
4.  Bungo won’t whiffle.  
    The premises impose no constraint on Bungo’s whiffling.  We can build one
    model in which Bungo whiffles and another in which Bungo doesn’t.  
    ⇒ Neither 3 nor 4 is forced.

5.  Not all raths will whiffle and only borogoves gimbled.  
    “Only borogoves gimbled’’ is impossible because every rath gimbled (F4), and
    raths are not borogoves (F1).  Thus statement 5 is false in every model.  
    ⇒ Not forced (indeed, impossible).

6.  Bungo gimbled.  
    Bungo gimbles iff Bungo happens to be a rath (all raths gimbled) or gimbles
    for some other independent reason; the information given does not decide
    this universally.  
    ⇒ Not forced.

Therefore the only statement that must be true in every situation described is:

Statement 1.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. No toves snicker-snacked Rafin. A rath galumphed over itself. A borogove snicker-snacked a tove that snicker-snacked it. Bungo isn't a borogove only if every rath snicker-snacked Rafin.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Rafin is a tove.
2. Not all toves snicker-snacked Rafin.
3. Rafin snicker-snacked a tove.
4. Every tove snicker-snacked Rafin.
5. A tove snicker-snacked Rafin.
6. Rafin snicker-snacked every tove.
results.model_response:
The facts we are given can be formalised as follows  

1. Every individual is **exactly one** of: tove (T), borogove (B) or rath (R); each of the three kinds exists.  
2. No tove snicker-snacks Rafin: ∀x (T(x) → ¬S(x,Rafin)).  
3. Some rath galumphed over itself: ∃x (R(x) ∧ G(x,x)).  
4. Some borogove and some tove snicker-snack each other: ∃b,t (B(b) ∧ T(t) ∧ S(b,t) ∧ S(t,b)).  
5. If Bungo is **not** a borogove then every rath snicker-snacks Rafin:  
 ¬B(Bungo) → ∀x (R(x) → S(x,Rafin)).

We ask which of the six candidate statements is true in **every** structure that satisfies 1–5.

•  Statement 2: “Not all toves snicker-snacked Rafin.”  
   Because there is at least one tove (fact 1) and, by fact 2, **no** tove snicker-snacks Rafin, it is necessarily false that *all* toves do so.  
   Hence statement 2 is true in every model.

To show that none of the other statements is forced, it suffices to build models in which each of them fails while all the facts 1–5 hold.  Three small models do the job:

Model A  
 Rafin is a tove; he never snicker-snacks himself.  
 There is another borogove (b) and a rath (r).  
 b and Rafin snicker-snack each other; r galumphs over itself.  
 Bungo is a borogove.  
 Facts 1–5 hold, but  
 • statement 3 (“Rafin snicker-snacked a tove”) is false, and  
 • statements 4, 5, 6 are false because no tove snicker-snacks Rafin.

Model B  
 Rafin is a borogove; there is a tove (t) and a rath (r).  
 A second borogove (b) and t snicker-snack each other; r galumphs over itself.  
 No tove snicker-snacks Rafin.  
 Bungo is a borogove.  
 Facts 1–5 hold, but statements 1, 3, 4, 5, 6 all fail.

Model C  
 Rafin is a rath; there are a tove (t) and a borogove (b).  
 b and t snicker-snack each other; Rafin galumphs over himself.  
 No tove snicker-snacks Rafin.  
 Bungo is a borogove.  
 Facts 1–5 hold, but statements 1, 3, 4, 5, 6 all fail.

Because each of statements 1, 3, 4, 5 and 6 is false in at least one admissible model, none of them is logically forced by the premises.

Conclusion  
The only statement that must be true is

2. Not all toves snicker-snacked Rafin.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. There is a tove that every borogove galumphed over. If Rafin didn't snicker-snacked Rafin, then not all toves galumphed over Bungo only if no raths galumphed over Bungo. A borogove chortled at Rafin and only toves are mimsy.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. A tove galumphed over a borogove.
2. Every tove galumphed over a borogove.
3. Every tove galumphed over every borogove.
4. No toves galumphed over every borogove.
5. A tove galumphed over every borogove.
6. A borogove galumphed over a tove.
results.model_response:
The only information we are told about “galumphing” that places an unconditional existential
requirement on the relation is  

“There is a tove that every borogove galumphed over.”

Formally:  
∃t (Tove(t) ∧ ∀b (Borogove(b) → Galumphed-over(b , t)))

Together with the fact that there is at least one borogove, this guarantees the existence of at
least one ordered pair 〈borogove , tove〉 in the galumphing relation.

That is exactly what statement 6 asserts:

6. A borogove galumphed over a tove.

Hence statement 6 must be true in every model that satisfies the given premises.

• Statement 1 need not be true: we can build a model in which no tove galumphs over any
  borogove (all galumphing runs only from every borogove to the one special tove).

• Statements 2, 3, 5 are stronger versions of 1 and are therefore not forced either.

• Statement 4 (“No toves galumphed over every borogove.”) is not forced; we can create a
  model in which the special tove galumphs over all borogoves as well, making 4 false.

Because the other five sentences can be false in some model that respects all the premises,
none of them is logically forced.

Final answer: 6 only.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. A borogove chortled at a tove. Every tove is uffish unless not all raths chortled at Bungo. If Bungo isn't a borogove, then Bungo is a borogove. If Rafin didn't chortle at Rafin, then Bungo didn't snicker-snacked Rafin. Not all toves snicker-snacked Zindle but it is not the case that Bungo chortled at Bungo.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. No borogoves chortled at Bungo.
2. Every borogove chortled at Bungo.
3. Not all borogoves chortled at Bungo.
4. Bungo chortled at every borogove.
5. Bungo chortled at a borogove.
6. A borogove chortled at Bungo.
results.model_response:
A key part of the data is the pair of facts

• “If Bungo isn’t a borogove, then Bungo is a borogove.”  
  This is equivalent to simply “Bungo is a borogove.”

• “It is not the case that Bungo chortled at Bungo.”  
  So Bungo (who is a borogove) did not chortle at Bungo.

Because there exists a borogove – namely Bungo – that did not chortle at Bungo, it follows that

 Not all borogoves chortled at Bungo.

That is statement 3.  
None of the other six statements is forced:

1. “No borogoves chortled at Bungo” can be false in a model where some other borogove does chortle at Bungo.  
2. “Every borogove chortled at Bungo” is outright impossible (Bungo himself did not).  
4. “Bungo chortled at every borogove” is impossible because Bungo didn’t chortle at himself.  
5. “Bungo chortled at a borogove” may or may not be true, depending on whether he chortles at some other borogove.  
6. “A borogove chortled at Bungo” may or may not be true, depending on the behaviour of other borogoves.

Therefore the only statement that must be true in every situation satisfying the given premises is:

Statement 3 – Not all borogoves chortled at Bungo.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Every tove galumphed over every rath that galumphed over every rath. Every rath will whiffle or Zindle didn't chortle at Rafin. A rath galumphed over Bungo unless every tove snicker-snacked Zindle. If every rath chortled at Rafin, then a borogove snicker-snacked Bungo only if Rafin won't whiffle. Not all toves snicker-snacked Zindle and a rath galumphed over Zindle.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Bungo galumphed over every rath.
2. Every rath galumphed over Bungo.
3. A rath galumphed over Bungo.
4. Not all raths galumphed over Bungo.
5. Bungo galumphed over a rath.
6. No raths galumphed over Bungo.
results.model_response:
First translate the information into first-order logic.

Let  

 T(x) = “x is a tove”  
 Bv(x) = “x is a borogove”  
 R(x) = “x is a rath” (exactly one of these three for every object, and each sort is non-empty)  

 G(x,y) = “x galumphed over y”  
 W(x) = “x will whiffle”  
 C(x,y) = “x chortled at y”  
 S(x,y) = “x snicker-snacked y”  

Named individuals: Bungo (b), Zindle (z) and Rafin (r).

Facts supplied:

F1.  ∀t∀ρ[(T(t) ∧ ρ is a rath that galumphed over every rath) → G(t,ρ)]  
     (in symbols: ∀t∀ρ[(T(t) ∧ R(ρ) ∧ ∀ρ′(R(ρ′)→G(ρ,ρ′))) → G(t,ρ)])

F2.  (∀ρ(R(ρ)→W(ρ)))  ∨  ¬C(z,r)

F3.  “A rath galumphed over Bungo unless every tove snicker-snacked Zindle.”  
     Unless = “if not … then …”, so  
     ¬∀t(T(t)→S(t,z))  →  ∃ρ(R(ρ) ∧ G(ρ,b))

F4.  [∀ρ(R(ρ)→C(ρ,r))]  →  [(∃β(Bv(β)∧S(β,b))) → ¬W(r)]

F5.  ¬∀t(T(t)→S(t,z))          (Not all toves snicker-snacked Zindle.)

F6.  ∃ρ(R(ρ) ∧ G(ρ,z))        (A rath galumphed over Zindle.)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Immediate consequence

Because fact F5 makes the antecedent of F3 true, F3 yields

 ∃ρ(R(ρ) ∧ G(ρ,b))   (∗)

i.e. “Some rath galumphed over Bungo.”

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Testing the six candidate conclusions

1.  b galumphed over every rath  
2.  every rath galumphed over b  
3.  a rath galumphed over b  
4.  not all raths galumphed over b  
5.  b galumphed over a rath  
6.  no rath galumphed over b  

Conclusion (3) is exactly statement (∗), which we have proved must hold.

To see that none of the other five statements is forced, build two sample models, both satisfying F1–F6.

A.  Universe has one rath ρ₁ with G(ρ₁,b); then “every rath galumphed over Bungo” is true.  
    Therefore statement 2 can be true, statement 4 false, 6 false, 1 false, 5 false.

B.  Universe has two raths ρ₁,ρ₂ with G(ρ₁,b) but ¬G(ρ₂,b); then “not all raths galumphed over Bungo” is true.  
    Therefore statement 4 can be true, statement 2 false, 6 false, 1 false, 5 false.

Since statements 1, 2, 4, 5 and 6 can be either true or false while all the given information is satisfied, none of them is forced.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Final answer

The only statement that must be true is

3.  A rath galumphed over Bungo.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Not all raths will whiffle but it is not the case that Zindle didn't snicker-snacked Zindle. Not all toves snicker-snacked Zindle but it is not the case that Bungo didn't snicker-snacked Bungo. Not all borogoves will whiffle and every rath snicker-snacked Zindle.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. A rath snicker-snacked Zindle.
2. Zindle snicker-snacked every rath.
3. Zindle snicker-snacked a rath.
4. Not all raths snicker-snacked Zindle.
5. Zindle didn't snicker-snacked Zindle and a rath snicker-snacked Zindle.
6. No raths snicker-snacked Zindle.
results.model_response:
Let  

T(x): x is a tove  
B(x): x is a borogove  
R(x): x is a rath  
W(x): x will whiffle  
S(x,y): x snicker-snacked y  

The information given translates as:

1. ∀x [T(x) ∨ B(x) ∨ R(x)] and the three classes are non-empty.  
2. ∃x [R(x) ∧ ¬W(x)].  
3. S(Zindle , Zindle).                         (because “it is not the case that Zindle didn’t …”)  
4. ∃x [T(x) ∧ ¬S(x , Zindle)].  
5. S(Bungo , Bungo).  
6. ∃x [B(x) ∧ ¬W(x)].  
7. ∀x [R(x) → S(x , Zindle)].               (“every rath snicker-snacked Zindle”)

Now check each proposed conclusion.

1. A rath snicker-snacked Zindle:  
   There is at least one rath (non-empty classes) and every rath satisfies S(x , Zindle) (7).  
   Therefore ∃x [R(x) ∧ S(x , Zindle)].  
   Statement 1 is necessarily true.

2. Zindle snicker-snacked every rath:  
   Requires ∀x [R(x) → S(Zindle , x)].  Nothing given relates Zindle as actor toward raths, so this need not hold.  
   Not necessarily true.

3. Zindle snicker-snacked a rath:  
   Requires ∃x [R(x) ∧ S(Zindle , x)].  Again, no information forces this; it may be false in some models.  
   Not necessarily true.

4. Not all raths snicker-snacked Zindle:  
   Negates statement 7, which we are explicitly given to be true.  
   Necessarily false.

5. Zindle didn’t snicker-snack Zindle and a rath snicker-snacked Zindle:  
   First conjunct contradicts fact 3.  Cannot be true.

6. No raths snicker-snacked Zindle:  
   Contradicts fact 7.  Cannot be true.

Therefore, the only statement that must be true is:

1. A rath snicker-snacked Zindle.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. A tove snicker-snacked every borogove. Every tove is uffish or every tove snicker-snacked Zindle. No toves are uffish or every tove snicker-snacked Zindle.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Zindle is uffish.
2. Every tove snicker-snacked Zindle.
3. Not all toves snicker-snacked Zindle.
4. Zindle snicker-snacked a tove.
5. No toves snicker-snacked Zindle.
6. Zindle snicker-snacked every tove.
results.model_response:
Let  

T(x)  = “x is a tove”  
B(x)  = “x is a borogove”  
R(x)  = “x is a rath” (the three kinds are exclusive and all non-empty)  
U(x)  = “x is uffish”  
S(x,y)= “x snicker-snacked y”  
z     = Zindle  

Premises  

1. ∃t (T(t) ∧ ∀y (B(y) → S(t,y)))                             (some tove snicker-snacked every borogove)  
2. (∀x (T(x) → U(x))) ∨ (∀x (T(x) → S(x,z)))                  (Premise B)  
3. (∀x (T(x) → ¬U(x))) ∨ (∀x (T(x) → S(x,z)))                 (Premise C)  

Because there is at least one tove, “all toves are uffish” and “no toves are uffish” cannot both be true.

• If “all toves are uffish” is true, Premise C can be satisfied only by its second disjunct, so  
  ∀x (T(x) → S(x,z)) is true.

• If “no toves are uffish” is true, Premise B can be satisfied only by its second disjunct, so  
  ∀x (T(x) → S(x,z)) is true.

• If neither “all toves are uffish” nor “no toves are uffish” is true (some toves are uffish and some are not), both disjuncts on the left fail, so again each premise forces  
  ∀x (T(x) → S(x,z)).

Thus, in every possible case the following MUST hold:  

  Every tove snicker-snacked Zindle.

No other condition is forced by the premises (Zindle’s own properties and actions are unrestricted, and the universal snicker-snacking just derived refutes “no toves snicker-snacked Zindle” and “not all toves snicker-snacked Zindle”).

Therefore the only statement that must be true is

2. Every tove snicker-snacked Zindle.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Every tove snicker-snacked every borogove. Zindle didn't snicker-snacked Zindle but it is not the case that Rafin isn't a tove. No borogoves are mimsy only if not all raths snicker-snacked Rafin. Not all toves snicker-snacked Zindle and only toves are mimsy.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Zindle and Rafin are mimsy.
2. Zindle is mimsy.
3. Zindle isn't a borogove.
4. Rafin is mimsy.
5. Zindle isn't mimsy.
6. Zindle is a borogove.
results.model_response:
The premises can be formalised as follows.

Sorts (mutually exclusive and jointly exhaustive, each non-empty)
 T(x): x is a tove  B(x): x is a borogove  R(x): x is a rath

Predicates  
 S(x,y): ‘x snicker-snacked y’  M(x): x is mimsy

Premises  
1. ∀x∀y (T(x) ∧ B(y) → S(x,y))              Every tove snicker-snacked every borogove.  
2. ¬S(Zindle, Zindle).  
3. T(Rafin).                                 (it is not the case that Rafin isn’t a tove)  
4. (∀y (B(y) → ¬M(y))) → (¬∀z (R(z) → S(z, Rafin))).  
5. ¬∀x (T(x) → S(x, Zindle)).               Not all toves snicker-snacked Zindle.  
6. ∀x (M(x) → T(x)).                        Only toves are mimsy.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
What must be true?

A key consequence of (1) and (5) is:

• Zindle cannot be a borogove.  
 Reason:  If Zindle were a borogove, then by (1) every tove would
 snicker-snack Zindle, contradicting (5).  
 Therefore ¬B(Zindle) is forced.

Nothing else is forced:

• Zindle may be a tove (and may or may not be mimsy), or Zindle may be a rath.  
• Rafin being a tove tells us nothing about Rafin’s being mimsy, because “only toves are mimsy” (6) is one-way.

Two consistent models show the other statements are not compelled.

Model A: Zindle a rath, no one mimsy → Zindle not mimsy, Rafin not mimsy.  
Model B: Zindle a tove and mimsy, Rafin not mimsy.  

Both satisfy every premise, yet they give opposite truth-values to
statements 2, 4 and 5.  Hence none of those statements is necessary.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Therefore the only statement that must be true is

3. Zindle isn’t a borogove.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Bungo didn't snicker-snacked Zindle and Rafin chortled at Zindle. Not all raths galumphed over Rafin and Rafin didn't snicker-snacked Bungo. Not all borogoves galumphed over Zindle just in case no raths chortled at Zindle. If every rath chortled at Rafin, then Zindle didn't galumph over Bungo only if not all toves galumphed over Bungo.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. If every rath snicker-snacked Bungo, then Zindle didn't galumph over Bungo only if every rath galumphed over Rafin.
2. Bungo didn't snicker-snacked Zindle and every rath galumphed over Rafin.
3. Zindle didn't galumph over Bungo only if not all raths snicker-snacked Rafin.
4. Bungo didn't snicker-snacked Zindle and not all raths galumphed over Rafin.
5. If Zindle didn't galumph over Bungo, then not all raths snicker-snacked Rafin.
6. Rafin didn't snicker-snacked Bungo and a rath galumphed over Zindle.
results.model_response:
Let us translate the information into first-order logic.

Predicates  
T(x) x is a tove  
B(x) x is a borogove  
R(x) x is a rath  

Exactly one of T, B or R holds of every object and there is at least one of each.

Binary relations  
S(x,y) x snicker-snacked y  
C(x,y) x chortled at y  
G(x,y) x galumphed over y  

Constants:  Bungo = b, Zindle = z, Rafin = r.

Premises

1.  ¬S(b , z)             (Bungo didn’t snicker-snack Zindle)

2.  C(r , z)              (Rafin chortled at Zindle)

3.  ¬∀x ( R(x) → G(x , r) )     (Not all raths galumphed over Rafin)

4.  ¬S(r , b)             (Rafin didn’t snicker-snack Bungo)

5.  (∃x ( B(x) ∧ ¬G(x , z) ))  ↔  (∀x ( R(x) → ¬C(x , z) ))  
    (‘Not all borogoves galumphed over Zindle’ iff ‘no rath chortled at Zindle’)

6.  (∀x ( R(x) → C(x , r) )) → ( ¬G(z , b) → ¬∀x ( T(x) → G(x , b) ) )  
    (If every rath chortled at Rafin, then:  
     if Zindle didn’t galumph over Bungo, not every tove galumphed over Bungo.)

We have to discover which numbered statements follow logically from these premises.

Statement 4  
“Bungo didn’t snicker-snack Zindle and not all raths galumphed over Rafin.”

The first conjunct is premise 1; the second is premise 3.  
Both are explicitly given, so statement 4 is true in every model that satisfies the premises.

To show that every other candidate need not be true, it suffices to build one model of the
premises in which the candidate is false.  
Here is such a model.

Universe: {b , z , r , d}  
Categories: T(b), T(z); B(r); R(d).  
(No element satisfies more than one of T, B, R.)

Interpret the remaining predicates as follows (only the needed facts are listed; everything
not forced may be taken false).

• S(b , z) = false                (premise 1)  
• S(r , b) = false                (premise 4)  
• S(d , b) = true                              (so every rath snicker-snacks Bungo)  

• C(r , z) = true                (premise 2)  
• For the only rath d: C(d , z) = false and C(d , r) = false.

• G(d , r) = false                             (not all raths galumphed over Rafin)  
• G(z , b) = false                             (Zindle doesn’t galumph over Bungo)  
• G(r , z) = false                             (chosen so that premise 5 holds)  

With these assignments every premise 1–6 is satisfied, yet:

• Statement 1 is false:  
  The antecedent “every rath snicker-snacked Bungo’’ is true (d is the only rath and
  S(d , b) is true) but the consequent  
  “Zindle didn’t galumph over Bungo only if every rath galumphed over Rafin’’ is false,
  because ¬G(z , b) is true while not every rath galumphed over Rafin.

• Statements 2, 3, 5 and 6 are also false in this model (details omitted for brevity).

Since each of the statements 1, 2, 3, 5 and 6 can be false while all premises are true,
none of them is entailed.  Statement 4, being the conjunction of two stated premises,
is entailed.

Final answer  
The only statement that must be true is statement 4.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Every borogove chortled at every borogove. Every rath chortled at every tove that chortled at it. A borogove galumphed over a rath that galumphed over it. A rath galumphed over every borogove that galumphed over it. A borogove chortled at Zindle if and only if Rafin chortled at Bungo.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. There is not a borogove that every borogove chortled at.
2. No borogoves chortled at every borogove.
3. No borogoves chortled at a borogove that chortled at it.
4. Zindle is a tove.
5. No borogoves chortled at a borogove.
6. A borogove chortled at every borogove.
results.model_response:
Necessary facts supplied in the description  

1. “Every borogove chortled at every borogove.”  
   ⇒ For all b₁,b₂ (if b₁ is a borogove and b₂ is a borogove then b₁ chortled-at b₂).

2–5  (about raths, galumphing and the special ‘Zindle/Rafin/Bungo’ condition) do not alter the truth of 1 in any way that could prevent or forbid borogove-to-borogove chortling.

From fact 1 we can read off two immediate logical consequences:

A. For every pair of borogoves b₁,b₂, b₁ chortled at b₂.  
B. Hence for every individual borogove b₂ there certainly is at least one (indeed every) borogove that chortled at b₂.  
C. Consequently there exists at least one borogove that chortled at every borogove (in fact every borogove has that property).

Now test each candidate statement.

1. “There is not a borogove that every borogove chortled at.”  
   – Contradicts B above; therefore it need not be true (indeed it is false).

2. “No borogoves chortled at every borogove.”  
   – Contradicts C; therefore it need not be true (it is false).

3. “No borogoves chortled at a borogove that chortled at it.”  
   – Since all pairs of borogoves chortle at one another (from 1), this is violated; hence it is not necessarily true.

4. “Zindle is a tove.”  
   – Nothing in the premises forces Zindle to belong to one particular species; Zindle could consistently be a borogove, a rath or a tove.  So this statement is not forced to be true.

5. “No borogoves chortled at a borogove.”  
   – Directly contradicted by fact 1; not necessarily true.

6. “A borogove chortled at every borogove.”  
   – Guaranteed by C; it is unavoidable that at least one (indeed all) borogove(s) have chortled at every borogove.

Therefore, the only statement that must be true under the given conditions is statement 6.

Final answer: 6 only.

3. “No toves chortled at Rafin and every rath chortled at Zindle.”  
   – This is exactly two of the premises we are given.  
   – Hence it must be true.

4. “Rafin and Bungo will burble.”

6. “Zindle won’t burble.”

F2. ¬chortle(Zindle , Bungo).

F4. (∀x [tove(x) → chortle(x , Bungo)]) → (∀x [rath(x) → chortle(x , Rafin)]).

A statement “must be true’’ iff it is true in every structure that satisfies F1–F4.

-------------------------------------------------
1.  Zindle didn’t chortle at Bungo just in case no borogoves are beamish.  
   “Just in case’’ = ↔.  
   Left side is guaranteed by F2, right side by F3; hence in every model both
   sides are true, so the biconditional (T ↔ T) is necessarily true.  
   Statement 1 must be true.

-------------------------------------------------
To show that the remaining statements are *not* forced, construct a model M that
satisfies F1–F4 but makes each of them false.

Universe: {z, b, r, g}  
   z = Zindle, b = Bungo, r = Rafin, g = some borogove.

   Beamish: ∅ (nobody is beamish) – this satisfies F3.  
   Chortling: the only forbidden fact is ¬chortle(z , b); let every other ordered
   pair chortle unless stated otherwise.  This satisfies F2; F4 is satisfied
   because the antecedent “every tove chortled at Bungo’’ is false
   (Bungo himself is a tove and we did not stipulate chortle(b , b)).  

With this model:

2. “Zindle and Rafin are beamish’’ is false.  
3. “Zindle and Bungo are beamish’’ is false.  
4. The antecedent “no borogoves are beamish’’ is true, and we can include
   chortle(z , z) while keeping some borogove (g) that does **not** chortle at
   Bungo, so the consequent fails and the conditional is false.  
5. Bungo is not beamish, yet no borogove chortles at Zindle, so the biconditional
   is false.  
6. Zindle is not beamish, so statement 6 is false.

Thus none of 2–6 is forced by F1–F4.

-------------------------------------------------
Final answer  

The only statement that must be true in this situation is

1. Zindle didn’t chortle at Bungo just in case no borogoves are beamish.

Statements 2, 3, 4, 5 and 6 are not guaranteed by the given information.

results.model_response:
First write the information in a form that makes its logical force plain.

A. Classification  
   – Everything is (exclusively) a tove, a borogove, or a rath.  
   – There is at least one of each kind.

B. Additional facts  
   1. Either  
        (i) Not every borogove snicker-snacked Rafin,      or  
        (ii) Bungo didn’t chortle at Zindle.                     (P ∨ Q)
   2. Rafin gimbled.                                             (G)  
      Not every tove will whiffle.                              (¬∀T W)  
   3. Bungo didn’t snicker-snack Bungo.                          (¬SBB)  
      No borogove will whiffle.                                 (∀B ¬W)

The six candidate conclusions are:

(1)  (∀B M) ↔ ¬∀B M                              (Every B is mimsy ⇔ not every B is mimsy)  
(2)  W (Zindle)  
(3)  G (Rafin) ∧ (∀x (W x → B x))                (Rafin gimbled & only borogoves whiffle)  
(4)  W (Zindle) ∧ W (Bungo)  
(5)  W (Zindle) ∧ W (Rafin)  
(6)  G (Rafin) ∧ ∀B ¬W x                         (Rafin gimbled & no borogove will whiffle)

Step 1 – What is forced by the premises?

•  G (Rafin) is explicitly stated.  
•  ∀B ¬W x (“no borogove will whiffle”) is explicitly stated.

Hence the conjunction of those two sentences, i.e. statement (6), must be true in every model that satisfies the premises.

Step 2 – Show that none of the other five sentences is forced.

Build a model that satisfies all the premises but falsifies each sentence in turn.

A convenient model:

Objects: {r1, t1, b1}  
Kinds:   r1 a rath, t1 a tove, b1 a borogove  
Whiffling:  t1 whiffles; r1, b1 do not.  
Mimsy:   choose values as needed (e.g. b1 not mimsy).  
Other verbs:  choose values so that clause (i) of P ∨ Q and ¬SBB are true.

This model satisfies

– Rafin = r1 gimbled.  
– Not every tove whiffles (r1 is not a tove, t1 doesn’t whiffle).  
– No borogove (b1) whiffles.  
– P ∨ Q is made true by taking “Bungo didn’t chortle at Zindle”.  
– ¬SBB is made true by stipulating it.

Now check the six statements in the model.

(1) ‘Every borogove is mimsy ⇔ Not every borogove is mimsy’ is automatically false, because the two sides can never have the same truth-value when there is at least one borogove.  
(2) Zindle will whiffle – undetermined; in the model Zindle (say b1) does not whiffle, so (2) is false.  
(3) The embedded universal ‘only borogoves will whiffle’ is false because t1 (a tove) whiffles, so (3) is false.  
(4) Bungo and Zindle will whiffle – false.  
(5) Zindle and Rafin will whiffle – false.  
(6) Rafin gimbled & no borogove will whiffle – true.

Because all the premises hold while (1)–(5) can be false, none of those five sentences is forced. Sentence (6) is forced, since it is built directly out of two premises.

Both hold in every model of the premises, so their conjunction – statement 5 – must be true.

The other statements are not forced

To show a statement is not logically forced it suffices to present
two concrete models that satisfy all the premises, one in which the
statement is true and one in which it is false.

Let the domain contain the individuals
Zindle (Z), Bungo (B), Rafin (R), a rath r1, a borogove g1 and a tove t1.
Class‐membership: Z and t1 are toves, g1 is a borogove, r1 is a rath, B and R are toves (any assignment compatible with 1 works).

Model A (“Zindle burbles”)

Burble:  Z, g1                (B and R do not burble)  
Gimble:  r1 only              (so some rath gimbled, no borogove gimbled)  
Chortle: every tove chortles at Bungo; g1 chortles at Rafin; nobody chortles at Zindle.

All premises hold, hence so does statement 2, while statements 1, 3, 4 and 6 are false here.

Model B (“Zindle doesn’t burble”)

Same as Model A except: Z does not burble and every borogove burbles.  
Premise 2 is satisfied because,
with Z not burbling, the antecedent of its internal conditional
(“not all toves chortled at Bungo”) is false – we again let every tove
chortle at Bungo – so the whole conditional is true.  
Here statement 3 is true while 2, 1, 4 fail; choosing g1 to gimble as well
makes the left side of statement 6 false while the right side is still
false, so the biconditional comes out true, showing even 6 can vary.

Thus none of 1, 2, 3, 4 or 6 is compelled by the premises.

Conclusion

The only statement that must be true in every situation satisfying the given information is statement 5.

Final answer: 5

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Every rath snicker-snacked every tove. A borogove snicker-snacked a borogove that snicker-snacked a tove. Zindle snicker-snacked a tove.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Every tove snicker-snacked a rath.
2. There is a tove that every rath snicker-snacked.
3. No toves snicker-snacked every rath.
4. A tove snicker-snacked every rath.
5. A tove snicker-snacked a rath.
6. Every tove snicker-snacked every rath.
results.model_response:
Let  

T = the set of all toves (non-empty)  
B = the set of all borogoves (non-empty)  
R = the set of all raths   (non-empty)

“x snicker-snacked y” is a binary relation S(x , y).

Given facts

F1. ∀r∈R  ∀t∈T :  S(r , t)                       (every rath snicker-snacks every tove)  
F2. ∃b₁,b₂∈B  ∃t∈T :  S(b₁ , b₂) ∧ S(b₂ , t)     (a borogove snicker-snacked a borogove that snicker-snacked a tove)  
F3. ∃z  ∃t∈T : S(z , t)                          (Zindle snicker-snacked a tove)

Now test each statement.

1. Every tove snicker-snacked a rath.  
   Not forced.  Model: one tove t, one rath r, one borogove b with  
   S(r , t), S(b , b), S(b , t).  
   No tove snicker-snacks any rath, yet all facts hold.  So statement 1 need not be true.

2. There is a tove that every rath snicker-snacked.  
   From F1, for every tove t and every rath r we have S(r , t).  
   Because T is non-empty, pick any t∈T; all raths snicker-snack it.  
   Hence ∃t∈T  ∀r∈R : S(r , t) is guaranteed.  Statement 2 must be true.

3. No toves snicker-snacked every rath.  
   Not forced.  Extend the previous model by adding S(t , r); then that tove t
   snicker-snacks every rath and all facts still hold.  So statement 3 need not be true.

4. A tove snicker-snacked every rath.  
   Not forced (reverse of 3).  In the first model above no tove snicker-snacks any rath, so statement 4 can be false.

5. A tove snicker-snacked a rath.  
   Also not forced: in the first model no tove ever snicker-snacks a rath.  Hence statement 5 can be false.

6. Every tove snicker-snacked every rath.  
   Clearly not entailed; same counter-model as for 1 suffices.

Conclusion:  
The only statement that must be true in every situation satisfying the given facts is statement 2.

Final answer: 2

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. No toves chortled at a borogove. A tove snicker-snacked itself. Zindle didn't snicker-snacked Zindle and Rafin didn't chortle at Bungo. A rath chortled at Zindle only if no borogoves are mimsy. If Bungo didn't snicker-snacked Bungo, then Bungo is beamish only if Zindle chortled at Zindle.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. There is not a borogove that every tove chortled at.
2. Every tove chortled at every borogove.
3. Every tove chortled at a borogove.
4. A tove chortled at a borogove.
5. A borogove will burble and no borogoves will burble.
6. A tove chortled at every borogove.
results.model_response:
The first given fact is

“No toves chortled at a borogove.”

That means  
for every creature t that is a tove and every creature b that is a borogove,  
chortle(t , b) is false.

Consequences:

• For any particular borogove b, no tove chortles at b at all, let alone every tove.  
  Therefore it is impossible for there to be “a borogove that every tove chortled at.”

• Because no tove chortles at any borogove, every statement that asserts the existence
  of (or universal chortling to) such a pair is not forced to be true.

Now examine the six candidate conclusions.

1. “There is not a borogove that every tove chortled at.”  
   This follows directly from the fact above, so it must be true.

2. “Every tove chortled at every borogove.” – contradicted by the given fact.

3. “Every tove chortled at a borogove.” – likewise contradicted.

4. “A tove chortled at a borogove.” – likewise contradicted.

5. “A borogove will burble and no borogoves will burble.”  
   The premises say nothing about burbling, so this self-contradictory pair is not forced.

6. “A tove chortled at every borogove.” – impossible by the given fact.

Hence, the only statement that must be true in the described situation is

Statement 1.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Every borogove is mimsy. A borogove chortled at every tove that chortled at it. If not all toves chortled at Bungo, then Bungo didn't snicker-snacked Bungo only if every borogove chortled at Bungo.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. No borogoves are mimsy.
2. Bungo is mimsy.
3. Not all borogoves are mimsy if and only if a borogove is mimsy.
4. Only borogoves are mimsy.
5. Not all borogoves are mimsy.
6. A borogove is mimsy.
results.model_response:
Premises (restated)

1. The universe is partitioned into three, non-overlapping kinds: toves, borogoves and raths, and there is at least one of each kind.

2. Every borogove is mimsy:      ∀x (Borogove(x) → Mimsy(x)).

3. (Chortling reciprocity)  ∀x∀y [Tove(x) ∧ Borogove(y) ∧ Chortled(x,y) → Chortled(y,x)].

4. The long conditional about Bungo and snicker-snacking involves no claim about being mimsy, a tove, a borogove, etc.  It is irrelevant to the truth–values of the six statements we are asked about, so it can be ignored for this purpose.

Now evaluate each candidate statement.

1. No borogoves are mimsy.
   This is the negation of premise 2, so it is necessarily false.

2. Bungo is mimsy.
   We are never told what kind of creature Bungo is; Bungo could be a tove, a borogove or a rath.  Hence this need not be true.

3. Not all borogoves are mimsy ↔ a borogove is mimsy.
   Premise 2 guarantees that ALL borogoves are mimsy, so “Not all borogoves are mimsy” is always false.
   Because there is at least one borogove (premise 1) and all borogoves are mimsy (premise 2), “a borogove is mimsy” is always true.
   Thus the biconditional is always false (false ↔ true).  It is not a necessary truth.

4. Only borogoves are mimsy.
   Premise 2 gives one direction (borogove ⇒ mimsy) but says nothing about mimsy ⇒ borogove.  Toves or raths could also be mimsy, so this is not guaranteed.

5. Not all borogoves are mimsy.
   Directly contradicts premise 2, so necessarily false.

6. A borogove is mimsy.
   From premise 1 we know a borogove exists, and from premise 2 every borogove is mimsy, hence at least one mimsy borogove exists.  This statement is necessarily true.

Conclusion

Of the six statements, the only one that must be true in every situation satisfying the given premises is:

6. A borogove is mimsy.

  – Every tove snicker-snacks (and is snicker-snacked by) at least one borogove.

• 2 (“No toves snicker-snacked a borogove”) contradicts P1 directly, so it cannot be true.

• 3 (“There is a tove that every borogove snicker-snacked”):  
   Let T1, T2 be toves; B1, B2 borogoves; have B1↔T1 and B2↔T2 only.

Statement 6.

results.model_response:
Let  

T(x) = “x is a tove”  
B(x) = “x is a borogove”  
R(x) = “x is a rath” ( T, B, R are mutually exclusive and jointly exhaustive; there is at least one of each)  
U(x) = “x is uffish”  
S(x , y) = “x snicker-snacked y”

2. ¬∀x ( T(x) → S(x , Zindle) )    (“Not all toves snicker-snacked Zindle”.)  
3. ∀x[ R(x) → ∀y( B(y) ∧ ∀z(T(z)→S(y , z)) → S(x , y) ) ].

We examine each candidate sentence.

1. Zindle is a tove.  
2. Zindle isn’t a tove.  

Nothing supplied forces Zindle into any one of the three species, so neither (1) nor (2) is guaranteed.

3. Bungo is uffish iff no toves snicker-snacked Zindle.  

Left side is true (U(Bungo)); the right side need not be—there may be some toves that did snicker-snack Zindle. Hence this biconditional is not forced to be true.

4. Bungo is uffish iff not all toves snicker-snacked Zindle.  

The right-hand condition, ¬∀x( T(x) → S(x , Zindle) ), is exactly the second given fact, so it is true in every admissible situation; the left-hand side, U(Bungo), is also given as true. Because both halves of the biconditional are always true, the whole “iff” statement is necessarily true.

5. Bungo isn’t uffish.  
6. Zindle isn’t uffish.  

Both contradict the explicit information that Bungo and Zindle are uffish, so they cannot be true.

Conclusion: the only statement that must be true is

4. Bungo is uffish just in case not all toves snicker-snacked Zindle.

results.model_response:
Let  

• rath(x), tove(x), borogove(x) be the three mutually-exclusive kinds (each non–empty).  
• S(x,y) mean “x snicker-snacked y”.

Given facts

F1  ∀x [rath(x) → ∃y (rath(y) ∧ S(x,y) ∧ S(y,x))]          (each rath snicker-snacks a rath that snicker-snacks it)  
F2  ∃r (rath(r) ∧ S(r,Zindle))                              (some rath snicker-snacks Zindle)  
F3  ∀b [borogove(b) → S(b,Zindle)]                          (every borogove snicker-snacks Zindle)  
(other sentences concern “uffish”, “chortled”, etc.; they put no further constraints on S among raths).

We must decide which of the six listed statements is forced (true in all models that satisfy F1–F3).

Statement 1.  ∃x∃y (rath(x) ∧ rath(y) ∧ S(x,y))  
F1 already gives, for every rath x, a rath y with S(x,y); hence such a pair certainly exists.  
So 1 is forced (must be true).

Statement 2.  ∃x [rath(x) ∧ ∀y (rath(y) → S(x,y))]  
Not forced.  Consider a model with two raths r1,r2 and let S(r1,r2) and S(r2,r1) be the only S-pairs among raths.  
F1 is satisfied, yet neither rath snicker-snacks both raths.  Hence 2 can be false.

Statement 3.  ∀x∀y [(rath(x) ∧ rath(y)) → S(x,y)]  
The same two-rath model shows this can be false, so 3 is not forced.

Statement 4.  ¬∃x∃y (rath(x) ∧ rath(y) ∧ S(x,y))  
This is the negation of statement 1, which we have just proved must be true.  
Therefore 4 is necessarily false (cannot hold in any model).

Statement 5.  ¬∃x [rath(x) ∧ ∀y (rath(y) → S(x,y))]  
Let the universe contain exactly one rath r1 and put S(r1,r1).  
F1 is met (y = r1), yet r1 snicker-snacks every rath, so the statement is false in that model.  
Hence 5 is not forced.

Statement 6.  ∃y [rath(y) ∧ ∀x (rath(x) → S(x,y))]  
In the two-rath model above, neither rath is snicker-snacked by both raths, so 6 can be false and is not forced.

Conclusion:  
The only statement that must be true in every situation satisfying the given information is statement 1.

Final answer: 1 only.

6. Zindle galumphed over every tove – possible, but not required.

Therefore the only statement that must be true is:

Statement 4 – “Not all toves galumphed over Zindle.”

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. No toves galumphed over Bungo but it is not the case that Rafin didn't galumph over Zindle. Not all raths galumphed over Rafin but it is not the case that Rafin isn't a tove. Rafin will whiffle unless every tove galumphed over Rafin. If every rath chortled at Rafin, then not all borogoves galumphed over Bungo. If not all toves will whiffle, then Rafin won't whiffle only if not all toves galumphed over Bungo.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Rafin galumphed over Bungo.
2. Bungo didn't galumph over Rafin.
3. Zindle and Rafin will whiffle.
4. Zindle will whiffle.
5. Bungo galumphed over Rafin.
6. Rafin didn't galumph over Bungo.
results.model_response:
Observe the two key premisses

• Rafin is a tove.  
• No tove galumphed over Bungo.

Because Rafin is a member of the class “tove”, the second premiss applies to him directly; therefore

  Rafin did not galumph over Bungo.  (¬ G(Rafin , Bungo))

This makes statement 6 necessarily true.

None of the other proposed statements is forced by the premisses:

1 / 5  Whether Rafin or Bungo galumphed over the other is left open except for the single prohibition just derived; so 1 is impossible and 5 is optional.

2  The premisses place no restriction on Bungo’s galumphing over Rafin; it may be true or false.

3 / 4  The rules involving “whiffling” do not guarantee that Rafin—or Zindle—will (or won’t) whiffle in every admissible model; both possibilities remain.

Hence the only statement that must be true in every situation satisfying the given information is

6. Rafin didn't galumph over Bungo.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Every rath snicker-snacked a tove. A borogove chortled at a borogove that chortled at a rath. If every rath snicker-snacked Bungo, then Bungo didn't snicker-snacked Bungo only if every rath chortled at Rafin.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. A tove snicker-snacked a rath.
2. Every tove snicker-snacked every rath.
3. Every tove snicker-snacked a rath.
4. A tove snicker-snacked every rath.
5. No toves snicker-snacked every rath.
6. A rath snicker-snacked a tove.
results.model_response:
Let  

T(x)  =  “x is a tove”  
B(x)  =  “x is a borogove”  
R(x)  =  “x is a rath”  

S(x,y) =  “x snicker-snacked y”  
C(x,y) =  “x chortled at y”  

Facts supplied

F1.  The domain is exhaustively and exclusively divided into toves, borogoves and raths and each of the three kinds exists.

F2.  ∀x ( R(x) → ∃y ( T(y) ∧ S(x , y) ) )  
  (Every rath snicker-snacked a tove.)

F3.  ∃b₁ b₂ r ( B(b₁) ∧ B(b₂) ∧ R(r) ∧ C(b₂ , r) ∧ C(b₁ , b₂) )  
  (A borogove chortled at a borogove that chortled at a rath.)

F4.  ( ∀x ( R(x) → S(x , Bungo) ) ) →  
  ( ¬S(Bungo , Bungo) → ∀x ( R(x) → C(x , Rafin) ) )  
  (The conditional about Bungo and Rafin.)

From F2 and the existence of at least one rath (F1) it follows immediately that

∃x ∃y ( R(x) ∧ T(y) ∧ S(x , y) ).               (1)

Statement (1) is exactly statement 6:

6.  A rath snicker-snacked a tove.

So 6 is compelled by the information.

To see that none of the other five statements is forced, construct a very small model satisfying F1–F4 but falsifying any chosen statement 1–5.  For example:

Objects:  
 t  (a tove)  r  (a rath)  b  (a borogove)

Relations:  
 S(r , t) holds; no other snicker-snacking happens.  
 C(b , b) and C(b , r) hold; no other chortling happens.

Let Bungo = b and Rafin = r.

•  F1 is satisfied by construction.  
•  F2 is satisfied because the only rath, r, snicker-snacks the tove t.  
•  F3 is satisfied by b chortling at itself and at r.  
•  F4 holds because its antecedent “every rath snicker-snacked Bungo” is false (r does not snicker-snack b).

In this model:

– Statement 1 is false (no tove snicker-snacks any rath);  
– Statements 2, 3, 4 are false;  
– Statement 5 is false if we simply add S(t , r);  
– Statement 6 is true.

Hence the only statement that must be true in every situation satisfying the given facts is

6. A rath snicker-snacked a tove.

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Zindle and Bungo are uffish. Bungo didn't chortle at Bungo and a rath galumphed over Zindle. If Rafin is a tove, then every rath is beamish only if every rath chortled at Bungo.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Zindle is uffish but it is not the case that Bungo didn't chortle at Bungo.
2. Zindle and Rafin are uffish.
3. Rafin and Zindle are uffish.
4. Zindle is uffish and Bungo didn't chortle at Bungo.
5. Rafin is uffish.
6. Zindle isn't uffish.
results.model_response:
The information we are told is:

1. Uffish(Zindle) and Uffish(Bungo).  
2. ¬ ChortleAt(Bungo, Bungo).  
3. ∃x (Rath(x) ∧ GalumphedOver(x, Zindle)).  
4. If Tove(Rafin) then (∀x (Rath(x) → Beamish(x))) → (∀x (Rath(x) → ChortleAt(x, Bungo)).  
5. Everything is exactly one of tove / borogove / rath (and all three kinds exist).

Now check each candidate statement.

1. Uffish(Zindle) ∧ ¬(¬ ChortleAt(Bungo,Bungo))  
   This demands ChortleAt(Bungo,Bungo), contradicting fact 2.  Cannot be true in any model.

2. Uffish(Zindle) ∧ Uffish(Rafin)  
   We know the first half, but nothing forces Uffish(Rafin).  May be false.

3. Uffish(Rafin) ∧ Uffish(Zindle)  
   Same problem as (2).

4. Uffish(Zindle) ∧ ¬ ChortleAt(Bungo,Bungo)  
   Both conjuncts are explicitly given, so (4) is true in every model.

5. Uffish(Rafin)  
   Not forced; may be false.

6. ¬ Uffish(Zindle)  
   Directly contradicts fact 1; always false.

Therefore the only statement that must be true in the situation described is:

4.  Zindle is uffish and Bungo didn't chortle at Bungo.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Bungo or Rafin are beamish. If not all borogoves are mimsy, then Bungo galumphed over Zindle only if every borogove chortled at Bungo. Only borogoves are beamish but it is not the case that Bungo isn't mimsy.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. A borogove is beamish.
2. No borogoves are beamish.
3. Not all borogoves are beamish.
4. Every borogove is beamish.
5. Only borogoves are beamish and not all borogoves are beamish.
6. Zindle is mimsy.
results.model_response:
Premises restated in logical form  

P1. Tove ∨ Borogove ∨ Rath (mutually exclusive) and each class is non-empty.  
P2. Bungo ∨ Rafin is Beamish.      (at least one of the two is beamish)  
P3. If ¬(all Borogove are Mimsy) then (Bungo galumphed Zindle → every Borogove chortled Bungo).  
P4. Only Borogove are Beamish.     (Beamish ⇒ Borogove)  
P5. ¬(Bungo isn’t Mimsy).          (Bungo is Mimsy)

What the premises force about “beamish”

• From P2 there exists at least one beamish creature.  
• From P4 every beamish creature is a borogove.  
⇒ Therefore at least one borogove is beamish.

No other premise puts any restriction on which or how many borogoves are beamish, so all of the following remain possible:

– Exactly one borogove (say Bungo) is beamish.  
– Every borogove is beamish.  
– Some, but not all, borogoves are beamish.

Testing the candidate conclusions

1. A borogove is beamish.  
   Guaranteed true by the reasoning above. (Must be true.)

2. No borogoves are beamish.  
   Impossible, because we have just shown at least one is. (Not forced.)

3. Not all borogoves are beamish.  
   Could be true in some models and false in others. (Not forced.)

4. Every borogove is beamish.  
   Could be true in some models and false in others. (Not forced.)

5. Only borogoves are beamish and not all borogoves are beamish.  
   First half is always true, second half needn’t be, so the conjunction is not forced.

6. Zindle is mimsy.  
   No premise mentions Zindle’s mimsy-ness, so this is not forced.

Conclusion

The only statement that must be true in every situation satisfying the premises is statement 1.

Final Answer: 1 only.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. No borogoves chortled at Zindle. A borogove is uffish just in case not all toves snicker-snacked Zindle. A borogove chortled at Zindle just in case Bungo isn't beamish.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Bungo is uffish.
2. Zindle is uffish.
3. Bungo isn't beamish.
4. Zindle and Bungo are uffish.
5. Bungo is beamish.
6. Zindle isn't uffish.
results.model_response:
The three given facts can be formalised as follows (B = “is a borogove”, C = “chortled at Zindle”, U = “is uffish”, S = “snicker-snacked Zindle”, BM = “Bungo is beamish”).

1. ∀x (Bx → ¬Cx)   “No borogoves chortled at Zindle.”

2. ∀x (Bx → (Ux ↔ ¬∀y(Ty → Sy))) “Borogove x is uffish iff not all toves snicker-snacked Zindle.”
 (The right-hand side is a single global statement, independent of x.)

3. ∀x (Bx → (Cx ↔ ¬BM)) “Borogove x chortled at Zindle iff Bungo isn’t beamish.”

There is at least one borogove, call it b.  
For that borogove:

• From (1): ¬Cb.  
• From (3): Cb ↔ ¬BM.

Because “↔” is a biconditional, the only way Cb ↔ ¬BM can be true when Cb is false is for ¬BM to be false – i.e. BM must be true.

So BM (Bungo is beamish) is forced by the premises.

Nothing in the premises fixes the truth-values of “Bungo is uffish”, “Zindle is uffish”, or their negations; all of those can be made true or false in different models that still satisfy the three given facts.  
Hence no other listed statement is forced.

Therefore the single statement that must be true is:

5. Bungo is beamish.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Every rath chortled at a borogove that chortled at it. Zindle gyred unless every borogove chortled at Bungo. A rath is beamish and Rafin isn't uffish.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. A borogove chortled at every rath.
2. No borogoves chortled at every rath.
3. Every borogove chortled at every rath.
4. Every borogove chortled at a rath.
5. A borogove chortled at a rath.
6. No borogoves chortled at a rath.
results.model_response:
Premises (symbolically)

1.  ∀r (Rath(r) → ∃b (Borogove(b) ∧ C(r , b) ∧ C(b , r)))
     – each rath chortles at, and is chortled at by, some borogove.

2.  (¬∀b (Borogove(b) → C(b , Bungo))) → Gyred(Zindle)
     – irrelevant to the rath–borogove chortling pattern.

3.  ∃r (Rath(r) ∧ Beamish(r)) and ¬Uffish(Rafin)
     – merely tell us that at least one rath exists (already guaranteed).

The question-statements concern only the relation
   C(b , r) : “borogove b chortled at rath r”.

What the premises force

Because at least one rath exists, pick such an rath r₀.
By premise 1 there is a borogove b₀ with C(r₀ , b₀) ∧ C(b₀ , r₀).
Hence C(b₀ , r₀) holds – i.e. some borogove chortled at some rath.

This is exactly statement 5:
5. A borogove chortled at a rath.  → MUST be true.

Showing the other statements are not forced

To show a statement is not necessary, it suffices to build a model of the
premises in which the statement is false.

• Statement 1 (“A borogove chortled at every rath”).
  Model: two raths r₁,r₂; two borogoves b₁,b₂.
         C(r₁ , b₁) & C(b₁ , r₁);  C(r₂ , b₂) & C(b₂ , r₂); no other C-facts.
  Premises satisfied.  No single borogove chortles at both raths → statement 1 false.

• Statement 2 (“No borogoves chortled at every rath”).
  Model: one rath r; one borogove b with mutual chortling C(b , r) & C(r , b).
  Here b chortles at every rath (there is only one) → statement 2 false.

• Statement 3 (“Every borogove chortled at every rath”).
  The previous two-rath model already violates this, so it is not necessary.

• Statement 4 (“Every borogove chortled at a rath”).
  Add to the two-rath model a third borogove b₃ that chortles at nobody.
  Premises still satisfied (requirements concern only raths), so statement 4 is false.

• Statement 6 (“No borogoves chortled at a rath”).
  Contradicted directly by premise 1, so it cannot be true in any model that
  satisfies the premises.

Conclusion

The only statement that must follow from the given information is

5. A borogove chortled at a rath.

All the others can be true in some compliant situations and false in others.

results.model_response:
Premises  

1. Every object is (exclusively) a tove, a borogove or a rath, and each of those kinds exists.  
2. Rafin isn’t uffish.  
3. Zindle is beamish and Rafin is beamish.  
4. No tove snicker-snacked a rath.  

Only premises 2 and 3 mention Rafin or Zindle’s being uffish/beamish, so they are the only ones relevant to the six candidate statements.

• From premise 2 we have ¬ uffish(Rafin).  
• From premise 3 we have beamish(Rafin) and beamish(Zindle).  
• We are told nothing about Zindle’s being uffish or not.  
• Nothing else in the premises links the categories (tove, borogove, rath) or the
“snicker-snack” relation to the properties “uffish” or “beamish”.

Now examine each statement.

1. “Rafin is uffish.”  
   Contradicts premise 2, so it is false; therefore it is not forced to be true.

2. “Zindle and Rafin are uffish.”  
   Also contradicts premise 2, so not forced to be true.

3. “Rafin isn’t uffish but it is not the case that Rafin isn’t beamish.”  
   This is (¬ uffish(Rafin)) ∧ ¬(¬ beamish(Rafin)) ≡ (¬ uffish(Rafin)) ∧ beamish(Rafin).  
   Both conjuncts are exactly what premises 2 and 3 state, so the whole statement is true in every model that satisfies the premises. It must be true.

4. “Zindle isn’t uffish.”  
   The premises leave Zindle’s uffishness open; this could be either true or false, so it is not forced to be true.

5. “Zindle is uffish.”  
   Same reason as #4—undetermined—so it is not forced to be true.

6. “Rafin isn’t uffish and Rafin isn’t beamish.”

Therefore, the only statement that must be true given the premises is:

Statement 3.

results.question:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Only borogoves are beamish and not all raths galumphed over Rafin. Bungo didn't chortle at Bungo but it is not the case that Rafin isn't a tove. Zindle isn't a borogove unless no borogoves are beamish.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Rafin is beamish.
2. Zindle and Bungo are beamish.
3. Zindle and Rafin are beamish.
4. Zindle isn't beamish.
5. Zindle is beamish.
results.model_response:
We translate the information into simple logical clauses.

A. Every creature is exactly one of: tove, borogove or rath; each class is non-empty.  
B. Only borogoves are beamish. (Formally: Beamish(x) → Borogove(x).)  
C. ¬∀x (Rath(x) → GalumphedOverRafin(x)). (Irrelevant to “beamish”.)  
D. Rafin is a tove. (because ¬(¬Tove(Rafin))).  
E. “Zindle isn't a borogove unless no borogoves are beamish.”

   “A unless B” ≡ “If not B then A”.  
   So: If some borogove is beamish, then Zindle is not a borogove.  
   Contrapositive: If Zindle is a borogove, then no borogoves are beamish.

Now examine Zindle’s possible status.

Case 1: Some borogove is beamish.  
        ⇒ By E, Zindle is not a borogove.  
        ⇒ By B (contrapositive), Zindle is not beamish.

Case 2: No borogove is beamish.  
        ⇒ Then nothing at all is beamish (because only borogoves ever can be).  
        ⇒ Zindle is not beamish.

In every logically permissible case, Zindle is not beamish.  
No other statement in the list is forced (for example, Rafin is a tove, hence certainly not beamish, so statement 1 fails, and the other compound statements depend on Bungo’s undetermined status).

Therefore the single statement that must be true is:

4. Zindle isn't beamish.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. A rath chortled at a borogove that chortled at a rath. Rafin chortled at every borogove. No toves snicker-snacked Zindle just in case every rath snicker-snacked Bungo. If not all toves galumphed over Zindle, then Bungo galumphed over Zindle only if not all toves galumphed over Bungo. If Zindle didn't galumph over Bungo, then every rath galumphed over Rafin only if no raths galumphed over Bungo.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. A borogove chortled at Rafin.
2. Not all borogoves chortled at Rafin.
3. No borogoves chortled at Rafin.
4. Rafin chortled at a borogove.
5. Every borogove chortled at Rafin.
6. Zindle is a tove.
results.model_response:
The information that is guaranteed to be true is that  

• Rafin chortled at every borogove, and  
• there is at least one borogove.

From those two facts alone it follows that there is at least one specific borogove that Rafin chortled at – namely, whichever borogove you choose from the non-empty set of borogoves.  
So

 4.  “Rafin chortled at a borogove”

must be true.

Nothing else is forced.

• A borogove need not chortle at Rafin (we can build a model in which none do), so 1, 3 and 5 are not forced.  
• Because we can also build a model in which every borogove does chortle at Rafin, statement 2 (“Not all borogoves chortled at Rafin”) is not forced either.  
• Zindle’s species is never determined by the premises; Zindle can consistently be taken to be a rath, a borogove or a tove, so 6 is not forced.

Therefore the only statement that must be true in every situation satisfying the given conditions is

 4.  Rafin chortled at a borogove.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Not all toves gimbled. No toves galumphed over Bungo and Rafin isn't a tove. A rath galumphed over Bungo unless not all toves will whiffle.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Bungo galumphed over a tove.
2. Every tove galumphed over Bungo.
3. A tove galumphed over Bungo.
4. Bungo galumphed over every tove.
5. Not all toves galumphed over Bungo.
6. Rafin will whiffle.
results.model_response:
Premises

P1. Every individual is (exclusively) a tove, a borogove, or a rath, and there is at least one of each.
P2. Not all toves gimbled.                     (¬∀x (Tove(x) → Gimbled(x)))
P3. No tove galumphed over Bungo.             (∀x (Tove(x) → ¬Galumphed-Over(x,Bungo)))
P4. Rafin is not a tove.                      (¬Tove(Rafin))
P5. If all toves will whiffle, then a rath galumphed over Bungo.  
    ( (∀x (Tove(x) → Will-Whiffle(x))) → ∃y (Rath(y) ∧ Galumphed-Over(y,Bungo)) )

Required-truth test for each statement

1. “Bungo galumphed over a tove.”  
   Nothing given concerns Bungo’s actions toward others.  Could be true, could be false.

2. “Every tove galumphed over Bungo.”  
   P3 says no tove galumphed over Bungo and we know at least one tove exists (P1).  
   Therefore this statement is false in the actual situation; it certainly is not forced to be true.

3. “A tove galumphed over Bungo.”  
   Directly contradicted by P3, so it is impossible rather than necessary.

4. “Bungo galumphed over every tove.”  
   Again, the premises say nothing about Bungo’s galumphing, so the truth of this sentence is completely undetermined.

5. “Not all toves galumphed over Bungo.”  
   Because P3 tells us NO toves did, and P1 guarantees the existence of at least one tove, it follows that it is **necessarily** true that not all toves galumphed over Bungo.

6. “Rafin will whiffle.”  
   The premises give no information about whether Rafin (who is a borogove or a rath) will or will not whiffle, so this statement is not forced.

Conclusion

The only statement that must be true is statement 5.

Answer: 5 only.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Zindle and Rafin are mimsy. If Zindle is mimsy, then a tove galumphed over Bungo. Rafin is a tove and no borogoves will whiffle.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Every tove is mimsy.
2. No toves are mimsy.
3. Zindle will whiffle.
4. Not all toves are mimsy.
5. A tove is mimsy.
6. Only toves are mimsy.
results.model_response:
Premises

1. Every creature is exactly one of: tove, borogove or rath, and at least one of each exists.  
2. Zindle is mimsy.  
3. Rafin is mimsy.  
4. If Zindle is mimsy, then a tove galumphed over Bungo.  
   (Since Zindle is mimsy, this conditional’s consequent is true.)  
5. Rafin is a tove.  
6. No borogove will whiffle.   (Borogove(x) → ¬Whiffle(x))

What the premises force:

• Because Rafin is both a tove (5) and mimsy (3), we have  
  ∃x (Tove(x) ∧ Mimsy(x)) – “A tove is mimsy”.

Nothing else is compelled:

• All-toves-are-mimsy (statement 1) is not forced: we can add another tove that is not mimsy without violating any premise.  

• No-toves-are-mimsy (statement 2) is impossible (contradicts Rafin), so it is certainly not forced true.  

• Zindle-will-whiffle (statement 3) is not forced: make Zindle a borogove and, by premise 6, he will not whiffle; that satisfies all premises.  

• Not-all-toves-are-mimsy (statement 4) is not forced: in a model with only one tove (Rafin) all toves are mimsy.  

• Only-toves-are-mimsy (statement 6) is not forced: let Zindle be a borogove; he is mimsy but not a tove, yet no premise forbids that.

Therefore the only statement that must be true is:

5. A tove is mimsy.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Rafin or Zindle are mimsy. Every tove chortled at every borogove. A borogove chortled at a borogove that chortled at a rath.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. No toves chortled at a borogove.
2. No toves chortled at every borogove.
3. Every borogove is mimsy just in case not all borogoves are mimsy.
4. Every tove chortled at a borogove.
5. There is a tove that every borogove chortled at.
6. Not all raths galumphed over Rafin and every rath galumphed over Rafin.
results.model_response:
Only statement 4 is forced to be true.

Reasoning:

• “Every tove chortled at every borogove” is one of the given facts.  
  Hence each tove certainly chortled at at least one borogove, so 4 (“Every tove chortled at a borogove”) follows directly.

• 1 contradicts the given fact (it says no tove ever chortled at a borogove), so it cannot be a necessary truth.

• 2 is also contradicted by the fact that each tove chortled at every borogove; there do exist toves that chortled at every borogove.

• 3 uses “just in case” (iff) to equate “all borogoves are mimsy” with its own negation “not all borogoves are mimsy”, an equivalence that is always false; therefore it is certainly not forced to be true.

• 5 is not guaranteed: nothing given requires borogoves to chortle at any tove, let alone all borogoves at the same tove.

• 6 is an explicit contradiction (“Not all raths … and every rath …”) about an unrelated predicate; there is no reason for it to hold, and in fact it cannot hold.

Therefore the sole statement that must be true in the described situation is statement 4.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. A rath snicker-snacked Zindle and a rath galumphed over Zindle. Every tove snicker-snacked Zindle unless every tove galumphed over Rafin. Zindle didn't galumph over Bungo only if Rafin snicker-snacked Rafin. Not all toves gyred if and only if every rath snicker-snacked Bungo. No borogoves are beamish just in case every rath snicker-snacked Bungo.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. No borogoves are beamish unless only toves gyred.
2. Zindle gyred.
3. Not all toves gyred just in case no borogoves are beamish.
4. No borogoves are beamish or only toves gyred.
5. Not all borogoves are beamish and every tove gyred.
6. Zindle and Rafin gyred.
results.model_response:
First write the three abbreviations we will need.

ER  =  Every rath snicker-snacked Bungo.  
NAG =  Not all toves gyred.  
NB  =  No borogoves are beamish.

Two of the given premisses are

P5  NAG  iff  ER  
P6  NB   iff  ER

Because a biconditional is transitive, from P5 and P6 we may chain the equivalences:

NAG  iff  ER  iff  NB ⇒ NAG  iff  NB.        (★)

So the statement

Not all toves gyred just in case no borogoves are beamish.

which is exactly the biconditional NAG ↔ NB, follows unavoidably from the premisses.

To see that none of the other proposed statements is forced, build a counter-model that satisfies all the premisses but falsifies each of them.

Example model (only the needed facts are listed):

Categories  
Zindle: tove  Rafin: rath  Bungo: borogove  Tove2: tove  

Snicker-snacks  
Rafin → Zindle  Zindle → ( none )  No rath snicker-snacks Bungo  

Galumphs  
Rafin → Zindle  Zindle → Bungo  

Gyres  
All toves gyre; Bungo also gyres; Rafin does not gyre  

Beamish  
Bungo is beamish; nobody else is beamish  

This makes  
ER false NAG false NB false,  
so all premisses, including the two biconditionals P5 and P6, are true, yet

1. (NB unless only-toves-gyre) is false,  
2. Zindle gyred – could be made false in another model,  
4. NB ∨ only-toves-gyre is false,  
5. (¬all borogoves beamish) ∧ (all toves gyre) is false,  
6. Zindle and Rafin gyred is false.

Hence none of 1,2,4,5,6 is forced.

Conclusion:  

The only statement that must be true in every situation satisfying the premisses is

3. Not all toves gyred just in case no borogoves are beamish.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Zindle didn't chortle at Rafin. Rafin galumphed over a tove. No toves galumphed over Bungo and not all raths galumphed over Rafin.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Bungo galumphed over a tove.
2. Not all toves galumphed over Bungo.
3. Every tove galumphed over Bungo.
4. A tove galumphed over Bungo.
5. Bungo galumphed over every tove.
6. Zindle is a tove.
results.model_response:
The only premise that speaks about toves and Bungo is  

  No toves galumphed over Bungo.  (∀x)[Tove(x) → ¬Galumphed(x , Bungo)]

Because there is at least one tove (the problem states every object is a tove, borogove or rath and that there is at least one of each), the above sentence immediately yields

  Not all toves galumphed over Bungo.

That is exactly statement 2, so 2 must be true in every situation that satisfies the premises.

All the other proposed statements can be false in some model that still satisfies the premises:

1. Bungo galumphed over a tove … not forced; could be true or false.  
3. Every tove galumphed over Bungo … contradicted by the premise (so it is necessarily false, not true).  
4. A tove galumphed over Bungo … contradicted by the premise (necessarily false).  
5. Bungo galumphed over every tove … not forced; could be true or false.  
6. Zindle is a tove … Zindle could just as well be a borogove or a rath.

Therefore the single statement that must be true is:

Statement 2.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Every rath is mimsy. There is not a rath that every tove galumphed over. A borogove galumphed over Rafin unless every tove snicker-snacked Zindle. No borogoves snicker-snacked Bungo only if Rafin snicker-snacked Rafin. A rath is beamish but it is not the case that Bungo is a rath.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Zindle and Rafin are mimsy.
2. Only raths are mimsy.
3. No raths are mimsy.
4. A rath is mimsy.
5. Zindle is mimsy.
6. Not all raths are mimsy.
results.model_response:
From the information given we know the following for certain:

• Everything belongs to exactly one of the three classes tove, borogove or rath, and there is at least one member of each class.  
• Every rath is mimsy ( “Every rath is mimsy.” ).

Because there is at least one rath and every rath is mimsy, it follows unavoidably that

∃x (x is a rath ∧ x is mimsy).

That is exactly statement 4:  “A rath is mimsy.”

None of the other data force any individual (Zindle, Rafin, etc.) to be in the class of raths, nor do they forbid toves or borogoves from being mimsy.  Hence each of the other listed sentences can be made true in some models of the premises and false in others; they are not logically entailed by the information provided.

Therefore the only statement that must be true in every situation satisfying the premises is:

4. A rath is mimsy.
