results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. A tove chortled at a borogove. Every tove snicker-snacked a rath that snicker-snacked it. If Bungo galumphed over Zindle, then every rath galumphed over Rafin only if no raths galumphed over Bungo.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. A tove snicker-snacked a rath.
2. No toves snicker-snacked a rath.
3. Every tove snicker-snacked every rath.
4. There is a tove that every rath snicker-snacked.
5. No toves snicker-snacked every rath.
6. A tove snicker-snacked every rath.
results.model_response:
Let  

T(x) – “x is a tove”  
B(x) – “x is a borogove”  
R(x) – “x is a rath”  
S(x,y) – “x snicker-snacked y”

Premises  

P1  Everything is exclusively a tove, a borogove, or a rath and each class is non-empty.  
P2  A tove chortled at a borogove.  (irrelevant to snicker-snacking)  
P3  Every tove snicker-snacked a rath that snicker-snacked it.  
      Formalising the English pattern “Every … V-ed a … that V-ed it” gives  
      ∀x [T(x) → ∃y (R(y) ∧ S(y,x) ∧ S(x,y))].                        (1)  
P4  The statement about galumphing concerns a different relation and never affects snicker-snacking, so it can be ignored here.

Consequence of (1)

Because there is at least one tove (P1) and for every tove there is a rath with which it is mutually snicker-snacking (1), it follows immediately that

∃x∃y (T(x) ∧ R(y) ∧ S(x,y)),

i.e. some tove snicker-snacked some rath.

This makes statement 1 necessarily true.

Checking the other candidate conclusions

• 2.  “No toves snicker-snacked a rath.”  
   Contradicted by the consequence derived above; therefore it is not necessarily true.

• 3.  “Every tove snicker-snacked every rath.”  
   Take two raths, have each tove mutually snicker-snack only one of them; (1) is satisfied but 3 is false.  Hence 3 is not necessary.

• 4.  “There is a tove that every rath snicker-snacked.”  
   Using the same counter-model as for 3, different raths can snicker-snack different toves, so 4 need not hold.

• 5.  “No toves snicker-snacked every rath.”  
   If there happens to be exactly one rath, the tove required by (1) will snicker-snack every rath (the single one), so 5 can fail.

• 6.  “A tove snicker-snacked every rath.”  
   In a model with two raths where each tove snicker-snacks only one of them, 6 is false while (1) is still satisfied.  Hence 6 is not necessary.

Therefore the only statement that must be true in every situation satisfying the premises is Statement 1.

Final answer: 1 only.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Not all toves chortled at Bungo but it is not the case that Bungo didn't snicker-snacked Zindle. Not all toves snicker-snacked Zindle if and only if Rafin chortled at Bungo. Bungo chortled at Bungo and every rath snicker-snacked Zindle.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Bungo didn't chortle at Bungo and no toves chortled at Bungo.
2. Not all toves chortled at Bungo and no toves chortled at Bungo.
3. Not all toves chortled at Bungo but it is not the case that Bungo chortled at Bungo.
4. Not all toves chortled at Bungo and Bungo didn't chortle at Bungo.
5. Not all toves chortled at Bungo and Bungo chortled at Bungo.
6. Bungo chortled at Bungo and no toves chortled at Bungo.
results.model_response:
Let

T(x)   = “x is a tove”  
C(x)   = “x chortled at Bungo”  
S(x)   = “x snicker-snacked Zindle”

The data given can be written:

1. ¬∀x (T(x) → C(x))          (“Not all toves chortled at Bungo.”)  
2. S(Bungo)              (“Bungo DID snicker-snack Zindle.”)  
3. [¬∀x (T(x) → S(x))] ↔ C(Rafin)   (irrelevant to the asked conclusions)  
4. C(Bungo)              (“Bungo chortled at Bungo.”)  
5. ∀x (x is a rath → S(x))       (irrelevant here)

From (1) we know:

 (α) There exists at least one tove that did NOT chortle at Bungo.

From (4) we know:

 (β) Bungo chortled at Bungo.

That is all that any of the proposed answers talk about.  Now check each option.

1. “Bungo didn’t chortle at Bungo and no toves chortled at Bungo.”  
 Contradicts (β); therefore not forced (indeed impossible).

2. “Not all toves chortled at Bungo and no toves chortled at Bungo.”  
 First part is true by (α); second part is not entailed, so the whole conjunction is not forced.

3. “Not all toves chortled at Bungo but it is not the case that Bungo chortled at Bungo.”  
 Second part contradicts (β); not forced.

4. “Not all toves chortled at Bungo and Bungo didn’t chortle at Bungo.”  
 Same contradiction with (β); not forced.

5. “Not all toves chortled at Bungo and Bungo chortled at Bungo.”  
 Both parts are exactly (α) and (β).  Hence this statement is certainly true in every model of the given information.

6. “Bungo chortled at Bungo and no toves chortled at Bungo.”  
 First part is true, second part not entailed; conjunction not forced.

Therefore the only statement that must be true is:

Statement 5.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Bungo or Rafin are uffish. Bungo is beamish. No toves will burble just in case Bungo isn't beamish.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. A tove will burble.
2. Every tove will burble.
3. Not all toves will burble.
4. Only toves will burble.
5. Rafin will burble.
6. No toves will burble.
results.model_response:
Let  

T(x) … “x is a tove”  
B(x) … “x is a borogove”  
R(x) … “x is a rath”  
U(x) … “x is uffish”  
M(x) … “x is beamish”  
Bur(x) … “x will burble”

Facts supplied  

F1. ∀x[ T(x) ∨ B(x) ∨ R(x) ] and the three predicates are mutually exclusive, with at least one object of each kind.  
F2. U(Bungo) ∨ U(Rafin).  
F3. M(Bungo).  
F4. “No toves will burble just in case Bungo isn’t beamish.”

“Just in case” means “if and only if”, so

F4. (¬∃x[T(x) ∧ Bur(x)]) ↔ ¬M(Bungo).

Step 1: Evaluate F4 using F3.  
Since M(Bungo) is true, ¬M(Bungo) is false.  
Therefore the left-hand side of the biconditional must also be false:

¬∃x[T(x) ∧ Bur(x)]  is false  
⟹  ∃x[T(x) ∧ Bur(x)]  is true.                (1)

Sentence (1) says “There exists at least one tove that will burble.”

Step 2: Test each candidate statement.

1. A tove will burble.                      Guaranteed by (1).  MUST be true.

2. Every tove will burble.                  Not forced; (1) only guarantees at least one.  
                                            (We can build a model with one burbling tove and
                                             another non-burbling tove.)

3. Not all toves will burble.               Also not forced; a model where every tove burbles
                                            satisfies all the given facts.

4. Only toves will burble.                  Unconstrained; borogoves or raths may or may not
                                            burble.

5. Rafin will burble.                       Nowhere implied by the facts.

6. No toves will burble.                    Directly contradicted by (1); must be false.

Conclusion  

The only statement that must be true in every situation compatible with the premises is:

1. A tove will burble.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Zindle or Bungo will whiffle. Only toves will whiffle. Every rath chortled at Bungo. If Zindle will whiffle, then every rath is mimsy only if every borogove chortled at Bungo.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Zindle will whiffle.
2. A tove will whiffle.
3. Every tove will whiffle.
4. Zindle and Bungo will whiffle.
5. Not all toves will whiffle.
6. No toves will whiffle.
results.model_response:
The premises guarantee that at least one of the two named individuals whiffles and that anything that whiffles is a tove.

1. “Zindle or Bungo will whiffle.”  ⇒  at least one of them whiffles.  
2. “Only toves will whiffle.”  ⇒  whoever whiffles must be a tove.

Together these two facts ensure that there is at least one tove that whiffles.  No other premise alters this conclusion, and all other listed statements can be made true or false by choosing a suitable model that still satisfies the premises.

Therefore the sole statement that must be true is:

2. A tove will whiffle.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Rafin is a tove. Every borogove is uffish but it is not the case that Rafin didn't snicker-snacked Bungo. Bungo isn't a borogove just in case not all toves snicker-snacked Zindle.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Bungo snicker-snacked every tove.
2. Every tove snicker-snacked Bungo.
3. No toves snicker-snacked Bungo.
4. Not all toves snicker-snacked Bungo.
5. A tove snicker-snacked Bungo.
6. Bungo snicker-snacked a tove.
results.model_response:
Let  

T(x) = “x is a tove”  
B(x) = “x is a borogove”  
R(x) = “x is a rath”  
S(x , y) = “x snicker-snacked y”  
U(x) = “x is uffish”

Premises  

1. Exclusive partition:  
   ∀x (T(x) ∨ B(x) ∨ R(x)) and at least one of each.

2. Rafin is a tove: T(Rafin).

3. Every borogove is uffish: ∀x (B(x) → U(x)).

   means an equivalence:

Derivation

From (2) and (4):
   T(Rafin)    and    S(Rafin , Bungo).

So there exists a tove that snicker-snacked Bungo:

   ∃x (T(x) ∧ S(x , Bungo)).                                   … (★)

Statement 5 on the list—“A tove snicker-snacked Bungo”—is exactly (★).  
Hence statement 5 must be true in every model of the premises.

None of the other listed statements are forced:

  make only Rafin do it and let some other tove fail to; the premises are still satisfied.

• “No toves snicker-snacked Bungo” (statement 3) is outright contradicted by (★).

• “Not all toves snicker-snacked Bungo” (statement 4) can be either true

• Statements 1 and 6, which concern Bungo snicker-snacking toves, are

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. A borogove galumphed over a tove that galumphed over a borogove. A borogove snicker-snacked Rafin only if not all borogoves snicker-snacked Rafin. If Rafin won't whiffle, then not all toves galumphed over Bungo only if no raths galumphed over Bungo.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Rafin snicker-snacked a borogove.
2. Not all borogoves snicker-snacked Rafin.
3. A borogove snicker-snacked Rafin.
4. Every borogove snicker-snacked Rafin.
5. No borogoves snicker-snacked Rafin.
6. Rafin snicker-snacked every borogove.
results.model_response:
The premises give only one unavoidable conclusion about “snicker-snacking”:

Premise  
A borogove snicker-snacked Rafin  only if  not all borogoves snicker-snacked Rafin.  
(Formally: ∃B (B is a borogove ∧ B S R) → ¬∀B (B is a borogove → B S R))

Because there is at least one borogove, assume for contradiction that  
every borogove snicker-snacked Rafin.  
Then, in particular, some borogove snicker-snacked Rafin, so the antecedent of the premise is true.  
The premise would then force “not all borogoves snicker-snacked Rafin”, a direct contradiction.  
Hence it is impossible that all borogoves snicker-snacked Rafin.

Therefore

2. Not all borogoves snicker-snacked Rafin must be true.

All the other listed statements can be either true or false without violating any premise; consequently none of them is forced by the information given.

Final answer: Statement 2 (and only statement 2) must be true.

5. Rafin is beamish.
6. Bungo isn't a rath.
results.model_response:
To discover what must be true, construct the logical consequences of the premises.

1. Everything is exactly one of: tove, borogove or rath (and each group is non-empty).

2. ∀x (rath(x) → chortled(x , Bungo))      – every rath chortles at Bungo.

      (∀x (rath(x) → chortled(x , Rafin))  →  beamish(Rafin)).

4. ∃x (rath(x) ∧ beamish(x))               – at least one beamish rath exists.

5. ¬ chortled(Bungo , Bungo).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
The key point

chortled(Bungo , Bungo).  
Premise 5 says the negation of that is true.  
Hence Bungo cannot be a rath.

Therefore           ¬ rath(Bungo).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Checking the six candidate statements

1. “Rafin isn’t beamish.”  
   Rafin may or may not be beamish; no premise fixes this.  Not necessary.

2. “Bungo is beamish.”  
   Unconstrained; Bungo could be beamish or not.  Not necessary.

3. “Bungo is a rath.”  
   Shown impossible.  Hence it certainly is not something that must be true.

4. “Rafin and Bungo are beamish.”

5. “Rafin is beamish.”  
   Not forced (same reason as 1).

6. “Bungo isn’t a rath.”

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Final Answer

Only statement 6 must be true:  
Bungo isn’t a rath.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. No borogoves are mimsy. Not all raths are mimsy. No borogoves are mimsy only if not all borogoves snicker-snacked Rafin.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Not all borogoves are mimsy.
2. Every borogove is mimsy.
3. Rafin is mimsy.
4. Not all borogoves are mimsy if and only if a borogove is mimsy.
5. A borogove is mimsy.
6. Only borogoves are mimsy.
results.model_response:
Given

1. All objects are either toves, borogoves, or raths (and these three kinds do not overlap); each kind is non-empty.
2. No borogove is mimsy.          (∀x[Bx → ¬Mx])
3. Not all raths are mimsy.       (¬∀x[Rx → Mx])
4. If no borogoves are mimsy then not all borogoves snicker-snacked Rafin.
   Because clause 2 already tells us there are no mimsy borogoves, the
   antecedent of (4) is true, so the consequent “not all borogoves
   snicker-snacked Rafin” is also true; nothing here affects who is mimsy.

From (2) we immediately obtain “Not all borogoves are mimsy” (there are
in fact none), i.e. ¬∀x[Bx → Mx].

Now test each candidate statement.

1. Not all borogoves are mimsy.  
   Directly follows from (2).  Must be true.

2. Every borogove is mimsy.  
   Contradicts (2).  Cannot be true in the given situation.

3. Rafin is mimsy.  
   The premises say nothing about Rafin’s mimsy-status.  Not forced.

4. Not all borogoves are mimsy ↔ a borogove is mimsy.  
   In the given situation the left side is true (see 1) but the right side
   is false (there are no mimsy borogoves), so the biconditional is false;
   it is not forced by the premises.

5. A borogove is mimsy.  
   Contradicts (2).  Not true.

6. Only borogoves are mimsy. (∀x[Mx → Bx])  
   Premises allow mimsy toves or mimsy raths; this statement is not
   entailed.

Therefore the only statement that must be true is:

Statement 1.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Zindle and Bungo are mimsy. A borogove chortled at a rath that chortled at a tove. Not all toves will whiffle but it is not the case that Rafin is a tove.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Zindle and Bungo will whiffle.
2. Zindle will whiffle.
3. Zindle won't whiffle.
4. Not all toves will whiffle and Zindle isn't mimsy.
5. Not all toves will whiffle but it is not the case that Zindle isn't mimsy.
6. Zindle and Rafin will whiffle.
results.model_response:
The information we are given already tells us two things for certain:

a) “Not all toves will whiffle.”  
b) “Zindle is mimsy.”  (The sentence “it is not the case that Zindle isn’t mimsy” is just the double-negative version of this.)

Now look at the six candidate statements.

1. “Zindle and Bungo will whiffle.” – We are never told anything about the whiffling of either Zindle or Bungo, so this need not be true.

2. “Zindle will whiffle.” – Also not guaranteed; no information forces Zindle to whiffle.

3. “Zindle won’t whiffle.” – Equally unfixed; Zindle might or might not whiffle.

4. “Not all toves will whiffle and Zindle isn’t mimsy.” – The first half is true, but the second half contradicts the given fact that Zindle is mimsy, so the whole statement cannot be true.

5. “Not all toves will whiffle but it is not the case that Zindle isn’t mimsy.” – This is exactly the conjunction of (a) and (b) above, both of which are certainly true. Hence this entire statement must be true.

6. “Zindle and Rafin will whiffle.” – Nothing in the premises requires either of them to whiffle, so this is not forced.

Therefore the only statement that must be true is:

Statement 5.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. A rath gimbled. No raths chortled at a rath. Every borogove snicker-snacked every borogove that snicker-snacked it. Every borogove chortled at every rath that chortled at every tove. No toves snicker-snacked Zindle if and only if a borogove chortled at Bungo.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. A rath chortled at every rath.
2. A rath chortled at a rath.
3. A rath chortled at every rath that chortled at it.
4. Every rath chortled at a rath.
5. There is a rath that every rath chortled at.
6. Every rath chortled at every rath.
results.model_response:
From the premisses we have the universal fact  
 “No raths chortled at a rath.”  
Formally: ∀x∀y[(Rath(x) ∧ Rath(y)) → ¬Chortled(x , y)].

That immediately yields:

• Statement 1 (∃x Rath(x) ∧ ∀y[Rath(y) → Chortled(x , y)]) is impossible.  
• Statement 2 (∃x∃y[Rath(x) ∧ Rath(y) ∧ Chortled(x , y)]) is impossible.  
• Statement 4 (∀x[Rath(x) → ∃y(Rath(y) ∧ Chortled(x , y))]) is impossible.  
• Statement 5 (∃y[Rath(y) ∧ ∀x(Rath(x) → Chortled(x , y))]) is impossible.  
• Statement 6 (∀x∀y[(Rath(x) ∧ Rath(y)) → Chortled(x , y)]) is impossible.

All five are therefore necessarily false.

Consider statement 3:

“A rath chortled at every rath that chortled at it.”

Formalising:

∃x [Rath(x) ∧ ∀y((Rath(y) ∧ Chortled(y , x)) → Chortled(x , y))].

Because no rath chortles at any rath, the antecedent
 Rath(y) ∧ Chortled(y , x)
is never satisfied; hence the conditional is vacuously true for every rath.  
Since the premisses guarantee the existence of at least one rath, the existential
quantifier is satisfied.  Thus statement 3 must be true.

Conclusion  
The only statement that is forced to be true by the given information is

3. A rath chortled at every rath that chortled at it.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. A borogove is uffish and a rath is uffish. No borogoves snicker-snacked Bungo only if not all borogoves snicker-snacked Rafin. Not all toves snicker-snacked Zindle and only toves gimbled.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Zindle and Rafin gimbled.
2. A borogove is uffish just in case no toves snicker-snacked Zindle.
3. A borogove is uffish just in case not all toves snicker-snacked Zindle.
4. Zindle and Bungo gimbled.
5. Zindle didn't gimble.
6. Zindle gimbled.
results.model_response:
Let  

T(x) = “x is a tove”  
B(x) = “x is a borogove”  
R(x) = “x is a rath”            (T, B, R form an exclusive and exhaustive partition and each is non–empty)  
U(x) = “x is uffish”  
S(x,y) = “x snicker-snacked y”  
G(x) = “x gimbled”

Premises

P1  ∀x(B(x) → U(x)) and ∀x(R(x) → U(x))                       (borogoves and raths are uffish)  
P2  [¬∃x(B(x) ∧ S(x,Bungo))] → [¬∀x(B(x) → S(x,Rafin))]       (conditional about snicker-snacking)  
P3  ¬∀x(T(x) → S(x,Zindle))                                    (not all toves snicker-snacked Zindle)  
P4  ∀x(G(x) → T(x))                                            (only toves gimbled)

We ask which of the six candidate statements is forced by P1–P4.

Candidate 3  
“A borogove is uffish just in case not all toves snicker-snacked Zindle.”

Read formally:

∀x [B(x) → (U(x) ↔ ¬∀y(T(y) → S(y,Zindle)))].

Because of P1, B(x) → U(x) is always true, and because of P3 the sentence
¬∀y(T(y) → S(y,Zindle)) is also always true.  Hence, for every borogove x we
have True ↔ True, making the biconditional true.  Thus statement 3 is true in
every model that satisfies the premises, i.e. it is entailed by the premises.

The other five statements can all be falsified while the premises remain
true; one counter-model (among many) suffices to show each of them is not
forced.

Example counter-model (for 1, 2, 4, 5, 6)

Domain: {t₁,t₂, b₁, r₁}.  
Tove: t₁, t₂  Borogove: b₁  Rath: r₁  
Let Bungo = b₁, Rafin = r₁, Zindle = r₁.  

Define predicates:

• U(x) true of b₁ and r₁ (satisfies P1).  
• S(b₁,Bungo) true, every other S(x,Bungo) false;  
   S(b₁,Rafin) true, S(t₁,Rafin) true; others false – this makes the antecedent
   of P2 false, so the conditional P2 is true.  
• S(t₁,Zindle) true, S(t₂,Zindle) false, so P3 (“not all toves …”) is true.  
• G(x) true of t₁ only; hence P4 is satisfied.

In this model  

– Zindle does not gimble, so statements 1, 4, 6 are false.  
– Rafin does not gimble, so statement 1 is false.  
– A tove (t₁) snicker-snacked Zindle, so “no toves snicker-snacked Zindle” is
  false; that makes statement 2 false.  
– Statement 5 (“Zindle didn’t gimble”) happens to be true here, but we could
  simply let G(Zindle) hold as well (making Zindle a tove) and keep all premises
  true, turning statement 5 into a falsehood.  Hence 5 is not forced either.

Because 1, 2, 4, 5 and 6 can be falsified while the premises still hold, none
of them is logically entailed.

Conclusion

The only statement that must be true given the premises is:

3.  A borogove is uffish just in case not all toves snicker-snacked Zindle.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. No raths chortled at Zindle. There is a borogove that every tove chortled at. Bungo didn't chortle at Bungo but it is not the case that Rafin didn't chortle at Bungo.

2. Zindle didn't chortle at Rafin unless not all raths chortled at Bungo.
3. Every rath chortled at Zindle but it is not the case that Rafin didn't chortle at Bungo.
4. Zindle didn't chortle at Rafin or not all raths chortled at Bungo.
5. No raths chortled at Zindle just in case Rafin chortled at Bungo.
6. Every rath chortled at Zindle and Rafin didn't chortle at Bungo.
results.model_response:
Premises (formalised)

P1  Every creature is exactly one of: tove, borogove, rath, and each sort is non-empty.  
P2  ∀x (Rath(x) → ¬Chortled(x , Zindle)).                 (no raths chortled at Zindle)  
P3  ∃y (Borogove(y) ∧ ∀x (Tove(x) → Chortled(x , y))).   (a borogove everyone tove chortled at)  
P4  ¬Chortled(Bungo , Bungo).                            (Bungo didn’t chortle at Bungo)  
P5  ¬¬Chortled(Rafin , Bungo)  ⇔  Chortled(Rafin , Bungo). (Rafin did chortle at Bungo)

Candidate statements

“just in case”  = “iff” (↔)  
“unless”         = “if not” (¬Q → P)

We test each candidate for logical consequence of P1–P5.

1. (¬Ch(Zindle,Bungo)) ↔ (∀x(Rath(x)→¬Ch(x,Rafin)))
   Both sides are unconstrained by the premises, so they can disagree in a model.

Exactly one of the listed statements must be true in the described situation:

P2.  ¬ C(Bungo , Bungo)

•  Rafin did not chortle at Zindle.  
•  Bungo did not chortle at Bungo.

Now inspect each candidate statement (remember “but” means logical “and”):

1.  C(Bungo,Bungo) ∧ C(Rafin,Zindle)          → false ∧ false = false  
2.  ¬C(Bungo,Bungo) ∧ C(Rafin,Zindle)         → true ∧ false = false  
3.  C(Bungo,Bungo) ∧ ¬C(Rafin,Zindle)         → false ∧ true  = false  
4.  C(Rafin,Zindle) ∧ C(Rafin,Bungo)          → false ∧ (unknown) = false  
5.  C(Rafin,Zindle) ∧ ¬C(Rafin,Bungo)         → false ∧ (unknown) = false  
6.  ¬C(Bungo,Bungo) ∧ ¬C(Rafin,Zindle)        → true  ∧ true  = true

Only statement 6 is forced to be true in every situation that satisfies the premises; all the others are certainly false (or at best not guaranteed).

results.model_response:
Premises (symbolised)

1. Every being is exactly one of: tove (T), borogove (B), rath (R); and there is at least one of each kind.

2. ¬∃x (T(x) ∧ G(x,Bungo)) (No tove galumphed over Bungo.)

3. ¬¬C(Bungo,Bungo) (It is not the case that Bungo didn’t chortle at Bungo.)  
  ⇒ C(Bungo,Bungo) is true.

4. G(Bungo,Bungo) → ( [∀x (R(x) → C(x,Bungo))] → [∀x (R(x) → ¬G(x,Bungo))] )

5. ¬∀x (B(x) → W(x)) (Not all borogoves will whiffle.)  
  ⇔ ∃x (B(x) ∧ ¬W(x)).

6. ∀x (T(x) → W(x)) (Every tove will whiffle.)

   – Both conjuncts are exactly premises 3 and 5, hence always true.

   – Premise 5 gives “not all borogoves whiffle”, not “none do”. Worlds in which some
     borogove does whiffle are allowed, so statement 2 is not forced.

3. C(Bungo,Bungo) ∧ ∀x (W(x) → B(x))  
   – Premise 6 says every tove whiffles, so there are whifflers that are not borogoves.
     This conjunction cannot hold in any permissible world, therefore it is not forced.

4. ¬C(Bungo,Bungo) ∧ ∃x (B(x) ∧ W(x))  
   – First conjunct contradicts premise 3; statement 4 is impossible, hence not forced.

5. C(Bungo,Bungo) ∧ ∃x (B(x) ∧ W(x))  
   – Premises allow but do not require some borogove to whiffle, so 5 is not forced.

6. W(Bungo)  
   – Bungo might be a tove (then he whiffles) but could instead be a borogove that
     doesn’t whiffle or a rath; thus W(Bungo) is not guaranteed.

Only statement 1 is entailed by the given information.

Final answer: 1.

F5.  ∃x ( T(x) ∧ ¬G(x, Zindle) )              (not all toves galumphed over Zindle)  
F6.  C(Rafin, Bungo)                          (Rafin chortled at Bungo)

2. There is at least one tove, one borogove and one rath.

5. “Zindle will whiffle.”  
   Never mentioned; can be either true or false.

6. “A tove chortled at Zindle.”

A. The conjuncts in statement 5 are precisely premises 3 and 5,
so 5 is always true in every model of the premises.

B. To see that the other statements are **not** forced by the
premises it is enough to build a single model of the premises in
which each of them is false.  Here is one such model:

  – Chortled(Zindle,Bungo).          (chosen so that 1 and 4 are false)  
  – Chortled(Ringo,Zindle).          (chosen so that 2 and 6 are false)  
  – No one else chortles.  
  – Uffish(Rafin) (satisfies premise 6).  
  – No rath snicker-snacks Bungo (so premise 2 is vacuously true).

This structure satisfies every given premise, yet

• Statement 1 is false because its right-hand side is true

  (Zindle did chortle at Bungo).

• Statement 2 (and the identical statement 6) is false because a
  rath (Ringo) chortled at Zindle, but Bungo did not gimbal.

• Statement 3 is false because Bungo did chortle at Zindle.

• Statement 4 is false because its left side is false (all raths
  gimbled) while its right side is true (Zindle did not fail to
  chortle at Bungo).

Since every premise holds and each statement except 5 can be made
false, only statement 5 is entailed by the information given.

Final answer

The only statement that must be true is:

5. Every rath gimbled and Bungo chortled at Zindle.

– We are told nothing about Zindle’s own galumphing, so 1 and 3 need not hold.  
– P3 says all raths galumph Zindle, so 2 and 4 are actually false in every model.  
– Zindle is uffish only if Zindle happens to be a rath; the information given does not fix Zindle’s category, so 6 is not guaranteed.

Hence the only statement that must be true is

5. A rath galumphed over Zindle.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Not all raths snicker-snacked Rafin. A tove snicker-snacked every rath. A tove snicker-snacked itself.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. There is a tove that every rath snicker-snacked.
2. No toves snicker-snacked every rath.
3. Every tove snicker-snacked a rath.
4. Every tove snicker-snacked every rath.
5. A tove snicker-snacked a rath.
6. No toves snicker-snacked a rath.
results.model_response:
Let  

T(x) … “x is a tove”  
B(x) … “x is a borogove”  
R(x) … “x is a rath”  
S(x,y) … “x snicker-snacked y”  
The three kinds are exclusive and exhaustive and each is inhabited.  
Rafin is a fixed individual constant.

Premises  

1. ∃x T(x), ∃x B(x), ∃x R(x) and ∀x (T(x) ∨ B(x) ∨ R(x)) and the three predicates are pairwise disjoint.  
2. ¬∀x (R(x) → S(x, Rafin)) (“Not all raths snicker-snacked Rafin”)  
   ⟹ ∃r (R(r) ∧ ¬S(r, Rafin)).  
3. ∃t (T(t) ∧ ∀r (R(r) → S(t,r))) (“A tove snicker-snacked every rath”).  
4. ∃t (T(t) ∧ S(t,t)) (“A tove snicker-snacked itself”).

We ask which candidate statements are forced by these premises.

Candidate 1  
∃t (T(t) ∧ ∀r (R(r) → S(r,t))).  
Not entailed.  Counter-model:  
One tove a, raths b and c, a snicker-snacks all raths, nothing snicker-snacks a.  
All premises true while 1 is false.

Candidate 2  
¬∃t (T(t) ∧ ∀r (R(r) → S(t,r))).  
Directly contradicted by premise 3, hence cannot be true in any model of the premises.

Candidate 3  
∀t (T(t) → ∃r (R(r) ∧ S(t,r))).  
Premises speak only about one special tove; other toves could fail to snicker-snack any rath.  
So 3 is not forced.

Candidate 4  
∀t∀r (T(t) ∧ R(r) → S(t,r)).  
Premise 3 gives only an existential, not a universal; 4 is not forced.

Candidate 5  
∃t∃r (T(t) ∧ R(r) ∧ S(t,r)).  
Premise 3 supplies a tove t that snicker-snacks every rath, and there is at least one rath, so such a pair (t,r) certainly exists.  
Therefore 5 must be true.

Candidate 6  
¬∃t∃r (T(t) ∧ R(r) ∧ S(t,r)).  
Negation of statement 5, hence impossible under the premises.

Conclusion  
The only statement that must be true, given the information provided, is

5. A tove snicker-snacked a rath.

Bungo will burble only if a tove galumphed over Bungo. If Bungo is beamish, then not all toves galumphed over Zindle only if no raths galumphed over Bungo.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

4. Bungo won't burble.

results.model_response:
From the given information we have

1. ∀x (Tove(x) → ¬Galumphed-over(x, Bungo))            (no tove galumphed over Bungo)  
2. Burble(Bungo) → ∃x (Tove(x) ∧ Galumphed-over(x, Bungo))   (Bungo burbles only if some tove galumphed over him)

Assume, for contradiction, that Bungo will burble:

 Burble(Bungo)  
 ⇒ by (2) ∃x (Tove(x) ∧ Galumphed-over(x, Bungo)).  
 Pick such an x; then Tove(x) and Galumphed-over(x, Bungo).  
 But by (1) Tove(x) ⇒ ¬Galumphed-over(x, Bungo).  
 Hence Galumphed-over(x, Bungo) ∧ ¬Galumphed-over(x, Bungo), a contradiction.

Therefore Burble(Bungo) is impossible; ¬Burble(Bungo) is forced.

Nothing else in the premises constrains whether Zindle burbles, so Burble(Zindle) may be either true or false.

Thus, of the five candidate conclusions, the only one that must be true is

4. Bungo won’t burble.

Nothing else that is given forces any further chortling facts:

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Every borogove gyred. Rafin isn't a tove and a borogove will whiffle. Rafin is a tove unless a rath is beamish.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Not all borogoves gyred.
2. No borogoves gyred.
3. A borogove gyred.
4. Rafin will whiffle.
5. Only borogoves gyred.
6. Rafin won't whiffle.
results.model_response:
Premises

1. Everything is exactly one of: tove, borogove or rath; there is at least one of each kind.
2. Every borogove gyred.  (∀x : borogove(x) → gyred(x))
3. Rafin isn’t a tove.
4. “A borogove will whiffle.”  ─ read in ordinary English as a universal claim: every borogove will whiffle.  (∀x : borogove(x) → whiffle(x))   (The exact reading makes no difference to the conclusions below; even an existential reading would not change the result.)
5. “Rafin is a tove unless a rath is beamish.”  
   Logical form:   if Rafin is not a tove → some rath is beamish,
   or contrapositively: if no rath is beamish → Rafin is a tove.

Necessary-truth test for the six candidate statements

1. Not all borogoves gyred.  
   Premise 2 says every borogove gyred, so this is false in the given situation; it is certainly not forced to be true.

2. No borogoves gyred.  
   Again contradicted by premise 2; not forced to be true.

3. A borogove gyred.  
   We have at least one borogove (premise 1) and every borogove gyred (premise 2).  
   Therefore at least one creature (that borogove) gyred.  
   This statement is true in every model of the premises, so it must be true.

4. Rafin will whiffle.  
   Rafin could be a borogove (then he would whiffle) or a rath (then we are told nothing about his whiffling).  
   The premises do not force either outcome, so the statement is not guaranteed.

5. Only borogoves gyred.  
   The premises say all borogoves gyred, but they say nothing about whether toves or raths might gyre as well.  
   Hence it is possible that some non-borogove gyres, so the statement is not guaranteed.

6. Rafin won’t whiffle.  
   Same reasoning as for #4: the premises leave it open whether Rafin whiffles.  
   Therefore this is not guaranteed either.

Conclusion

Of the six statements, the only one that must be true in every situation satisfying the premises is

3. A borogove gyred.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. A rath galumphed over a borogove that galumphed over it. Every rath snicker-snacked every rath that snicker-snacked it. Rafin is a tove and Bungo is a rath.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Every rath snicker-snacked every rath.
2. A rath snicker-snacked a rath.
3. A rath snicker-snacked every rath.
4. A rath snicker-snacked every rath that snicker-snacked it.
5. No raths snicker-snacked every rath.
6. Every rath snicker-snacked a rath.
results.model_response:
Let  

• T(x) = “x is a tove”,  
• B(x) = “x is a borogove”,  
• R(x) = “x is a rath”,  

and let  

• S(x,y) = “x snicker-snacked y”,  
• G(x,y) = “x galumphed over y”.  

Facts given  

1. (Partition) Every object is exactly one of T, B or R and each class is non-empty.  
2. (Mutual galumph) ∃r,b [ R(r) ∧ B(b) ∧ G(r,b) ∧ G(b,r) ].  
3. (Reciprocating raths) ∀x∀y [ R(x) ∧ R(y) ∧ S(y,x) → S(x,y) ].  
4. Rafin is a tove, Bungo is a rath.

We test each statement to see if it is forced (true in every model of the facts).

1. “Every rath snicker-snacked every rath.”  
   This would require S to relate every ordered pair of raths.  
   Take a model in which no one snicker-snacks anyone at all; fact 3 is still satisfied (its antecedent is false everywhere). Statement 1 is then false. So 1 is **not** forced.

2. “A rath snicker-snacked a rath.”  
   In the same ‘no-snicker-snacking’ model, this existential is false.  
   Hence 2 is **not** forced.

3. “A rath snicker-snacked every rath.”  
   Again take the empty S relation; the statement is false.  
   So 3 is **not** forced.

4. “A rath snicker-snacked every rath that snicker-snacked it.”  
   Fact 3 actually says that every rath does this.  
   Because there is at least one rath (fact 1), there certainly exists a rath with this property.  
   Therefore statement 4 is **always true** – it must hold.

5. “No raths snicker-snacked every rath.”  
   Build a model with exactly one rath; let it snicker-snack itself.  
   Then that lone rath does snicker-snack every rath (namely itself), so statement 5 can be false.  
   Hence 5 is **not** forced.

6. “Every rath snicker-snacked a rath.”  
   In the empty-S model no rath snicker-snacks anyone, so the statement can be false.  
   Thus 6 is **not** forced.

Conclusion: the only statement that must be true is

4. A rath snicker-snacked every rath that snicker-snacked it.

If Rafin didn't snicker-snacked Rafin, then every rath is mimsy only if not all toves galumphed over Bungo.

1. Zindle and Rafin are mimsy.
2. Zindle isn't mimsy.

results.model_response:
The only information in the premises that links Rafin to any of the three exclusive kinds (tove, borogove, rath) is the pair of sentences

1. Every rath galumphed over every rath, and  
2. Not all raths galumphed over Rafin.

Write G(x,y) for “x galumphed over y’’ and Rath(x) for “x is a rath’’.  
Sentence 1 says

  ∀x∀y( Rath(x) ∧ Rath(y) → G(x,y) ).                        (†)

Sentence 2 says

  ¬∀x( Rath(x) → G(x,Rafin) ), i.e. ∃x( Rath(x) ∧ ¬G(x,Rafin) ).    (‡)

Assume, toward a contradiction, that Rafin is a rath.  
Take the x supplied by (‡); that x is a rath and ¬G(x,Rafin).  
Because Rafin is (by the assumption) also a rath, both antecedents of (†) are true for this x and y = Rafin, so (†) forces G(x,Rafin).  
That contradicts ¬G(x,Rafin).

Therefore the assumption is impossible and Rafin cannot be a rath:

  ¬Rath(Rafin)      (Rafin isn’t a rath).

No premise links “being a rath’’ in any analogous way to Zindle or Bungo, nor do the premises relate the predicates “mimsy’’, “chortled”, or “snicker-snacked’’ to “rath’’ or to each other.  Hence Zindle’s or Bungo’s mimsy-status and the truth of “Rafin chortled at Zindle’’ place no further compulsory restrictions.

So, across all models that satisfy the given information, the only statement that must always hold is

6. Rafin isn’t a rath.

None of the other listed statements is forced; each can be made true in some models and false in others.

Final answer: Statement 6 only.

2. Rafin is a rath.  
3. Bungo snicker-snacked a (some) borogove – so at least one borogove exists.

Consequences  
• From 4 there is at least one creature (a tove) that whiffles.  
• From 5 zero borogoves whiffle.  
• We are told nothing about whether raths whiffle.

Evaluation of the candidate statements  

1. “Only borogoves will whiffle and not all borogoves will whiffle.”

2. “Every borogove will whiffle.”

4. “A borogove will whiffle.”

5. “Only borogoves will whiffle and no borogoves will whiffle.”

6. “Not all borogoves will whiffle.”

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

2. A rath galumphed over a rath.
3. Every rath galumphed over every rath.
4. A rath galumphed over every rath.
5. A rath galumphed over every rath that galumphed over it.
6. Every rath galumphed over a rath.
results.model_response:
Premises restated in logical form  

1. Every thing is either a tove, a borogove, or a rath (and never more than one of these), and each of the three kinds exists.  
2. Bungo and Rafin are uffish.  
3. ¬∃x∃y (Rath(x) ∧ Rath(y) ∧ Galumphed-over(x,y))  (no rath galumphed over a rath).  
4. (∃x (Rath(x) ∧ Galumphed-over(x, Rafin))) ∨ (∃x (Rath(x) ∧ Galumphed-over(x, Zindle))).  

We test each candidate statement to see whether it must be true in every model that satisfies the premises.

Statement 1: ∃r (Rath(r) ∧ ∀r₂ (Rath(r₂) → Galumphed-over(r₂,r))).  
Impossible, because premise 3 forbids any Galumphed-over between two raths.  
⇒ Need not be true.

Statement 2: ∃x∃y (Rath(x) ∧ Rath(y) ∧ Galumphed-over(x,y)).  
Directly contradicted by premise 3.  
⇒ False in every model; not a necessary truth.

Statement 3: ∀x∀y ((Rath(x) ∧ Rath(y)) → Galumphed-over(x,y)).  
Also ruled out by premise 3.  
⇒ Need not be true.

Statement 4: ∃x (Rath(x) ∧ ∀y (Rath(y) → Galumphed-over(x,y))).  
Would require some rath to galumph over each rath, contradicting premise 3 (take y = x).  
⇒ Impossible; not a necessary truth.

Statement 5: ∃x (Rath(x) ∧ ∀y ((Rath(y) ∧ Galumphed-over(y,x)) → Galumphed-over(x,y))).  
Because of premise 3, the antecedent Rath(y) ∧ Galumphed-over(y,x) is always false (no rath can galumph over a rath).  
Thus the conditional is vacuously true for every y, so it is true for every rath whatsoever.  
Since at least one rath exists, such an x certainly exists.  
⇒ Statement 5 holds in every model; it must be true.

Statement 6: ∀x (Rath(x) → ∃y (Rath(y) ∧ Galumphed-over(x,y))).  
Requires each rath to galumph over some rath, again impossible by premise 3.  
⇒ Not necessarily true.

Conclusion: The only statement that must be true given the premises is

5. A rath galumphed over every rath that galumphed over it.

results.model_response:

1. The three kinds – toves, borogoves and raths – are exclusive and jointly exhaustive; there is at least one of each.

2. Premises:
   a) No rath galumphed over a rath.  
   b) Every rath galumphed over Rafin.

   If Rafin were a rath, premise a would forbid any rath (in particular ρ) from galumphing over Rafin.  
   That contradiction shows that Rafin cannot be a rath.

4. Since Rafin is neither a rath (just proved) nor a tove (premise c), Rafin must be a borogove.

   6. Rafin isn’t a rath.

The other statements are not forced.  For example, a model in which Bungo is a tove (so 1 is false and 5 is true) and a model in which Bungo is a borogove (so 1 is true and 5 is false) can both be set up without violating any premise, and statement 3 is a self-contradictory conjunction that never has to be true.

results.model_response:

Premise 3:  “A borogove is uffish …”  
Premise 4:  “Not all toves chortled at Bungo.”

Thus, in every situation that satisfies the premises

• there exists at least one borogove that is uffish, and  
• at least one tove failed to chortle at Bungo.

Statement 1 is exactly the conjunction of those two facts:

1. “A borogove is uffish and not all toves chortled at Bungo.”

Because both conjuncts are explicit premises, statement 1 is true in every model of the premises and therefore must be true.

None of the other statements is forced by the premises:

2. could be false if some (but not all) toves chortled at Bungo.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Every borogove snicker-snacked every tove. Not all borogoves snicker-snacked Rafin only if not all toves chortled at Rafin. Not all toves gyred just in case no toves snicker-snacked Zindle.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. There is a tove that every borogove snicker-snacked.
2. A tove snicker-snacked a borogove.
3. No toves snicker-snacked every borogove.
4. A tove snicker-snacked every borogove.
5. Every tove snicker-snacked every borogove.
6. Every tove snicker-snacked a borogove.
results.model_response:
Let  

T(x)  = “x is a tove”  
B(x)  = “x is a borogove”  
R(x)  = “x is a rath”

S(x,y) = “x snicker-snacked y”  
C(x,y) = “x chortled at y”  
G(x)    = “x gyred”

Premises

P1  Everybody is exactly one of T, B, R and each class is non-empty.  
P2  ∀x(Bx → ∀y(Ty → S(x,y)))                    (every borogove snicker-snacked every tove)  
P3  (¬∀x(Bx → S(x,Rafin))) → (¬∀y(Ty → C(y,Rafin)))  
P4  (¬∀y(Ty → G(y))) ↔ (∀y(Ty → ¬S(y,Zindle)))

Candidate conclusions

1  ∃y(Ty ∧ ∀x(Bx → S(x,y)))  
2  ∃x∃y(Tx ∧ By ∧ S(x,y))  
3  ¬∃x(Tx ∧ ∀y(By → S(x,y)))  
4  ∃x(Tx ∧ ∀y(By → S(x,y)))  
5  ∀x(Tx → ∀y(By → S(x,y)))  
6  ∀x(Tx → ∃y(By ∧ S(x,y)))

-------------------------------------------------
What the premises force

From P2:

For every tove y and every borogove x, S(x,y) holds.  
Fix any tove t (there is at least one by P1).  
Then ∀x(Bx → S(x,t)); hence

∃y(Ty ∧ ∀x(Bx → S(x,y))).                         (*)

That is exactly statement 1.  
So 1 must be true in every model of the premises.

-------------------------------------------------
Why none of the others is forced

To show a statement is not forced it suffices to build a model of the
premises in which the statement fails.  Two small structures do the job.

Model A (makes 2,4,5,6 false, 3 true)
• Objects: one tove t, two borogoves b₁,b₂, one rath r.  
• S relation: the only true snicker-snacks are those required by P2,
  i.e. b₁ S t and b₂ S t.  No other S-pairs.  
• Choose the other predicates arbitrarily so that P3, P4 are satisfied.

In Model A  
 – No tove ever snicker-snacks a borogove, so statements 2, 4, 5, 6 are false.  
 – Hence statement 3 (which says no tove snicker-snacks every borogove) is true.

Model B (makes 3 false, 4 true)
Same domain as Model A, but add S(t,b₁) and S(t,b₂).  
Now the tove t snicker-snacks every borogove, so statement 3 is false
and statement 4 is true, while 5 and 6 may still be falsified by adding
another tove that doesn’t snicker-snack anyone.

Because we can have one model where each of 2–6 is false and another
where each is true, none of them is forced by the premises.

-------------------------------------------------
Final answer

1. Must be true.  
2. – 6. Need not be true.

Thus the only statement that is forced in every situation described is statement 1.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Every tove galumphed over a tove. Zindle isn't a borogove unless every tove galumphed over Rafin. A borogove snicker-snacked Bungo only if Rafin didn't snicker-snacked Rafin. If Rafin is a tove, then Bungo galumphed over Zindle only if every rath galumphed over Rafin. Not all toves galumphed over Zindle and Bungo is a rath.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. A tove galumphed over every tove.
2. No toves galumphed over a tove.
3. No toves galumphed over every tove.
4. Every tove galumphed over every tove.
5. There is a tove that every tove galumphed over.
6. A tove galumphed over a tove.
results.model_response:
Let  

T(x)  =  “x is a tove”  
B(x)  =  “x is a borogove”  
R(x)  =  “x is a rath”  
G(x,y)=  “x galumphed over y”  

Given facts (paraphrased in first–order form)

F1.  ∀x [T(x) ∨ B(x) ∨ R(x)] and at least one object satisfies each predicate.  
F2.  ∀x (T(x) → ∃y (T(y) ∧ G(x,y))).              (Every tove galumphed over a tove.)  
F3.  (¬B(Zindle)) ← ¬∀x (T(x) → G(x,Rafin)).     (“unless” clause)  
     (Equivalent contrapositive:  B(Zindle) → ∀x (T(x) → G(x,Rafin))).  
F4.  (∃x (B(x) ∧ S(x,Bungo))) → ¬S(Rafin,Rafin).  
F5.  T(Rafin) → (G(Bungo,Zindle) → ∀x (R(x) → G(x,Rafin))).  
F6.  ¬∀x (T(x) → G(x,Zindle)).                    (Not all toves galumphed over Zindle.)  
F7.  R(Bungo).                                    (Bungo is a rath.)

Statements to test (must they be true?)

1. ∃x (T(x) ∧ ∀y (T(y) → G(x,y))).  
2. ¬∃x∃y (T(x) ∧ T(y) ∧ G(x,y)).  
3. ¬∃x (T(x) ∧ ∀y (T(y) → G(x,y))).  
4. ∀x∀y ((T(x) ∧ T(y)) → G(x,y)).  
5. ∃y (T(y) ∧ ∀x (T(x) → G(x,y))).  
6. ∃x∃y (T(x) ∧ T(y) ∧ G(x,y)).

Step 1.  What must be true?

From F1 there is at least one tove, say t.  
From F2, that tove galumphed over some tove (possibly itself), say u.  
Hence ∃x∃y (T(x) ∧ T(y) ∧ G(x,y)) is inevitable.  
That is exactly statement 6.  So 6 MUST be true.

Step 2.  Show the other statements are not forced (supply counter-models).

Model A (shows 1,4,5 false – therefore not forced)  
Domain: {t1,t2,ra,bo,bn}.  
t1,t2 are toves; bo is a borogove; bn (Bungo) is a rath; ra (Rafin) is a rath.  
G relation: G(t1,t1), G(t2,t2) – nothing else.  
All premises F1–F7 are satisfied, yet:  
 • No tove galumphed over every tove (1 false, 3 true).  
 • Certainly not every tove galumphed over every tove (4 false).  
 • No single tove is galumphed over by every tove (5 false).

Model B (shows 3 false – therefore not forced)  
Keep the same domain, but let Rafin = t1 (so Rafin is a tove) and add  
G(t1,t1), G(t1,t2), G(t2,t1).  
Now t1 galumphs over both toves, so a tove DOES galumph over every tove; 3 is false while all premises still hold.  (Here 1 is true, showing it is not forced either.)

Model A and Model B together demonstrate that 1,3,4,5 can be either true or false while all premises stay valid; they are not logically entailed.

Step 3.  Statement 2 is outright incompatible with F2 (every tove galumphs over some tove).  Hence 2 cannot possibly be true in every model.

Conclusion

The only statement that must be true, given the premises, is

6.  A tove galumphed over a tove.

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Rafin snicker-snacked Bungo. A rath snicker-snacked Zindle and every borogove snicker-snacked Zindle. If Rafin didn't snicker-snacked Rafin, then every rath snicker-snacked Bungo only if not all toves will whiffle. Not all borogoves will whiffle and Bungo isn't mimsy.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Zindle will whiffle.
2. No borogoves snicker-snacked Zindle.
3. A borogove snicker-snacked Zindle.
4. Zindle snicker-snacked a borogove.
5. Zindle snicker-snacked every borogove.
6. Not all borogoves snicker-snacked Zindle.
results.model_response:
Given information

A1. Every creature is exactly one of: tove, borogove, rath, and there is at least one of each.

A2. Rafin snicker-snacked Bungo. S( Rafin , Bungo )

A3. A rath snicker-snacked Zindle. ∃x[ rath(x) ∧ S(x , Zindle) ]

A4. Every borogove snicker-snacked Zindle. ∀x[ borogove(x) → S(x , Zindle) ]

A5. (¬S(Rafin , Rafin)) → ( (∀x[ rath(x) → S(x , Bungo)] → ¬∀x[ tove(x) → whiffle(x) ] )

A6. Not all borogoves will whiffle. ¬∀x[ borogove(x) → whiffle(x) ]

A7. Bungo isn’t mimsy.

What must follow?

Statement-by-statement test

1. “Zindle will whiffle.”  
   Nothing supplied links Zindle (or anyone) to whiffling, so Zindle might or might not whiffle.  Not forced.

2. “No borogoves snicker-snacked Zindle.”  
   By A4 every borogove does snicker-snack Zindle, and by A1 at least one borogove exists.  Therefore statement 2 is necessarily false, not necessarily true.

3. “A borogove snicker-snacked Zindle.”  
   We have both (i) there is at least one borogove (A1) and (ii) every borogove snicker-snacks Zindle (A4).  Hence at least one borogove snicker-snacks Zindle in every possible situation.  Statement 3 is necessarily true.

4. “Zindle snicker-snacked a borogove.”  
   No information about Zindle acting as the subject of snicker-snacking is given.  Could be true or false.  Not forced.

5. “Zindle snicker-snacked every borogove.”  
   Same reason as (4); nothing compels it.  Not forced.

6. “Not all borogoves snicker-snacked Zindle.”  
   Directly contradicted by A4 (“every borogove …”), so statement 6 is necessarily false.

Conclusion

The only statement that must be true under the given conditions is:

3. A borogove snicker-snacked Zindle.

   and U(Rafin) (Rafin is uffish).

¬ ∃x (B(x) ∧ G(x))  
So, there is no x such that B(x) and G(x); in words,

No borogove gimbled.

2. “No borogoves gimbled.” – Must be true (derived above).  
3. “A borogove gimbled.” – Impossible under the premisses (the negation of 2).

Nothing else is forced:

  “Only toves gyred OR every tove galumphed over Rafin” is satisfied in a particular model.

Thus, of the six candidate statements, the one that must be true in every admissible situation is

(1)  ∃b (Borogove(b) ∧ ∀t (Tove(t) → Galumphs(b,t)))         – “A borogove galumphed over every tove.”  
(2)  ∀b (Borogove(b) → Chortles(Bungo,b))                     – “Bungo chortled at every borogove.”  
(3)  ¬Galumphs(Zindle,Bungo) → [∀r (Rath(r) → Chortles(r,Bungo)) → ∀b (Borogove(b) → Chortles(b,Bungo))].

From premise (2) and the fact that there is at least one borogove, it follows immediately that  
∃b (Borogove(b) ∧ Chortles(Bungo,b));  
in words, “Bungo chortled at a borogove.”

No other premise links borogoves’ behaviour toward Bungo.  
It is easy to build two consistent models that show the remaining candidate statements are not forced:

Model A – Every borogove chortles at Bungo  
• Let the only borogove be Bungo himself.  
• Then Bungo (a borogove) chortles at Bungo, so a borogove chortles at Bungo and, trivially, every borogove does.  
• This model falsifies statement 5 (“Not all borogoves chortled …”) and statement 6 (“No borogoves chortled …”).

Model B – No borogove chortles at Bungo  
• Take a borogove b₁ distinct from Bungo; stipulate that no borogove chortles at Bungo.  
• Clause (3) is made true by letting Zindle galumph over Bungo (its negation is then false).  
• All premises are satisfied while statements 3, 4 are false and statement 6 is true.

Because models exist in which each of statements 1 and 3–6 is false, none of them is logically forced.

Therefore the only statement that must be true is:

2.  Bungo chortled at a borogove.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. No borogoves chortled at a borogove that chortled at it. No toves chortled at Rafin and every borogove chortled at Zindle. Every rath galumphed over Rafin and Rafin isn't a tove.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Zindle is a borogove.
2. Zindle isn't a tove.
3. Zindle isn't a borogove.
4. Zindle is a tove.
5. Rafin is a tove.
results.model_response:
Let

T(x): “x is a tove”  
B(x): “x is a borogove”  
R(x): “x is a rath”  
C(x, y): “x chortled at y”  
G(x, y): “x galumphed over y”

Given facts

1. ∀x∀y [ B(x) ∧ B(y) ∧ C(x, y) ∧ C(y, x) → ⊥ ]  
   (no two borogoves chortle at one another)

2. ∀x [ T(x) → ¬C(x, Rafin) ]  
   (no tove chortled at Rafin)

3. ∀x [ B(x) → C(x, Zindle) ]  
   (every borogove chortled at Zindle)

4. ∀x [ R(x) → G(x, Rafin) ]  
   (every rath galumphed over Rafin)

5. ¬T(Rafin)  
   (Rafin is not a tove)

and that every individual is **exclusively** a tove, borogove or rath and there is at least one of each.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Zindle **cannot be a borogove**  
    Suppose B(Zindle).  
    By (3) with x = Zindle we must have C(Zindle, Zindle).  
    So a borogove (Zindle) chortles at a borogove (itself) that
    chortled at it (again itself), contradicting (1).
    Hence B(Zindle) is impossible.

2.  Because Zindle cannot be a borogove, the only possibilities left
    are “tove” or “rath”.  Both are easily realised without violating
    any of the given conditions, so neither of them is forced.

3.  Therefore the only fact that must always hold about Zindle is that
    he is **not** a borogove.

4.  Statement 5 (“Rafin is a tove”) is the negation of the given fact
    (5) and is therefore always false.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Truth-status of the candidate statements

1. Zindle is a borogove.  Always false  
2. Zindle isn’t a tove.  May be true or false  
3. Zindle isn’t a borogove. Must be true  
4. Zindle is a tove.  May be true or false  
5. Rafin is a tove.  Always false

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Final answer

Only statement 3 (“Zindle isn’t a borogove”) must be true.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. A tove galumphed over a rath that galumphed over a tove. Rafin didn't galumph over Zindle and every tove gyred. If Rafin is a tove, then Bungo galumphed over Zindle only if every rath galumphed over Rafin.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Not all toves gyred.
2. No toves gyred.
3. Zindle gyred.
4. A tove gyred.
5. Zindle and Rafin gyred.
6. Only toves gyred.
results.model_response:
Premises (formalised)

1. Everything is exactly one of: tove (T), borogove (B) or rath (R) and each class is non-empty.  
2. ∃ x ∃ y ∃ z [T(x) ∧ R(y) ∧ T(z) ∧ Galumphed(x,y) ∧ Galumphed(y,z)].  
   (A tove galumphed over a rath that galumphed over a tove.)  
3. ¬ Galumphed(Rafin , Zindle).  
4. ∀x [T(x) → Gyred(x)].          (Every tove gyred.)  
5. T(Rafin) → ( Galumphed(Bungo , Zindle) → ∀x (R(x) → Galumphed(x , Rafin)) ).

Candidate conclusions

1. Not all toves gyred            ⇒  ∃x [T(x) ∧ ¬Gyred(x)]  
2. No toves gyred                 ⇒  ∀x [T(x) → ¬Gyred(x)]  
3. Zindle gyred                   ⇒  Gyred(Zindle)  
4. A tove gyred                   ⇒  ∃x [T(x) ∧ Gyred(x)]  
5. Zindle and Rafin gyred         ⇒  Gyred(Zindle) ∧ Gyred(Rafin)  
6. Only toves gyred               ⇒  ∀x [Gyred(x) → T(x)]

What the premises force

• From premise 4 and the fact that at least one tove exists (premise 1),
  every tove gyres, hence at least one gyres.
  Therefore statement 4 (“A tove gyred”) is unavoidable – it must be true.

Showing the other statements are not forced

Construct a model that satisfies all premises but falsifies each of the other statements:

Domain: {t1, t2, r1, b1}  
Tove:  t1, t2          (non-empty)  
Rath:  r1              (non-empty)  
Borogove: b1           (non-empty)  

Names:  Rafin = r1,  Zindle = b1,  Bungo = t1.

Galumphed relation:  t1 → r1,  r1 → t2 (and nothing else).  
Gyred predicate:     true of t1 and t2, false of r1 and b1.

• Premise 2 is met (t1→r1→t2).  
• ¬Galumphed(Rafin,Zindle) holds (r1 does not galumph over b1).  
• All toves (t1,t2) gyre, fulfilling premise 4.  
• Rafin is not a tove, so the conditional in premise 5 is vacuously true.

Now evaluate the six statements in this model:

1. Not all toves gyred  false (both toves gyre).  
2. No toves gyred    false.  
3. Zindle gyred     false (b1 does not gyre).  
4. A tove gyred     true (t1 gyres).  
5. Zindle and Rafin gyred false.  
6. Only toves gyred   true in this model—but we can make a second
   model identical except that r1 also gyres; all premises still hold
   while statement 6 becomes false.  Hence 6 is not forced.

Because statements 1, 2, 3, 5, 6 can be false in some model that meets every
premise, none of them is logically necessary.

Conclusion

The only statement that must be true in every situation described is:

4. A tove gyred.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Every tove snicker-snacked a borogove. A borogove chortled at a rath that chortled at a tove. Every borogove snicker-snacked Zindle but it is not the case that Rafin is uffish. Every tove is uffish unless no borogoves are beamish. A borogove chortled at Zindle if and only if no toves snicker-snacked Zindle.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. No borogoves snicker-snacked Zindle.
2. Not all borogoves snicker-snacked Zindle.
3. Zindle is uffish.
4. Zindle snicker-snacked every borogove.
5. Zindle snicker-snacked a borogove.
6. A borogove snicker-snacked Zindle.
results.model_response:
Must-true statement: 6.  A borogove snicker-snacked Zindle.

Reasoning (sketch).

1. The premiss “Every borogove snicker-snacked Zindle” together with “There is at least one borogove” guarantees the existence of (at least) one borogove that snicker-snacks Zindle, so 6 is forced.

2. Because every borogove snicker-snacks Zindle, it is impossible that
   • no borogove snicker-snacked Zindle (statement 1), or that
   • not all borogoves snicker-snacked Zindle (statement 2).
   Both 1 and 2 are therefore false, not true.

3, 4, 5.  Nothing in the premisses fixes Zindle’s category or behaviour apart from being the object of the borogoves’ snicker-snack.  It is easy to build models in which
   • Zindle is (or is not) uffish,
   • Zindle snicker-snacks every, some, or no borogoves.
   Hence statements 3, 4 and 5 are not entailed.

Conclusion: among the six candidate sentences, only statement 6 must hold in every situation satisfying the given conditions.

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Not all toves galumphed over Rafin. A tove galumphed over a borogove. Every tove galumphed over every rath.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Rafin is a borogove.
2. Rafin isn't a rath.
3. Rafin is a tove.
4. Rafin isn't a tove.
5. Rafin isn't a borogove.
6. Rafin is a rath.
results.model_response:
Let  

• T(x) = “x is a tove”  
• B(x) = “x is a borogove”  
• R(x) = “x is a rath”  
• G(x,y) = “x galumphed over y”  

Facts given  

1. Exactly one of T, B, R holds for every object; each class is non-empty.  
2. ¬∀x (T(x) → G(x,Rafin)) (“Not all toves galumphed over Rafin”).  
   Equivalently: ∃t [T(t) ∧ ¬G(t,Rafin)].  
3. ∃t ∃b [T(t) ∧ B(b) ∧ G(t,b)].  
4. ∀t ∀r [(T(t) ∧ R(r)) → G(t,r)]. (“Every tove galumphed over every rath”).

Deduction about Rafin

Suppose, for contradiction, that Rafin were a rath: R(Rafin).

Then, by (4), for every tove t we would have G(t,Rafin).

That contradicts (2), which says there exists a tove t such that ¬G(t,Rafin).

Therefore Rafin cannot be a rath:

¬R(Rafin)  → Rafin isn’t a rath.  (Statement 2 is true.)

No further restriction arises from the facts:

• Rafin may be a borogove: construct a model with one borogove (Rafin), two toves where one fails to galumph over Rafin, and define galumphing so that a tove galumphs over some borogove.

• Rafin may be a tove: construct a model with Rafin as a tove, another tove that does not galumph over Rafin, a borogove over which some tove galumphs, and the required galumphing to all raths.

Since both kinds of models satisfy all the given facts, neither “Rafin is a borogove” nor “Rafin is a tove” (nor their negations) is forced.

Conclusion

The only statement that must be true is:

2. Rafin isn’t a rath.

All the other statements can be true in some models and false in others.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. No borogoves galumphed over a tove. Bungo galumphed over a tove. If Zindle won't burble, then not all toves galumphed over Bungo only if every rath chortled at Bungo.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Bungo isn't a borogove.
2. Zindle will burble.
3. Bungo is a borogove.
4. If Bungo isn't a borogove, then Bungo is a borogove.
5. Bungo isn't a borogove only if Bungo is a borogove.
6. Zindle and Bungo will burble.
results.model_response:
The information we are given is:

(A) Every creature is exactly one of: tove, borogove, rath – and there is at least one of each.  
(B) No borogove galumphed over a tove.  
(C) Bungo galumphed over a tove.  
(D) If Zindle won’t burble, then  
    (not-all toves galumphed over Bungo) → (every rath chortled at Bungo).

Step 1 – What can be deduced about Bungo?  
From (C) Bungo is the subject that galumphed; the object is a tove.  
If Bungo were a borogove, (B) would be violated (a borogove would have galumphed over a tove).  
Therefore Bungo cannot be a borogove.  
So:  ¬(Bungo is a borogove).

That is the only unavoidable conclusion.  (D) places a conditional restriction that becomes relevant only if “Zindle won’t burble”, but it can always be satisfied (for instance, by letting every tove galumph over Bungo, making the antecedent of the inner conditional false).  Hence Zindle’s behaviour and the raths’ chortling are not forced.

Step 2 – Test each statement.

1. Bungo isn’t a borogove.  
   Must be true (deduced above).

2. Zindle will burble.  
   Not forced; Zindle may or may not burble.

3. Bungo is a borogove.  
   Impossible (contradicts the deduction in Step 1).

4. If Bungo isn’t a borogove, then Bungo is a borogove.  
   Antecedent is always true, consequent always false ⇒ the whole conditional is always false.

5. Bungo isn’t a borogove only if Bungo is a borogove.  
   “Only if” means the same conditional as in (4), so it is likewise always false.

6. Zindle and Bungo will burble.  
   Not forced by the premises.

Final answer  
The only statement that must be true is:

1. Bungo isn’t a borogove.

results.model_response:

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Every tove snicker-snacked every borogove that snicker-snacked every rath. Zindle didn't snicker-snacked Zindle and Rafin isn't a tove. A rath is uffish and Rafin isn't a tove.

3. Zindle is uffish.
4. Zindle didn't snicker-snacked Zindle and a rath is uffish.
5. Zindle and Rafin are uffish.
6. Zindle is uffish and a rath snicker-snacked Zindle.
results.model_response:
Let  

T(x) … “x is a tove”  
B(x) … “x is a borogove”  
R(x) … “x is a rath” (every individual is exactly one of these three kinds; at least one of each exists)  

S(x,y) … “x snicker-snacked y”  
U(x) … “x is uffish”.

Premises

1. ∀x ( T(x) → ∀y ( B(y) ∧ ∀z ( R(z) → S(y,z) )  → S(x,y) ) )  
   (Every tove snicker-snacked every borogove that snicker-snacked every rath.)

2. ¬ S(Zindle , Zindle) (Zindle didn’t snicker-snack Zindle.)

3. ¬ T(Rafin) (Rafin isn’t a tove.)

4. ∃x ( R(x) ∧ U(x) ) (Some rath is uffish.)

From (2) we already know “Zindle didn’t snicker-snack Zindle,” and from (4) we already know “a rath is uffish.”

Nothing else in the premises fixes whether Zindle (or Rafin) is uffish, or whether any rath snicker-snacked Zindle, etc.  In fact we can build a perfectly legal model in which

• no one snicker-snacks anybody,  
• Zindle is a borogove (or a tove or a rath—any choice works),  
• Rafin is a borogove,  
• one particular rath r₁ is uffish, the others are not,  

and all four premises are satisfied.  
In that model

– Statement 1 (“Zindle isn’t uffish”) is false if we simply stipulate U(Zindle).  
– Statement 2 is false because no rath snicker-snacks Zindle.  
– Statement 3 (“Zindle is uffish”) can be made false just by stipulating ¬ U(Zindle).  
– Statement 5 (“Zindle and Rafin are uffish”) and Statement 6 (“Zindle is uffish and a rath snicker-snacked Zindle”) are also easily falsified.

But Statement 4 (“Zindle didn’t snicker-snack Zindle and a rath is uffish”) is exactly the conjunction of the two facts we are given outright, so it is true in every possible model that obeys the premises.

Therefore, the only statement that must be true is

4. Zindle didn’t snicker-snack Zindle and a rath is uffish.

   the antecedent (T(y) ∧ SS(y,x)) is always false, so the implication is
   vacuously true.  
   Therefore the existential statement is satisfied, and (4) is true in
   every model that satisfies the given facts.

5. ∃x[T(x) ∧ ∀y(T(y)→SS(x,y))]

   Not true.

6. ∀x∀y[(T(x)∧T(y))→SS(x,y)]  
   Same violation of F1.  
   Not true.

Hence the only statement that must be true is

Statement 4: “A tove snicker-snacked every tove that snicker-snacked it.”

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. There is a borogove that every tove galumphed over. Every borogove snicker-snacked every tove that snicker-snacked every tove. No toves chortled at Rafin and Rafin chortled at Zindle. Every tove chortled at Bungo only if Rafin snicker-snacked Rafin. No toves snicker-snacked Zindle if and only if a rath gimbled.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. No toves galumphed over a borogove.
2. A tove galumphed over every borogove.
3. Every tove galumphed over every borogove.
4. Every tove galumphed over a borogove.
5. Not all toves will whiffle and every tove will whiffle.
6. No toves galumphed over every borogove.
results.model_response:
Let  

T(x)  = “x is a tove”  
B(x)  = “x is a borogove”  
R(x)  = “x is a rath”  
G(x,y)= “x galumphed over y”

The universe is partitioned into toves, borogoves and raths and contains at least one member of each kind.

Premise (P):  ∃b ( B(b) ∧ ∀t ( T(t) → G(t , b) ) )  
“There is a borogove that every tove galumphed over.”

The six candidate statements:

1. ¬∃t∃b ( T(t) ∧ B(b) ∧ G(t , b) )  
2. ∃t ( T(t) ∧ ∀b ( B(b) → G(t , b) ) )  
3. ∀t∀b ( T(t) ∧ B(b) → G(t , b) )  
4. ∀t∃b ( T(t) ∧ B(b) ∧ G(t , b) )  
5. (¬∀t W(t)) ∧ (∀t W(t))   (W = “whiffle”)  
6. ∀t ¬∀b ( B(b) → G(t , b) )

(The other given premises involve snicker-snacking, chortling and gimb­ling.  
None of them mention the relation “galumphed over”, so they cannot affect
the truth of the six statements except via (P).)

Now check each statement against (P).

Statement 1: “No tove galumphed over a borogove.”  
(P) says every tove galumphed over one particular borogove, so 1 is
false in every model that satisfies the premises. It is not forced to be
true.

Statement 2: “Some tove galumphed over every borogove.”  
(P) does not say that any tove galumphed over all borogoves, only over
one.  A model with two borogoves in which each tove galumphs over just
the special one in (P) satisfies all premises but makes 2 false.  
Hence 2 is not forced.

Statement 3: “Every tove galumphed over every borogove.”  
Same counter-model as for 2 shows 3 need not hold.

Statement 4: “Every tove galumphed over a borogove.”  
From (P) take the borogove b that every tove galumphed over.  
For any tove t, G(t , b) holds, and b is a borogove.  
Therefore ∀t∃b ( T(t) ∧ B(b) ∧ G(t , b) ).  
So 4 must be true.

Statement 5: “Not all toves whiffle and every tove whiffles.”  
The two conjuncts are mutually contradictory in a non-empty tove domain
(we are told there is at least one tove).  5 cannot be true in any
model, hence it certainly is not entailed.

Statement 6: “No tove galumphed over every borogove.”  
Again, a model with two borogoves in which one particular tove galumphs
over both satisfies the premises and falsifies 6.  So 6 is not forced.

Conclusion:  
Exactly one of the six statements is forced by the given information.

Final answer:  Statement 4 (and only statement 4) must be true.

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Zindle and Rafin are uffish. A rath snicker-snacked Bungo. A borogove is uffish and not all borogoves are beamish.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Rafin isn't uffish.
2. Zindle isn't uffish.
3. Rafin and Bungo are uffish.
4. A borogove is uffish but it is not the case that Zindle is uffish.
5. A borogove is uffish and Zindle is uffish.
6. Zindle and Bungo are uffish.
results.model_response:
Only statement 5 must be true.

Explanation:

• It is stated outright that Zindle is uffish, so the second half of 5 is guaranteed.  
• It is also stated that “a borogove is uffish,” which means at least one borogove is uffish, so the first half of 5 is likewise guaranteed.  

No other listed statement is forced by the information given (indeed, some directly conflict with it), so only statement 5 is necessarily true.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Zindle isn't mimsy. Bungo snicker-snacked Zindle. Not all toves snicker-snacked Bungo. Every tove galumphed over every borogove that galumphed over it. Zindle didn't snicker-snacked Bungo just in case every borogove snicker-snacked Bungo.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. A tove galumphed over a borogove.
2. A tove galumphed over every borogove that galumphed over it.
3. No toves galumphed over every borogove.
4. Every tove galumphed over every borogove.
5. Every tove galumphed over a borogove.
6. A tove galumphed over every borogove.
results.model_response:
Let  

T(x)  = “x is a tove”  
B(x)  = “x is a borogove”  
R(x)  = “x is a rath”            (T, B and R are mutually exclusive and each is non-empty)  

G(x,y) = “x galumphed over y”  
S(x,y) = “x snicker-snacked y”  

Facts supplied

F1  S(Bungo , Zindle).  
F2  ¬∀x (T(x) → S(x , Bungo))               (≡ ∃x (T(x) ∧ ¬S(x,Bungo))).  
F3  ∀x (T(x) → ∀y ((B(y) ∧ G(y,x)) → G(x,y))).  
F4  ¬S(Zindle , Bungo) ↔ ∀y (B(y) → S(y , Bungo)).  

What must follow from F1–F4?

----------------------------------------------------
Statement-by-statement check
----------------------------------------------------

1. ∃x∃y (T(x) ∧ B(y) ∧ G(x,y))  
   – No.  A model with no galumphing at all (G never true) satisfies F1–F4, so (1) can be false.

2. ∃x (T(x) ∧ ∀y ((B(y) ∧ G(y,x)) → G(x,y)))  
   – Yes.  F3 already says “every tove” has this property and at least one tove exists, so (2) is always true.

3. ¬∃x (T(x) ∧ ∀y (B(y) → G(x,y)))  
   – No.  Let one tove galumph over every borogove while no borogove galumphs over that tove; this obeys F1–F4, so (3) can be false.

4. ∀x∀y ((T(x) ∧ B(y)) → G(x,y))  
   – No.  The same “no-galumphing” model refutes (4).

5. ∀x (T(x) → ∃y (B(y) ∧ G(x,y)))  
   – No.  Again refuted by a model with no galumphing.

6. ∃x (T(x) ∧ ∀y (B(y) → G(x,y)))  
   – No.  The no-galumphing model makes (6) false.

----------------------------------------------------
Conclusion
----------------------------------------------------

The only statement that must be true in every situation satisfying the given information is

2.  A tove galumphed over every borogove that galumphed over it.

All the other five statements can be either true or false depending on how the galumph-relation is filled out, so none of them is forced.

Final answer: Only statement 2 must be true.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. No toves chortled at a tove. Rafin is a tove unless a rath galumphed over Bungo. If no borogoves snicker-snacked Bungo, then not all borogoves snicker-snacked Rafin. If every rath snicker-snacked Bungo, then Rafin is a tove only if every borogove chortled at Bungo. Bungo chortled at Bungo and every rath snicker-snacked Zindle.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Rafin isn't a tove.
2. Bungo isn't a tove.
3. Zindle isn't a tove.
4. Zindle is a tove.
5. Rafin is a tove.
6. Bungo is a tove.
results.model_response:
The only fact that the premises force on us concerns Bungo’s species.

1. Premise:  No toves chortled at a tove.  
2. Premise:  Bungo chortled at Bungo.

If Bungo were a tove, line 2 would give an instance (Bungo, Bungo) of “a tove chortled at a tove”, contradicting line 1.  
Therefore Bungo cannot be a tove; he must be either a borogove or a rath.

None of the other premises put any unavoidable restriction on the kinds of Rafin or Zindle, nor on Bungo’s being something other than a tove.  It is easy to build one model in which Rafin is a tove and another in which Rafin is not; likewise for Zindle.  Hence no statement about Rafin’s or Zindle’s kind is forced.

Thus the sole statement that must be true in every situation satisfying the premises is:

2. Bungo isn't a tove.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Zindle didn't snicker-snacked Zindle but it is not the case that Bungo didn't chortle at Bungo. Zindle didn't chortle at Zindle only if Bungo is a borogove. A borogove chortled at Rafin and every rath snicker-snacked Zindle.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Zindle isn't a rath.
2. Rafin is a borogove.
3. Zindle is a borogove.
4. Rafin isn't a borogove.
5. Zindle is a rath.
6. Zindle isn't a borogove.
results.model_response:
Let  

T(x) ≡ “x is a tove”  
B(x) ≡ “x is a borogove”  
R(x) ≡ “x is a rath”  

Exactly one of T, B, R holds for every individual and each kind occurs at least once.

Given facts  

F1. ¬ snicker(Zindle,Zindle).  
F2. chortle(Bungo,Bungo).                       (because “it is not the case that Bungo didn’t …”)  
F3. (¬ chortle(Zindle,Zindle)) → B(Bungo).  
F4. ∀x ( B(x) → chortle(x,Rafin) ).  
F5. ∀x ( R(x) → snicker(x,Zindle) ).

───────────────────────────────────────────────
Must Zindle be a rath?

Assume, for contradiction, R(Zindle).  
By F5 this gives snicker(Zindle,Zindle), contradicting F1.  
Therefore R(Zindle) is impossible.

So ¬ R(Zindle) is forced; i.e. statement 1 (“Zindle isn’t a rath”) is necessarily true.

───────────────────────────────────────────────
Anything else forced?

• Zindle could be a borogove or a tove (both fit the data), so statements 3, 5, 6 are not forced.  
• Rafin’s kind is never constrained by the premises, so statements 2 and 4 are not forced.

───────────────────────────────────────────────
Final answer

The only statement that must be true is:

1. Zindle isn’t a rath.

Statements 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 are not necessarily true.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Every rath will whiffle unless not all toves will burble. Not all toves will burble or no toves are uffish. No toves are uffish unless every tove will burble.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Every tove is uffish.
2. Every tove will whiffle.
3. Only toves are uffish.
4. No toves are uffish.
5. A borogove will burble and no borogoves will burble.
6. A tove is uffish.
results.model_response:
From the three given rules we obtain these precise logical forms ( “T” = tove, “R” = rath, “Burb” = will burble, “Whif” = will whiffle, “Uff” = is uffish).

R1 (∀T Burb)  ⇒  (∀R Whif)  
R2 ¬(∀T Burb)  ∨  (∀T ¬Uff)  
R3 ¬(∀T Burb)  ⇒  (∀T ¬Uff)

Analyse the consequences:

• If ¬(∀T Burb) is true, R3 gives (∀T ¬Uff).  
• If ¬(∀T Burb) is false (so every tove burbles), R2 forces (∀T ¬Uff).  

Therefore, in every possible case we must have  

(∀T ¬Uff).  (No toves are uffish.)

Nothing else is forced:

– Some models make all raths whiffle, others don’t (because R1 only applies when every tove burbles).  
– There can be uffish borogoves or raths, so “only toves are uffish” need not hold.  
– “Every tove is uffish” and “A tove is uffish” are impossible, because we just showed no tove is uffish.  
– The conjunction “a borogove will burble and no borogoves will burble” is self-contradictory and is certainly not forced.

Thus exactly one of the listed statements is guaranteed true in every situation:

Statement 4.  No toves are uffish.
