results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Rafin didn't galumph over Rafin. Only borogoves are beamish. Bungo galumphed over every borogove. No toves galumphed over Bungo and Bungo didn't snicker-snacked Zindle. Not all raths snicker-snacked Rafin only if not all borogoves galumphed over Bungo.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Zindle is beamish.
2. Only borogoves are beamish and no toves galumphed over Bungo.
3. If Bungo is beamish, then not all toves galumphed over Bungo only if no borogoves are beamish.
4. If Bungo galumphed over Bungo, then not all toves galumphed over Bungo only if no borogoves are beamish.
5. Zindle and Rafin are beamish.
6. Not all borogoves are beamish and no toves galumphed over Bungo.
results.model_response:
The information we are given already includes the two facts

• Only borogoves are beamish.  
• No toves galumphed over Bungo.

Therefore the compound statement  

2.  “Only borogoves are beamish and no toves galumphed over Bungo.”  

is necessarily true in every situation that satisfies the premises.

For each of the other proposed statements one can construct a model that meets every one of the premises yet makes the statement false; hence none of them is forced to be true.

Consequently, the only statement that must be true is

Statement 2.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Every tove gyred. No toves chortled at Rafin and every borogove chortled at Zindle. A borogove is mimsy unless every tove will burble.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. No toves chortled at Rafin and every tove gyred.
2. Zindle will burble.
3. Rafin will burble.
4. Zindle won't burble.
5. Zindle and Rafin will burble.
6. Rafin chortled at Rafin just in case not all toves gyred.
results.model_response:
The facts we are told are part of the description of the “situation”; therefore every interpretation of that situation has to satisfy them.

Facts (restated in symbolic form).

F1. ∀x (Tove(x) → Gyred(x))                                     – every tove gyred.  
F2. ∀x (Tove(x) → ¬Chortled-at(x , Rafin))                     – no tove chortled at Rafin.  
F3. ∀x (Borogove(x) → Chortled-at(x , Zindle))                 – every borogove chortled at Zindle.  
F4. (¬∀y (Tove(y) → Will-burble(y))) → ∀x (Borogove(x) → Mimsy(x))  
    – if not every tove will burble, then every borogove is mimsy.  
F5. The three kinds (tove, borogove, rath) are mutually exclusive and exhaustive and each is non-empty.

To find which of the listed statements must be true, we keep F1–F5 fixed and see whether the candidate statement holds in every model that satisfies them.

1. “No toves chortled at Rafin and every tove gyred.”
   This is exactly the conjunction of facts F1 and F2, so it is true in every admissible model.  
   ⇒ MUST be true.

2. “Zindle will burble.”
3. “Rafin will burble.”
4. “Zindle won’t burble.”
5. “Zindle and Rafin will burble.”

   None of the premises connect “will-burble” with either Zindle or Rafin, so we can build a model in which they do burble and another in which they do not.  Hence none of the statements 2–5 is forced by the premises.  

6. “Rafin chortled at Rafin just in case not all toves gyred.”
   Premise F1 tells us that all toves gyred, so the right-hand side of the biconditional (“not all toves gyred”) is always false.  
   Whether the left-hand side (“Rafin chortled at Rafin”) is true or false is unconstrained provided Rafin is not a tove.  
   • If Rafin is a borogove (or a rath) we can let him chortle at himself, making the left-hand side true and the biconditional false.  
   Therefore statement 6 is not forced by the premises.

Conclusion: the only statement that must hold in every situation that satisfies the given facts is statement 1.

Final answer: Statement 1 only.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Rafin chortled at Zindle and every rath chortled at Zindle. If a rath chortled at Zindle, then every rath chortled at Rafin. If a borogove snicker-snacked Bungo, then Rafin is a tove only if every rath chortled at Bungo.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. No raths chortled at Rafin.
2. A borogove will burble and no borogoves will burble.
3. Not all raths chortled at Rafin.
4. Rafin chortled at every rath.
5. Every rath chortled at Rafin.
6. Rafin chortled at a rath.
results.model_response:
Let  

T(x) = “x is a tove”,  
B(x) = “x is a borogove”,  
R(x) = “x is a rath”,  
C(x,y) = “x chortled at y”,  
S(x)   = “x snicker-snacked Bungo”.  

Facts that are given

1. T, B, R form an exclusive and exhaustive classification; there is at least one object of each sort.  
2. C(Rafin,Zindle).  
3. ∀x (R(x) → C(x,Zindle)).  
4. [∃x (R(x) ∧ C(x,Zindle))] → ∀x (R(x) → C(x,Rafin)).  
5. If ∃x (B(x) ∧ S(x)), then T(Rafin) → ∀x (R(x) → C(x,Bungo)).

Step 1  
Because there is at least one rath and every rath chortled at Zindle (Fact 3),
∃x (R(x) ∧ C(x,Zindle)) is true.

Step 2  
By Fact 4 and the truth of its antecedent obtained in Step 1 we get

  ∀x (R(x) → C(x,Rafin)).

So, necessarily,

  Every rath chortled at Rafin.

Step 3  
Now test each candidate statement.

1. “No raths chortled at Rafin.” Contradicted by Step 2 → not necessarily true.  
2. “A borogove will burble and no borogoves will burble.” A self-contradiction, never necessarily true.  
3. “Not all raths chortled at Rafin.” Contradicted by Step 2.  
4. “Rafin chortled at every rath.” Nothing forces Rafin to chortle at any rath, so not necessarily true.  
5. “Every rath chortled at Rafin.” Established in Step 2 – must be true.  
6. “Rafin chortled at a rath.” Zindle could be a rath or not; the statement is not forced.

Conclusion  
The only statement that must be true in every situation satisfying the given information is

5. Every rath chortled at Rafin.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. There is a borogove that every borogove snicker-snacked. Every tove is uffish or not all toves will whiffle. If Bungo is a borogove, then Zindle isn't a borogove. Rafin gimbled and every tove will whiffle. Zindle isn't mimsy and Bungo isn't mimsy.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. A borogove will burble and no borogoves will burble.
2. No toves are uffish.
3. Only toves are uffish.
4. Not all toves are uffish.
5. Zindle will whiffle.
6. Every tove is uffish.
results.model_response:
We can treat the whimsical words as ordinary logical predicates.

Given premises  
P1  Every creature is exactly one of: tove, borogove or rath (and each class is non-empty).  
P2  ∃b (borogove(b) ∧ ∀x (borogove(x) → snicker-snacked(x , b))).  
P3  (∀x (tove(x) → uffish(x))) ∨ ¬∀x (tove(x) → whiffle(x)).  
P4  borogove(Bungo) → ¬borogove(Zindle).  
P5  gimbled(Rafin) ∧ ∀x (tove(x) → whiffle(x)).  
P6  ¬mimsy(Zindle) ∧ ¬mimsy(Bungo).

Step 1:  From P5 we know that every tove will whiffle:  
∀x (tove(x) → whiffle(x)) is true.

Step 2:  Examine the disjunction in P3.  
The right-hand disjunct ¬∀x (tove(x) → whiffle(x)) is the negation of the sentence we have just affirmed in Step 1, so it is false.  
Therefore the left-hand disjunct must be true:

∀x (tove(x) → uffish(x)).      (1)

Statement (1) tells us that every tove is uffish.

Step 3:  Check the candidate conclusions.

1. “A borogove will burble and no borogoves will burble.”  
   This is self-contradictory; it is not forced by the premises.

2. “No toves are uffish.”  
   Contradicts (1); cannot be required.

3. “Only toves are uffish.”  
   (1) does not exclude the possibility that borogoves or raths are also uffish, so this is not forced.

4. “Not all toves are uffish.”  
   Directly contradicts (1); not forced.

5. “Zindle will whiffle.”  
   We do not know Zindle’s species; if Zindle is not a tove, (1) and P5 give us nothing.  So this is not forced.

6. “Every tove is uffish.”  
   This is exactly what we proved in (1); it must be true in every model of the premises.

Hence the only statement that must be true is:

6. Every tove is uffish.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Every borogove chortled at every borogove that chortled at it. A rath galumphed over a borogove that galumphed over a borogove. Zindle isn't mimsy and Rafin is a tove.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. A borogove chortled at every borogove.
2. No borogoves chortled at every borogove.
3. A borogove chortled at a borogove.
4. Every borogove chortled at every borogove.
5. A borogove chortled at every borogove that chortled at it.
6. Every borogove chortled at a borogove.
results.model_response:
Premises  
P1  Every creature is either a tove, a borogove or a rath (exclusively), and there is at least one borogove.  
P2  For every borogove B and every borogove C: if C chortles at B, then B chortles at C.  
P3  (Galumphing statement – irrelevant to chortling.)  
P4  (Zindle/Rafin note – irrelevant.)

Analysis of the candidate conclusions

1. “A borogove chortled at every borogove.”  
   Not forced – it is consistent with the premises that no borogove chortles at anyone.

2. “No borogoves chortled at every borogove.”  
   Also not forced – it is consistent with the premises that some borogove does chortle at everyone.

3. “A borogove chortled at a borogove.”  
   Not forced – the premises allow the possibility that no chortling occurs at all.

4. “Every borogove chortled at every borogove.”  
   Not forced – same counter-example as above (zero chortling).

5. “A borogove chortled at every borogove that chortled at it.”  
   The premise P2 says that this property holds for every borogove.  
   Since at least one borogove exists, it is certainly true that a (i.e. at least one) borogove has that property.  
   Therefore statement 5 must be true.

6. “Every borogove chortled at a borogove.”  
   Not forced – there may be borogoves that never chortle.

Conclusion  
The only statement that is guaranteed by the premises is:

5. A borogove chortled at every borogove that chortled at it.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Every tove snicker-snacked Zindle. Zindle gyred unless Zindle isn't a borogove. Not all toves snicker-snacked Zindle just in case no toves snicker-snacked Zindle.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Zindle snicker-snacked a tove.
2. Not all toves snicker-snacked Zindle.
3. Zindle snicker-snacked every tove.
4. Zindle gyred.
5. No toves snicker-snacked Zindle.
6. A tove snicker-snacked Zindle.
results.model_response:
Let  

T(x) = “x is a tove”  
B(x) = “x is a borogove”  
R(x) = “x is a rath”  
S(x , y) = “x snicker-snacked y”  
G(x) = “x gyred”  

Z denotes the particular creature “Zindle”.

Facts given

1.  Partition of the universe:  T(x) ∨ B(x) ∨ R(x), exactly one of them for every x, with at least one member of each kind.

2.  Every tove snicker-snacked Zindle:  
   ∀x (T(x) → S(x , Z)).

3.  “Zindle gyred unless Zindle isn’t a borogove.”  
   “P unless Q” is standardly rendered “if ¬Q then P”.  
   Hence:  B(Z) → G(Z).

4.  “Not all toves snicker-snacked Zindle just in case no toves snicker-snacked Zindle.”  
   “P just in case Q” means “P ↔ Q”, so  
   (¬∀x (T(x) → S(x , Z))) ↔ (¬∃x (T(x) ∧ S(x , Z))).

Immediate consequences

• From 2 there is at least one tove that snicker-snacked Zindle (because there is at least one tove).

• The biconditional in 4 is satisfied automatically when all toves do in fact snicker-snack Zindle (the left-hand side is false, the right-hand side is false, so the biconditional is true).  Thus 4 imposes no further restriction.

What must be true?

1. Zindle snicker-snacked a tove.  
   Not forced: if Zindle happens to be a rath who snicker-snacks nobody, 1 is false.

2. Not all toves snicker-snacked Zindle.  
   Contradicted by fact 2; so it is certainly not a necessary truth.

3. Zindle snicker-snacked every tove.  
   No information at all about what Zindle snicker-snacked; could be either way.

4. Zindle gyred.  
   Only guaranteed if Zindle is a borogove; Zindle might instead be a tove or a rath, so 4 is not forced.

5. No toves snicker-snacked Zindle.  
   Contradicted by fact 2.

6. A tove snicker-snacked Zindle.  
   This follows directly from fact 2 together with the existence of at least one tove, so 6 is true in every admissible model.

To show nothing else is forced, construct a model in which only 6 holds:

• Objects:  Z (a rath), T₁ (a tove), B₁ (a borogove).  
• Snicker-snacking:  S(T₁ , Z) is true; all other S-facts are false.  
• Gyre:  no one gyres.  

All stated premises are satisfied, yet statements 1–5 are all false while 6 is true.

Therefore the only statement that must be true is:

Statement 6.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Every rath is uffish. No toves chortled at Rafin but it is not the case that Rafin chortled at Zindle. A rath galumphed over Zindle and Rafin didn't chortle at Bungo.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. No raths are uffish.
2. Not all raths are uffish.
3. Only raths are uffish.
4. A rath is uffish.
5. Zindle and Rafin are uffish.
6. Zindle is uffish.
results.model_response:
We know the following for certain:

1. There is at least one rath (because each of the three kinds exists).
2. Every rath is uffish.

From 1 and 2 it follows that at least one rath is uffish.

Nothing in the information given forces any conclusions about whether
• all uffish things are raths,
• any non-rath (tove or borogove) is or is not uffish,
• Zindle or Rafin (or Bungo) are uffish.

Hence the only statement that must hold in every situation satisfying the premises is:

4. A rath is uffish.

All the other statements can be false in some permissible model, so they are not logically compelled.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Rafin gimbled. Rafin and Zindle are uffish. If not all borogoves are mimsy, then a borogove snicker-snacked Rafin only if Rafin didn't snicker-snacked Rafin.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Rafin gimbled but it is not the case that Rafin isn't uffish.
2. Rafin and Zindle gimbled.
3. Zindle and Rafin gimbled.
4. Rafin gimbled and Rafin isn't uffish.
5. Zindle gimbled.
6. Zindle didn't gimble.
results.model_response:
Premises

1. ∀x [tove(x) ∨ borogove(x) ∨ rath(x)], and the three kinds are mutually exclusive; each kind has at least one member.
2. gimbled(Rafin).
3. uffish(Rafin) ∧ uffish(Zindle).
4. If ¬∀x(borogove(x) → mimsy(x)), then
      [∃y(borogove(y) ∧ snicker-snacked(y,Rafin)) → ¬snicker-snacked(Rafin,Rafin)].

Nothing in (1)–(4) says anything about Zindle’s having gimbled, nor does it deny it; nothing contradicts Rafin’s being uffish, etc.  The set of premises is consistent, so we ask which candidate sentences are true in every model that satisfies them.

Candidate sentences

1. gimbled(Rafin) ∧ ¬¬uffish(Rafin) (that is, gimbled(Rafin) ∧ uffish(Rafin))
   – Both conjuncts are explicitly given in premises (2) and (3).
   – Therefore sentence 1 is true in every model.

2. gimbled(Rafin) ∧ gimbled(Zindle)
3. gimbled(Zindle) ∧ gimbled(Rafin)
5. gimbled(Zindle)
6. ¬gimbled(Zindle)

   – Premises are completely silent about whether Zindle gimbled, so there are models in which she did and models in which she didn’t.  Hence sentences 2, 3, 5, and 6 are not forced to be true.

4. gimbled(Rafin) ∧ ¬uffish(Rafin)
   – The second conjunct contradicts premise (3).  Thus sentence 4 is false in every model (certainly not “must be true”).

Conclusion

The only statement that must be true, given the premises, is statement 1.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. A tove galumphed over Rafin. Not all toves snicker-snacked Zindle and only borogoves are beamish. Not all toves snicker-snacked Zindle and not all toves galumphed over Zindle.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Only borogoves are beamish and not all toves galumphed over Zindle.
2. Zindle isn't beamish.
3. No toves galumphed over Zindle or no borogoves are beamish.
4. No toves galumphed over Zindle unless no borogoves are beamish.
5. Zindle is beamish.
6. Zindle and Rafin are beamish.
results.model_response:
Premises (re-phrased symbolically)

1. ∀x [tove(x) ∨ borogove(x) ∨ rath(x)] and these three sets are pair–wise exclusive and each non-empty.  
2. ∃t [tove(t) ∧ galumphed-over(t , Rafin)].  
3. ¬∀t [tove(t) → snicker-snacked(t , Zindle)]        (so some tove did NOT snicker-snack Zindle).  
4. ∀x [beamish(x) → borogove(x)]                     (“Only borogoves are beamish”).  
5. ¬∀t [tove(t) → galumphed-over(t , Zindle)]         (so some tove did NOT galumph over Zindle).

Candidate conclusions

1. Only borogoves are beamish  ∧  not all toves galumphed over Zindle.  
   – This is exactly the conjunction of Premise 4 and Premise 5, so it is true in every model of the premises.

2. Zindle isn’t beamish.  
   – Consistent counter-model: let Zindle be a borogove and beamish.  Premises remain satisfied, so 2 is not forced.

3. (No toves galumphed over Zindle)  ∨  (No borogoves are beamish).  
   – Counter-model: let one tove galumph over Zindle and let some borogove be beamish. Both disjuncts are false while all premises hold, so the whole disjunction is not forced.

4. No toves galumphed over Zindle unless no borogoves are beamish  
   (formal reading: ¬(No borogoves are beamish) → No toves galumphed over Zindle).  
   – Same counter-model as for 3 falsifies this conditional, so 4 is not forced.

5. Zindle is beamish.  
   – Counter-model: make Zindle a rath; the premises stay true, so 5 is not forced.

6. Zindle and Rafin are beamish.  
   – Counter-model: make neither of them beamish; premises stay true, so 6 is not forced.

Conclusion

The only statement that must be true, given the premises, is statement 1.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Zindle or Bungo are mimsy. A borogove chortled at Rafin but it is not the case that Rafin isn't uffish. Zindle gyred and every rath gyred.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Rafin gyred.
2. Zindle and Bungo gyred.
3. A borogove chortled at Rafin but it is not the case that Zindle gyred.
4. Zindle and Rafin gyred.
5. Zindle didn't gyre.
6. A borogove chortled at Rafin and Zindle gyred.
results.model_response:
The only statement that is forced to be true by the given information is

6. A borogove chortled at Rafin and Zindle gyred.

Reasoning:

• The premises explicitly state both “A borogove chortled at Rafin” and “Zindle gyred”, so their conjunction (6) must hold.

• None of the other candidate statements is guaranteed:
  – 1 and 4 depend on whether Rafin gyred, which is not fixed by the premises.  
  – 2 depends on whether Bungo gyred, which is likewise un-specified.  
  – 3 and 5 conflict with the explicit fact that Zindle did gyre, so they cannot be necessarily true.

Hence, statement 6 is the sole proposition that must be true in every situation satisfying the premises.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. A borogove will whiffle. A borogove is uffish and Rafin didn't snicker-snacked Bungo. A tove galumphed over Rafin only if not all borogoves galumphed over Bungo. If Bungo didn't snicker-snacked Bungo, then not all toves galumphed over Zindle only if not all toves galumphed over Bungo. If not all toves galumphed over Zindle, then Rafin won't whiffle only if no raths galumphed over Bungo.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Zindle and Bungo will whiffle.
2. Zindle and Rafin will whiffle.
3. A borogove is uffish and a borogove will whiffle.
4. Zindle won't whiffle.
5. Zindle will whiffle.
6. Every borogove is uffish and a borogove will whiffle.
results.model_response:
Necessary truths we can extract right away from the stipulations

1. Everything is either a tove, a borogove or a rath – and there is at least one of each.
2. “A borogove will whiffle” – i.e. every borogove whiffles.  
3. “A borogove is uffish” – i.e. every borogove is uffish.  
4. Rafin did not snicker-snack Bungo.

The remaining conditional sentences do not place any direct restriction on whether Zindle, Bungo or Rafin themselves whiffle; they only link those possibilities to various galumphing and snicker-snacking contingencies that are otherwise unconstrained.  
Consequently Zindle’s and Bungo’s whiffling (or not) can vary from model to model, so none of the options that mention their whiffling is forced.

What is forced is everything that follows from (2) and (3):

• Because there is at least one borogove and every borogove is uffish, “a borogove is uffish” is certainly true.  
• Because every borogove whiffles, “a borogove will whiffle” is certainly true.  
• And because the statements in (2) and (3) are universal, “every borogove is uffish” is certainly true.

Now we compare with the six candidate statements.

1. Zindle and Bungo will whiffle. – not forced.  
2. Zindle and Rafin will whiffle. – not forced.  
3. A borogove is uffish and a borogove will whiffle. – must be true (see above).  
4. Zindle won’t whiffle. – not forced.  
5. Zindle will whiffle. – not forced.  
6. Every borogove is uffish and a borogove will whiffle. – must be true (the first conjunct is guaranteed by (3) above, the second by (2) above).

Therefore the statements that must be true in every situation satisfying the given information are:

3 and 6.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Bungo didn't chortle at Bungo but it is not the case that Rafin isn't a tove. Zindle gyred but it is not the case that Rafin didn't chortle at Bungo. Only toves gimbled and no borogoves will whiffle.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Not all toves chortled at Bungo.
2. Bungo chortled at a tove.
3. Every tove chortled at Bungo.
4. Bungo chortled at every tove.
5. No toves chortled at Bungo.
6. A tove chortled at Bungo.
results.model_response:
From the information given we can deduce the following facts that hold in every possible interpretation of the story.

1. “Everything is either a tove, a borogove or a rath (and only one of them).”
2. “It is not the case that Rafin isn’t a tove.”  →  Rafin is a tove.
3. “It is not the case that Rafin didn’t chortle at Bungo.”  →  Rafin chortled at Bungo.
4. “Bungo didn’t chortle at Bungo.”  (This tells us nothing about anyone else’s chortling.)

Thus we have an individual – Rafin – who
• is a tove, and  
• chortled at Bungo.

So in every model that satisfies the premises

  A tove (namely Rafin) chortled at Bungo.

That is exactly statement 6.

The truth of the other proposed statements depends on what the rest of the (unspecified) toves, Bungo and the other creatures do, and the premises place no constraints on those possibilities. Therefore none of the other statements is forced to be true.

Hence the only statement that must be true is:

6. A tove chortled at Bungo.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Rafin snicker-snacked Rafin only if not all toves are beamish. A borogove is mimsy just in case every borogove snicker-snacked Bungo. Every rath snicker-snacked Bungo just in case every borogove snicker-snacked Bungo.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. If every borogove is mimsy, then every rath snicker-snacked Bungo only if Bungo didn't snicker-snacked Bungo.
2. If not all borogoves are mimsy, then Bungo didn't snicker-snacked Bungo only if every rath is mimsy.
3. If not all borogoves are mimsy, then every rath snicker-snacked Bungo only if Bungo didn't snicker-snacked Bungo.
4. A borogove is mimsy just in case every rath snicker-snacked Bungo.
5. Not all borogoves are mimsy if and only if every rath snicker-snacked Bungo.
6. Every borogove is mimsy just in case every rath snicker-snacked Bungo.
results.model_response:
From the three given facts we can introduce two key abbreviations.

P  =  every borogove snicker-snacked Bungo  
   (∀y)(Borogove(y) → S(y,Bungo))

Q  =  every rath snicker-snacked Bungo  
   (∀y)(Rath(y) → S(y,Bungo))

1.  “A borogove is mimsy just in case every borogove snicker-snacked Bungo’’  
  For every borogove x:  Mimsy(x) ↔ P.  
  Hence  

   All borogoves are mimsy (call this A)  ⇔ P.   (1)

2.  “Every rath snicker-snacked Bungo just in case every borogove snicker-snacked Bungo’’  
  P ↔ Q.                      (2)

Immediately from (1) and (2)

  A  ⇔ P  ⇔ Q                 (3)

so “every borogove is mimsy’’ and “every rath snicker-snacked Bungo’’ always have the same truth-value, and “not all borogoves are mimsy’’ always has the opposite truth-value.

Now check each candidate sentence.

1.  A → (Q → ¬S(Bungo,Bungo))  
    If A is true, (3) gives Q true, but nothing forces ¬S(Bungo,Bungo).  
    Counter-example possible ⇒ not forced.

2.  ¬A → (¬S(Bungo,Bungo) → every rath is mimsy)  
    With ¬A we have Q false (3).  We can make ¬S(Bungo,Bungo) true and pick a
    rath that is not mimsy.  Counter-example ⇒ not forced.

3.  ¬A → (Q → ¬S(Bungo,Bungo))  
    Because ¬A implies Q is false (3), the inner conditional is automatically
    true; if A is true the outer antecedent is false.  Therefore sentence 3
    is true in every model and is forced.

4.  For every borogove x: Mimsy(x) ↔ Q  
    But Mimsy(x) ↔ P and P ↔ Q, so this biconditional holds for every
    borogove.  Sentence 4 is forced.

5.  (¬A) ↔ Q  
    Yet (3) gives A ↔ Q, so ¬A ↔ Q is generally false.  Counter-example ⇒
    not forced.

6.  A ↔ Q  
    This is exactly (3).  Sentence 6 is forced.

Thus the statements that must be true in every situation satisfying the
premises are:

3, 4 and 6.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Zindle is uffish. No toves chortled at Rafin. There is not a rath that every rath chortled at.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Rafin isn't uffish.
2. Zindle and Rafin are uffish.
3. Zindle is uffish and no toves chortled at Rafin.
4. Rafin and Zindle are uffish.
5. Rafin is uffish.
6. Zindle isn't uffish.
results.model_response:
The only information we are given about the two named individuals is

• Zindle is uffish.  
• No toves chortled at Rafin.

Nothing else in the premises places ​any​ restriction on whether Rafin is (or is not) uffish.  
Consequently:

1. “Rafin isn’t uffish.” – could be true or false.  
2. “Zindle and Rafin are uffish.” – requires Rafin to be uffish, which isn’t guaranteed.  
3. “Zindle is uffish and no toves chortled at Rafin.” – both halves are exactly the premises, so this ​must​ be true.  
4. “Rafin and Zindle are uffish.” – again depends on Rafin; not guaranteed.  
5. “Rafin is uffish.” – not guaranteed.  
6. “Zindle isn’t uffish.” – contradicts a given premise, so it cannot be true.

Therefore the one and only statement that must be true in every situation satisfying the premises is statement 3.

Final answer: 3 only.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. No borogoves galumphed over a rath. Not all toves chortled at Bungo but it is not the case that Bungo didn't chortle at Bungo. No toves galumphed over Bungo and no toves chortled at Rafin.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Rafin chortled at every tove.
2. A tove chortled at Rafin.
3. Rafin chortled at a tove.
4. Every tove chortled at Rafin.
5. No toves chortled at Rafin and Rafin isn't a tove.
6. Not all toves chortled at Rafin.
results.model_response:
The only statement that must be true is  

6.  Not all toves chortled at Rafin.

Reasoning

•  From the given information we have the universal fact  
  “No toves chortled at Rafin.”  
 Formally:  ∀x (Tove(x) → ¬Chortled(x,Rafin)).

•  There is at least one tove in the universe.

•  Because at least one tove exists and none of them chortled at Rafin, it is certainly not the case that all toves chortled at Rafin.  
 Hence statement 6 is guaranteed to be true in every model of the premises.

The other statements are not forced:

1. Rafin chortled at every tove – not determined; Rafin might chortle at none.  
2. A tove chortled at Rafin – ruled out by the premises, so it cannot be true.  
3. Rafin chortled at a tove – not determined; may be false.  
4. Every tove chortled at Rafin – contradicted by the premises.  
5. No toves chortled at Rafin and Rafin isn’t a tove – the first half is true, the second half is undetermined, so the whole conjunctive statement is not guaranteed.

Therefore, the single statement that must hold is statement 6.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. A tove chortled at itself. Rafin chortled at Zindle and no toves chortled at Bungo. A rath is beamish and every rath gyred.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Not all toves chortled at Bungo.
2. Bungo chortled at a tove.
3. Not all toves chortled at Bungo and Bungo chortled at Bungo.
4. Every tove chortled at Bungo.
5. Bungo chortled at every tove.
6. A tove chortled at Bungo.
results.model_response:
The facts we are told are:

a) Every creature is exactly one of: tove, borogove, rath, and there is at least one of each.

b) A tove chortled at itself.     (∃x (Tove(x) ∧ Chortle(x,x)))

c) Rafin chortled at Zindle.      (Chortle(Rafin,Zindle))

d) No toves chortled at Bungo.    (∀x (Tove(x) → ¬Chortle(x,Bungo)))

e) A rath is beamish.             (∃x (Rath(x) ∧ Beamish(x)))

f) Every rath gyred.              (∀x (Rath(x) → Gyred(x)))


Now examine the six candidate statements.

1. Not all toves chortled at Bungo.
   Premise (d) says no tove chortled at Bungo at all, and premise (a) guarantees there is at least one tove.  
   Hence there certainly is a tove that did not chortle at Bungo.  
   Statement 1 must therefore be true.

2. Bungo chortled at a tove.
   We are told nothing about Bungo’s chortling, so this may be true or false in different models.  
   Not forced.

3. Not all toves chortled at Bungo and Bungo chortled at Bungo.
   The first half is forced (see 1) but the second half is not; the whole conjunction is therefore **not** forced.

4. Every tove chortled at Bungo.
   Directly contradicted by premise (d); it is impossible.

5. Bungo chortled at every tove.
   Again, Bungo’s chortling is unconstrained; this need not hold.

6. A tove chortled at Bungo.
   Premise (d) explicitly says the opposite; impossible.

Thus the only statement that must be true in every situation satisfying the given information is:

Statement 1.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Every rath snicker-snacked every borogove that snicker-snacked every rath. A rath galumphed over Zindle and Rafin didn't galumph over Zindle. If not all toves will whiffle, then Zindle didn't galumph over Bungo only if every rath galumphed over Rafin. Bungo is a rath and every rath snicker-snacked Zindle.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Zindle will whiffle.
2. Zindle and Bungo will whiffle.
3. Zindle won't whiffle.
4. Zindle and Rafin will whiffle.
5. A rath snicker-snacked Zindle and a rath galumphed over Zindle.
6. Zindle didn't snicker-snacked Zindle and a rath galumphed over Zindle.
results.model_response:
Only statement 5 is guaranteed to be true.

Reasoning  

1. From the premiss “every rath snicker-snacked Zindle” and the fact that “Bungo is a rath”, we know that (at least) the rath Bungo snicker-snacked Zindle. Hence “a rath snicker-snacked Zindle” is certain.

2. From the premiss “a rath galumphed over Zindle” we already have “a rath galumphed over Zindle”.

Combining 1 and 2 gives exactly statement 5:  
“A rath snicker-snacked Zindle and a rath galumphed over Zindle.”

Nothing supplied forces any particular creature (including Zindle, Bungo or Rafin) either to whiffle or not to whiffle, nor do we learn whether or not Zindle snicker-snacked Zindle. Consequently none of statements 1, 2, 3, 4 or 6 must hold in every model that satisfies the given information.

Therefore the sole statement that must be true is:

5. A rath snicker-snacked Zindle and a rath galumphed over Zindle.

Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Zindle didn't snicker-snacked Zindle and no toves chortled at Rafin. Not all raths galumphed over Rafin and Rafin isn't a tove. No toves galumphed over Zindle or Rafin is a tove. A rath snicker-snacked Bungo only if Bungo is a borogove. If a borogove snicker-snacked Rafin, then Zindle didn't chortle at Rafin only if not all toves galumphed over Bungo.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Every tove galumphed over Zindle.
2. A tove galumphed over Zindle.
3. Zindle galumphed over every tove.
4. Zindle galumphed over a tove.
5. No toves galumphed over Zindle.
6. Not all toves will whiffle and every tove will whiffle.
results.model_response:
The only statement that must be true is  

5. No toves galumphed over Zindle.

Reasoning  

1. One of the given facts is the disjunction  
   (No toves galumphed over Zindle) OR (Rafin is a tove).

2. Another given fact is that Rafin isn’t a tove.

3. Since the second disjunct is false, the disjunction can be true only if the first
   disjunct is true. Hence  
   No toves galumphed over Zindle.

4. That is exactly statement 5, so 5 must be true.

5. Because at least one tove exists, “No toves galumphed over Zindle” immediately
   falsifies statements 1 and 2.  
   The premises impose no constraints that force 3, 4 or 6 to be true, and it is
   easy to construct models in which each of them is false.

Therefore, statement 5 – and only statement 5 – is forced by the information given.

2. A borogove snicker-snacked a tove.
3. A tove snicker-snacked a borogove.
4. No toves snicker-snacked every borogove.
5. Every tove snicker-snacked every borogove.
6. Every tove snicker-snacked a borogove.
results.model_response:
Let  

Tove(x), Borogove(x), Rath(x) be the three (mutually exclusive) kinds,  
Snicker(x,y) = “x snicker-snacked y”,  
Chortle(x,y), Galumph(x,y), Burble(x), Gimble(x) as in the wording, and let the
named individuals be Bungo, Zindle and Rafin.  
The premisses can be formalised as

1.  ∀x (Tove(x) ∨ Borogove(x) ∨ Rath(x)),  each object falling in exactly one class;  
   ∃x Tove(x), ∃x Borogove(x), ∃x Rath(x).

2.  ∃b (Borogove(b) ∧ ∀t (Tove(t) → Snicker(b,t))).      (a borogove snicker-snacked every tove)

3.  ¬∀t (Tove(t) → Chortle(t,Bungo)).                    (not all toves chortled at Bungo)

4.  ¬Snicker(Bungo,Zindle).                              (Bungo didn’t snicker-snack Zindle)

5.  (∃b (Borogove(b) ∧ Galumph(b,Rafin))) ∨
     (∀b (Borogove(b) → Chortle(b,Bungo))).              (disjunction about galumphing/chortling)

6.  (¬∀t Tove(t)→Gimble(t)) ↔ (∃b (Borogove(b) ∧ Chortle(b,Zindle))). (biconditional)

7.  (¬∀t (Tove(t) → Burble(t))) ∨ (∃r (Rath(r) ∧ Burble(r))).        (burbling clause)


Which of the candidate conclusions is forced by these premisses?

A. ∃t (Tove(t) ∧ ∀b (Borogove(b) → Snicker(t,b)))                    (list-item 1)  
B. ∃b∃t (Borogove(b) ∧ Tove(t) ∧ Snicker(b,t))                       (list-item 2)  
C. ∃t∃b (Tove(t) ∧ Borogove(b) ∧ Snicker(t,b))                       (list-item 3)  
D. ¬∃t (Tove(t) ∧ ∀b (Borogove(b) → Snicker(t,b)))                   (list-item 4)  
E. ∀t∀b ((Tove(t) ∧ Borogove(b)) → Snicker(t,b))                     (list-item 5)  
F. ∀t (Tove(t) → ∃b (Borogove(b) ∧ Snicker(t,b)))                    (list-item 6)

Step 1.  What must be true?

From premiss 2 we have a particular borogove b₀ that snicker-snacked every tove.  
Because there is at least one tove (premiss 1), there is certainly some tove t₀ with  
Borogove(b₀) ∧ Tove(t₀) ∧ Snicker(b₀,t₀).  
Thus statement B (candidate 2) is satisfied in every model of the premisses.
So 2 must be true.

Step 2.  What is not forced?

• To make A, E or F hold we need snicker-snacking done by all (or some) toves.
  Nothing in the premisses obliges any tove to snicker-snack anyone.
  We can build a model in which no tove ever snicker-snacks; in such a model
  A, E and F are all false, showing they are not logically necessary.

• Statement D is the negation of A; it can be made true by the same model
  just mentioned, but we can also build a model in which some tove does
  snicker-snack every borogove (add one extra Snicker-pair) without violating
  any premiss, making D false there.  
  Hence D is not forced either.

• Statement C merely requires one tove to snicker-snack one borogove.
  In the first model (where no tove snickers) it is false, so it is not forced.

Since only B is inevitable while all other candidates can be falsified in some
model satisfying the premisses, the sole statement that must be true is:

A borogove snicker-snacked a tove.   (List item 2)

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Not all borogoves are beamish and a rath galumphed over Zindle. Every rath chortled at Zindle and a rath galumphed over Zindle. If Rafin is beamish, then every rath is beamish only if no borogoves are beamish.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. If every rath is beamish, then no borogoves are beamish only if Zindle chortled at Zindle.
2. No raths chortled at Zindle just in case no borogoves are beamish.
3. Not all borogoves are beamish and every rath chortled at Zindle.
4. Zindle isn't beamish.
5. Zindle and Rafin are beamish.
6. Zindle is beamish.
results.model_response:
The only assertion that is forced by the information given is statement 3.

Explanation  

• “Not all borogoves are beamish” is one of the stated facts.  
• “Every rath chortled at Zindle” is also one of the stated facts.  

Hence their conjunction in statement 3 must hold in every model that satisfies the premises.

All the other statements can be made true in some admissible models and false in others, so none of them is logically entailed.

Therefore, the set of statements that must be true is:
3. Not all borogoves are beamish and every rath chortled at Zindle.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. A borogove chortled at a rath that chortled at it. Every borogove chortled at Bungo or every tove snicker-snacked Zindle. Not all toves snicker-snacked Zindle and not all toves galumphed over Zindle.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Not all borogoves chortled at Bungo.
2. Bungo chortled at a borogove.
3. Every borogove chortled at Bungo.
4. Bungo isn't a borogove if and only if a borogove chortled at Bungo.
5. Bungo chortled at every borogove.
6. No borogoves chortled at Bungo.
results.model_response:
Because the sentence

“Every borogove chortled at Bungo or every tove snicker-snacked Zindle”

is stated to be true, while the later information

“Not all toves snicker-snacked Zindle”

tells us the second disjunct is false, the first disjunct must be true:

Every borogove chortled at Bungo.

All other given facts (the existence of at least one tove, borogove and rath, the mutual chortling between one borogove and one rath, and the statements about galumphing) do not force any further relationship involving Bungo. In particular we cannot deduce that:

• some borogove failed to chortle at Bungo,  
• Bungo chortled at any borogove,  
• Bungo is or is not a borogove,  
• Bungo chortled at every borogove, or  
• no borogove chortled at Bungo.

Therefore, of the six candidate statements, the only one that must be true is:

3. Every borogove chortled at Bungo.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Not all borogoves galumphed over Bungo. A tove galumphed over every rath. A borogove galumphed over every tove.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. A borogove galumphed over a tove.
2. A tove galumphed over every borogove.
3. Every tove galumphed over every borogove.
4. Every tove galumphed over a borogove.
5. A tove galumphed over a borogove.
6. No toves galumphed over every borogove.
results.model_response:
Let  

T(x) : x is a tove  
B(x) : x is a borogove  
R(x) : x is a rath  
G(x,y) : x galumphed over y  
“Bungo’’ is a fixed individual of the domain.

Facts that are given

F1  ∀x [T(x) ∨ B(x) ∨ R(x)] and the three classes are exclusive; each class is non–empty.  
F2  ¬∀x (B(x) → G(x,Bungo))      (at least one borogove did ​not​ galumph over Bungo).  
F3  ∃t [T(t) ∧ ∀r (R(r) → G(t,r))]  (some tove galumphed over every rath).  
F4  ∃b [B(b) ∧ ∀t (T(t) → G(b,t))]  (some borogove galumphed over every tove).

Which of the candidate statements follow from F1–F4?

-------------------------------------------------
1.  ∃x∃y [B(x) ∧ T(y) ∧ G(x,y)]

From F4 pick the borogove b that galumphed over every tove.  
Because there is at least one tove y, we have B(b) ∧ T(y) ∧ G(b,y).  
Hence statement 1 is forced to be true.

-------------------------------------------------
To show that none of the other statements is forced, build models that satisfy F1–F4 but falsify the particular statement.

Model (used for 2, 3, 4, 5):  
Domain = {t₁,t₂,b₁,b₂,r₁}.  
t’s are toves, b’s borogoves, r’s raths.

G-relation  
•  b₁ galumphs over every tove (to satisfy F4).  
•  t₁ galumphs over every rath (to satisfy F3).  
•  No tove galumphs over any borogove (so G(t,b) is always false).  
•  b₁ fails to galumph over Bungo (so F2 holds).

In this model:  
– A tove over every borogove (2) is false.  
– Every tove over every borogove (3) is false.  
– Every tove over a borogove (4) is false.  
– A tove over a borogove (5) is false.

Model (used for 6):  
Domain = {t₁,b₁,r₁}.  
Let t₁ galumph over r₁ (F3), let b₁ galumph over t₁ (F4),  
and also let t₁ galumph over b₁.  b₁ does not galumph over Bungo (F2).

Here a tove (t₁) galumphs over every borogove, so statement 6 is false.

Since each of the statements 2–6 can be false while all premises hold, none of them is entailed.

-------------------------------------------------
Final conclusion

The only statement that must be true in every situation satisfying the given information is:

1. A borogove galumphed over a tove.

Statements 2, 3, 4, 5 and 6 are not guaranteed.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Bungo is mimsy. No toves are mimsy. Every borogove galumphed over every tove.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Bungo is a tove.
2. Bungo isn't a tove.
3. Bungo isn't mimsy.
4. Bungo isn't a borogove.
5. Bungo is a borogove.
results.model_response:
The facts we are given are

1. Everything is **either** a tove, a borogove, or a rath (and nothing can be more than one of these).
2. There is at least one of each kind.
3. Bungo is **mimsy**.
4. No toves are mimsy.   (¬∃x (Tove(x) ∧ Mimsy(x)))
5. Every borogove galumphed over every tove.   (irrelevant to Bungo’s species)

From 3 and 4

Bungo is mimsy  
No tove is mimsy  
⇒ Bungo cannot be a tove.

That single deduction is all that the premises force on us:

• Bungo is **not** a tove – this must be true.  
• Nothing in the premises tells us whether Bungo is (or is not) a borogove or a rath; both remain possible.  
• Premise 3 already states that Bungo is mimsy, so “Bungo isn’t mimsy” is necessarily false.

Therefore:

1. Bungo is a tove.          Not forced (actually impossible)  
2. Bungo isn’t a tove.         Must be true  
3. Bungo isn’t mimsy.         Must be false  
4. Bungo isn’t a borogove.      Undetermined  
5. Bungo is a borogove.       Undetermined

Final answer: Statement 2 is the only one that must be true.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. A rath galumphed over Zindle unless a rath is beamish. No borogoves are beamish unless only toves gyred. Every rath gyred but it is not the case that Bungo is a rath.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Zindle gyred.
2. Only raths gyred.
3. Zindle and Bungo gyred.
4. No raths gyred.
5. Not all raths gyred.
6. A rath gyred.
results.model_response:
Let  

 T(x) : x is a tove  
 Bo(x): x is a borogove  
 R(x) : x is a rath  
 Gy(x): x gyred  
 Be(x): x is beamish  
 Gα(x) : x galumphed over Zindle  

and take Z for Zindle and B for Bungo.

Given facts

F1. ∀x [T(x) ⊕ Bo(x) ⊕ R(x)] and each class is non-empty.  
F2. (¬∃x [R(x) ∧ Be(x)]) → ∃x [R(x) ∧ Gα(x,Z)]             (“A rath galumphed … unless a rath is beamish.”)  
F3. (¬ Only-toves-gyred) → ∀x [Bo(x) → ¬Be(x)]             (“No borogoves are beamish unless only toves gyred.”)  
  “Only toves gyred” means ∀x (Gy(x) → T(x)).  
F4. ∀x [R(x) → Gy(x)]                                     (“Every rath gyred.”)  
F5. ¬R(B)                                                 (“Bungo is not a rath.”)

Step 1.  “Only toves gyred” is impossible.  
Because F4 says every rath gyred and at least one rath exists (F1), anyone who gyred cannot all be toves.  
Hence ¬ Only-toves-gyred is always true in any model of the premises.

Step 2.  From F3 and Step 1 we obtain  
  ∀x [Bo(x) → ¬Be(x)] .  
So no borogove is beamish.  
(This tells us nothing about raths, toves, Zindle or Bungo with respect to Gy.)

Step 3.  What the premises force about gyred things.

•  From F4 and the fact that at least one rath exists, we have  
 ∃x [R(x) ∧ Gy(x)] .  
So at least one gyred rath is guaranteed.

•  Nothing else in the premises requires Zindle, Bungo, any tove or any borogove to gyre or not to gyre; both possibilities are compatible with all the facts.

Constructing two explicit models shows this:

Model A  
 Classes: one rath r, one tove t (= Z), one borogove b (= B).  
 Gyred: r only.  
 Beamish: none.  
 Galumphed: r galumphed over Z (allowed because no rath is beamish).  
 All premises hold.

Model B  
 Same as A, except both r and t gyre.  
 All premises still hold (raths still gyre, “only toves gyred” still false, etc.).

Consequences for the candidate conclusions

1. Zindle gyred.  – true in Model B, false in Model A ⇒ not forced.  
2. Only raths gyred. – true in Model A, false in Model B ⇒ not forced.  
3. Zindle and Bungo gyred. – false in Model A ⇒ not forced.  
4. No raths gyred. – contradicts F4 ⇒ impossible, hence not “must be true”.  
5. Not all raths gyred. – contradicts F4 ⇒ impossible, hence not “must be true”.  
6. A rath gyred. – holds in every model because F1 ensures a rath exists and F4 says every rath gyred.

Therefore, the only statement that must be true is

6. A rath gyred.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Not all toves chortled at Bungo and no toves chortled at Rafin. A rath snicker-snacked Zindle and a borogove will whiffle. No toves chortled at Bungo and a borogove will whiffle. If Zindle gimbled, then every rath chortled at Rafin only if a borogove snicker-snacked Bungo. If every rath snicker-snacked Bungo, then Bungo didn't snicker-snacked Bungo only if not all toves will whiffle.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Every tove snicker-snacked Zindle or not all raths chortled at Bungo.
2. If not all toves chortled at Bungo, then every rath snicker-snacked Bungo only if Zindle chortled at Zindle.
3. Not all toves chortled at Bungo and a rath snicker-snacked Zindle.
4. Zindle and Rafin will whiffle.
5. Zindle will whiffle.
6. Every tove snicker-snacked Zindle unless not all raths chortled at Bungo.
results.model_response:
The premises give us (among other things)

• “No toves chortled at Bungo.”  
  (Hence, certainly “Not all toves chortled at Bungo.”)

• “A rath snicker-snacked Zindle.”  

Consequently the conjunction  
“Not all toves chortled at Bungo and a rath snicker-snacked Zindle”  
is forced to be true in every model of the information.

None of the other six candidate statements is forced:

– 1 and 6 can be falsified in a model where every rath chortles at Bungo while some tove fails to snicker-snack Zindle.  
– 2 can be falsified by making every rath snicker-snack Bungo but having Zindle not chortle at Zindle.  
– 4 and 5 can be falsified by letting Zindle (and Rafin) belong to a species other than borogove and simply not whiffle.

Therefore the only statement that must be true is

3. Not all toves chortled at Bungo and a rath snicker-snacked Zindle.

6. Rafin gyred.
results.model_response:
Premises (paraphrased)

P1  Everything in the universe is either a tove, a borogove or a rath, and there is at least one of each.  
P2  There exists a rath such that every tove chortled at it.  
P3  If not all borogoves are mimsy, then (Rafin is beamish → every borogove chortled at Bungo).  
P4  Only borogoves are beamish.          ( beamish → borogove )  
P5  Every rath gyred.                    ( rath → gyred )

What must follow

Because P5 tells us that every single rath gyred and P1 guarantees the existence of at least one rath, it is absolutely certain that

∃x (x is a rath ∧ x gyred).

That is exactly statement 4:  “A rath gyred.”

None of the other candidate statements is forced:

• 1 (“Not all raths gyred”) is the negation of P5, so it is false in every admissible situation.  
• 2 (“Only raths gyred”) is not guaranteed; the premises say nothing about whether toves or borogoves might also gyre.  
• 3 (“No raths gyred”) directly contradicts P5.  
• 5 and 6 concern Rafin and Bungo.  The premises never say that either of them is a rath (or gyred for some other reason), so their gyring cannot be guaranteed.

Therefore the sole statement that must be true in every situation satisfying the premises is:

4.  A rath gyred.

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. A rath snicker-snacked a borogove that snicker-snacked a borogove. Not all raths galumphed over Rafin and every borogove snicker-snacked Zindle. Zindle didn't galumph over Bungo only if not all raths snicker-snacked Rafin.

2. A borogove snicker-snacked Zindle.
3. Zindle is a borogove.
4. Zindle snicker-snacked every borogove.
5. Zindle snicker-snacked a borogove.
6. Not all borogoves snicker-snacked Zindle.
results.model_response:
Premises (informally restated)

A. There is at least one tove, one borogove and one rath, and every creature is exactly one of those three kinds.

B. A rath snicker-snacked a borogove that (in turn) snicker-snacked a borogove.
   (∃r)(∃b₁)(∃b₂) [Rath(r) ∧ Borogove(b₁) ∧ Borogove(b₂) ∧ SS(r , b₁) ∧ SS(b₁ , b₂)]

C. Not all raths galumphed over Rafin.
   (∃r)[Rath(r) ∧ ¬G(r , Rafin)]

D. Every borogove snicker-snacked Zindle.
   (∀b)[Borogove(b) → SS(b , Zindle)]

E. Zindle didn’t galumph over Bungo only if not all raths snicker-snacked Rafin.
   ¬G(Zindle , Bungo) → (∃r)[Rath(r) ∧ ¬SS(r , Rafin)]

Consequences that follow with certainty

1. From (A) there exists at least one borogove.
2. From (D) every borogove snicker-snacks Zindle.
3. Combining 1 and 2 gives: ∃b [Borogove(b) ∧ SS(b , Zindle)].

That existential statement is exactly option 2.

Checking each of the proposed statements

1. “No borogoves snicker-snacked Zindle.”  
   Premise D says the opposite. Not necessarily true.

2. “A borogove snicker-snacked Zindle.”  
   Guaranteed by the reasoning above. MUST be true.

3. “Zindle is a borogove.”  
   Premises place no restriction on Zindle’s kind. Need not be true.

4. “Zindle snicker-snacked every borogove.”  
   Nothing given implies this. Need not be true.

5. “Zindle snicker-snacked a borogove.”  
   Could be arranged either way without violating any premise. Not forced.

6. “Not all borogoves snicker-snacked Zindle.”  
   Premise D says all borogoves do snicker-snack Zindle, so 6 is false, not necessary.

Therefore, the only statement that must be true in every situation satisfying the premises is:

2. A borogove snicker-snacked Zindle.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Every rath chortled at a rath that chortled at it. A rath snicker-snacked Zindle and a borogove will whiffle. Every rath chortled at Zindle and no toves chortled at Bungo.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Zindle chortled at a rath.
2. Zindle will whiffle.
3. Not all raths chortled at Zindle.
4. No raths chortled at Zindle.
5. A rath chortled at Zindle.
6. Zindle chortled at every rath.
results.model_response:
The premises give us the following facts.

1. Every creature is (exclusively) a tove, a borogove, or a rath, and there is at least one of each.
2. Every rath chortled at a rath that chortled at it.            (Reciprocity among raths only)
3. A rath snicker-snacked Zindle.                                (There exists a rath that did so)
4. A borogove will whiffle.                                      (If borogove then whiffle)
5. Every rath chortled at Zindle.                                (Universal → Zindle)
6. No toves chortled at Bungo.                                   (Irrelevant to the six test statements)

Now examine the candidate statements.

1. “Zindle chortled at a rath.”  
   Zindle would have to be a rath (so that premise 2 applies) before we could force any such
   chortling; but Zindle could instead be a tove or a borogove, in which case nothing requires
   Zindle to chortle at anyone.  Hence statement 1 need not be true.

2. “Zindle will whiffle.”  
   Only borogoves are certain to whiffle (premise 4).  Zindle could be a rath or a tove, so this
   is not guaranteed.

3. “Not all raths chortled at Zindle.”  
   Premise 5 says every rath chortled at Zindle, so this statement is necessarily false.

4. “No raths chortled at Zindle.”  
   Directly contradicted by premise 5; necessarily false.

5. “A rath chortled at Zindle.”  
   There is at least one rath (premise 1) and every rath chortled at Zindle (premise 5).  
   Therefore at least one rath certainly chortled at Zindle.  Statement 5 must be true.

6. “Zindle chortled at every rath.”  
   Even if Zindle happens to be a rath, premise 2 only forces Zindle to chortle at those raths
   that chortled at it; it does not force chortling at all raths, and if Zindle is not a rath
   nothing forces any chortling at all.  So this is not guaranteed.

Conclusion: The only statement that must be true in every situation satisfying the premises is

5. A rath chortled at Zindle.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. A rath snicker-snacked itself. Every borogove is uffish and Rafin is uffish. Zindle isn't mimsy and a rath galumphed over Zindle.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Every borogove is uffish and a rath galumphed over Zindle.
2. Not all raths galumphed over Rafin and every rath galumphed over Rafin.
3. Zindle and Rafin are mimsy.
4. Rafin is mimsy.
5. Zindle is mimsy.
6. Rafin and Zindle are mimsy.
results.model_response:
Premises (restated)

P1. Every creature is exactly one of: tove, borogove, rath; and at least one of each exists.  
P2. A (some) rath snicker-snacked itself.  
P3. Every borogove is uffish.  
P4. Rafin is uffish.  
P5. Zindle is not mimsy.  
P6. A (some) rath galumphed over Zindle.

Now test each candidate statement.

1. “Every borogove is uffish and a rath galumphed over Zindle.”
   – The first half is precisely P3; the second half is P6.  
   – Both are given as premises, so their conjunction is certainly true in every model of the premises.  
   ⇒ Statement 1 must be true.

2. “Not all raths galumphed over Rafin and every rath galumphed over Rafin.”
   – The two clauses are logical negations of each other (given that at least one rath exists by P1).  
   – They cannot both be true simultaneously, so the whole conjunction is impossible.  
   ⇒ Statement 2 is necessarily false.

3. “Zindle and Rafin are mimsy.”  
4. “Rafin is mimsy.”  
5. “Zindle is mimsy.”  
6. “Rafin and Zindle are mimsy.”

   From P5 we know Zindle is not mimsy.  
   – Any statement claiming Zindle is mimsy (3, 5, 6) is therefore false.  
   – Whether Rafin is mimsy is completely undetermined by the premises, so statements 3, 4, 6 are not forced to be true.

Conclusion

The only statement that must be true is:

1. Every borogove is uffish and a rath galumphed over Zindle.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. No toves chortled at Rafin and every rath chortled at Zindle. A rath will burble unless every borogove chortled at Bungo. Only toves are mimsy and Bungo is a rath.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Zindle chortled at Bungo.
2. Zindle and Rafin will burble.
3. Zindle will burble.
4. Zindle didn't chortle at Bungo.
5. Bungo didn't chortle at Zindle.
6. Bungo chortled at Zindle.
results.model_response:
Required facts:

A. Everything is either a tove, a borogove or a rath, with at least one of each.  
B. No tove chortled at Rafin.  
C. Every rath chortled at Zindle.  
D. “A rath will burble unless every borogove chortled at Bungo.”  
   (If there is even one borogove that did not chortle at Bungo, then every rath burbles; otherwise nothing is said about raths burbling.)  
E. Only toves are mimsy.  
F. Bungo is a rath.

Now examine each candidate statement.

1 .  Zindle chortled at Bungo – not forced.  
   Nothing in A-F mentions anyone chortling at Bungo except borogoves (condition D) and that is optional. We can build a model where Zindle does or does not chortle at Bungo, so this statement is not necessary.

2 .  Zindle and Rafin will burble – not forced.  
   Zindle and Rafin could be toves or borogoves (not raths); if so rule D never applies to them, so they may fail to burble. Hence the statement is not necessary.

3 .  Zindle will burble – same reasoning as in 2; not necessary.

4 .  Zindle didn’t chortle at Bungo – the negation of statement 1; since 1 is not forced, 4 isn’t forced either.

5 .  Bungo didn’t chortle at Zindle – impossible.  
   From C and F, Bungo is a rath, and every rath chortled at Zindle, so Bungo certainly did chortle at Zindle. Therefore statement 5 is always false, not always true.

6 .  Bungo chortled at Zindle – MUST be true.  
   As just noted, Bungo is a rath (F) and every rath chortled at Zindle (C), so this statement holds in every possible situation satisfying the given conditions.

Hence the only statement that must be true is:

6. Bungo chortled at Zindle.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. No toves snicker-snacked a borogove that snicker-snacked it. Every tove snicker-snacked every borogove that snicker-snacked every tove. A rath snicker-snacked a tove that snicker-snacked a borogove.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Every tove snicker-snacked every borogove.
2. No toves snicker-snacked every borogove.
3. No toves snicker-snacked a borogove.
4. No borogoves snicker-snacked every tove.
5. A tove snicker-snacked every borogove.
6. Every tove snicker-snacked a borogove.
results.model_response:
Let  

T(x) = “x is a tove”  
B(x) = “x is a borogove”  
R(x) = “x is a rath”  
S(x,y) = “x snicker-snacked y”

The universe is partitioned into T, B and R, each non-empty.

Premises

P1  (No mutual T–B snicker-snacking)  
 ∀x∀y [ T(x) ∧ B(y) ∧ S(y,x) → ¬S(x,y) ].

P2  (Toves attack any borogove that attacks all toves)  
 ∀x∀y [ T(x) ∧ B(y) ∧ (∀z (T(z) → S(y,z))) → S(x,y) ].

P3  (There is a rath–tove–borogove chain)  
 ∃r∃t∃b [ R(r) ∧ T(t) ∧ B(b) ∧ S(r,t) ∧ S(t,b) ].

Candidate statements

1. ∀t∀b (T(t) ∧ B(b) → S(t,b))  
2. ¬∃t [T(t) ∧ ∀b (B(b) → S(t,b))]  
3. ¬∃t∃b [T(t) ∧ B(b) ∧ S(t,b)]  
4. ¬∃b [B(b) ∧ ∀t (T(t) → S(b,t))]  
5. ∃t [T(t) ∧ ∀b (B(b) → S(t,b))]  
6. ∀t ∃b [T(t) ∧ B(b) ∧ S(t,b)]

Which ones are forced by P1–P3?

Step 1.  P3 instantly refutes statement 3, because it furnishes a concrete tove t and borogove b with S(t,b).

Step 2.  Show that statement 4 MUST be true.

Assume, for contradiction, that there exists a borogove b₀ that snicker-snacked every tove:  
 ∀t (T(t) → S(b₀,t)).

Then by P2 every tove snicker-snacks b₀, i.e. ∀t (T(t) → S(t,b₀)).  
For any particular tove t₀ we now have both S(b₀,t₀) and S(t₀,b₀), contradicting P1.  
Hence no such borogove can exist; statement 4 is necessarily true.

Step 3.  The remaining statements are not compelled.

• Statement 1 can be made false by letting some borogove attack no toves (P2 then imposes no obligation on the toves), so some tove could omit attacking that borogove.

• Statement 2 can be falsified by creating a tove that attacks all borogoves, provided none of the borogoves attack it (this respects P1 and P2).

• Statement 5 is the negation of statement 2, so it can also be either true or false depending on the model.

• Statement 6 can be falsified by having a tove that attacks no borogove (again consistent with all premises).

Therefore only statement 4 is forced by the premises.

Final answer: 4 only.

A rath snicker-snacked a tove that snicker-snacked a borogove. No toves snicker-snacked Zindle only if Zindle isn't a borogove. Not all toves snicker-snacked Zindle if and only if Rafin chortled at Bungo. If not all toves chortled at Bungo, then no borogoves are beamish only if Zindle chortled at Zindle.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. No raths chortled at every rath.
2. There is a rath that every rath chortled at.
3. A rath chortled at a rath.
4. No raths chortled at a rath.
5. A rath chortled at every rath.
6. Every rath chortled at every rath.
results.model_response:
Only statement 3 is forced by the information given.

Reasoning

1.  From “Every rath chortled at a rath that chortled at it” and the fact that at least one rath exists, pick any rath r.  
   The clause guarantees a (possibly identical) rath s such that r chortled s.  
   Therefore “A rath chortled at a rath’’ is inevitably true.  
   So statement 3 must hold.

2.  The same clause does not require anything stronger.  
   We can build two different models that respect every one of the
   stated conditions yet show the other five candidate sentences may be either true or false:

   • Model A – nobody chortles at every rath  
     Raths R₁ and R₂ with R₁ ↔ R₂ (each chortles the other), no self-chortling.  
     All the other conditions (snicker-snacks, Zindle, Bungo, etc.) can be satisfied as described; here  
     - sentence 1 is true,  
     - sentences 2, 4, 5, 6 are false.

   • Model B – one rath chortles at every rath  
     Raths R₁ and R₂ with R₁ chortling both R₁ and R₂, R₂ chortling R₁.  
     Again the remaining premises can be met; here  
     - sentence 1 is false,  
     - sentences 2, 5 may be true,  
     - sentences 4, 6 are false.

   Because the five disputed sentences can switch truth-value while all the given premises stay satisfied, none of them is logically forced.

Conclusion

3. A rath chortled at a rath.   (Must be true.)

All the other listed statements can be either true or false, so none of them is a necessary consequence of the premises.

Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. No toves chortled at Zindle. A borogove chortled at a tove that chortled at a borogove. If Bungo isn't a borogove, then no borogoves are mimsy.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Zindle chortled at a tove.
2. Zindle is mimsy.
3. A tove chortled at Zindle.
4. Every tove chortled at Zindle.
5. Not all toves chortled at Zindle.
6. Zindle chortled at every tove.
results.model_response:
We have the following facts.

1. Everything is exactly one of: tove, borogove or rath, and there is at least one of each.
2. No tove chortled at Zindle.                        (∗)
3. There exists a borogove B and a tove T such that  
   B chortled at T and T chortled at some borogove.      (gives a “chortling chain” but says
   nothing about Zindle)
4. If Bungo is not a borogove then no borogove is mimsy. (concerns Bungo and “mimsy” only)

Now test each candidate statement to see whether it must hold in every model that
satisfies the facts.

1. “Zindle chortled at a tove.”  
   Nothing given forces Zindle to chortle at anyone.  
   Possible model: nobody chortles at anyone at all; fact 3 can still be satisfied by
   a single triple (B,T,B).  So 1 need not be true.

2. “Zindle is mimsy.”  
   Only fact 4 talks about “mimsy”, and it involves Bungo, not Zindle.  Zindle can be
   mimsy or not, so 2 need not be true.

3. “A tove chortled at Zindle.”  
   This directly contradicts fact (∗).  Therefore 3 is never true, let alone
   necessarily true.

4. “Every tove chortled at Zindle.”  
   Also contradicts fact (∗).  Hence never true.

5. “Not all toves chortled at Zindle.”  
   Because there is at least one tove and, by fact (∗), no tove chortled at Zindle,
   it follows that at least one tove (in fact all of them) failed to chortle at
   Zindle.  Therefore statement 5 is true in every admissible model.

6. “Zindle chortled at every tove.”  
   Again, nothing forces Zindle to chortle at anyone at all, so 6 need not be true.

Conclusion:  
The only statement that must be true is

5. Not all toves chortled at Zindle.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. A borogove chortled at Rafin. Every borogove chortled at every rath that chortled at every tove. Zindle is uffish but it is not the case that Bungo didn't chortle at Bungo.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Rafin chortled at Rafin just in case a borogove chortled at Bungo.
2. A borogove chortled at Rafin and Rafin didn't chortle at Bungo.
3. A borogove chortled at Rafin and Bungo chortled at Bungo.
4. A borogove chortled at Rafin but it is not the case that Rafin didn't chortle at Bungo.
5. Rafin chortled at Rafin just in case not all borogoves chortled at Bungo.
6. A borogove chortled at Rafin but it is not the case that Bungo chortled at Bungo.
results.model_response:
Premises (formalised)

Let Tove(x), Borogove(x), Rath(x) be exclusive and exhaustive kinds  
(∀x [Tove(x) ∨ Borogove(x) ∨ Rath(x)] and no object is two of them) and let each kind be non-empty.

Let Ch(x,y) abbreviate ‘x chortled at y’.

1.  ∃b [Borogove(b) ∧ Ch(b , Rafin)]                “A borogove chortled at Rafin.”
2.  ∀b [ Borogove(b) → ∀r( Rath(r) ∧ ∀t(Tove(t)→Ch(r,t)) → Ch(b , r) ) ]  
    “Every borogove chortled at every rath that chortled at every tove.”
3.  ¬¬ Ch(Bungo , Bungo)                               “It is not the case that Bungo didn’t chortle at Bungo.”

From 3 we immediately have

A.  Ch(Bungo , Bungo).                               (Bungo chortled at Bungo.)

From 1 we immediately have

B.  ∃x [Borogove(x) ∧ Ch(x , Rafin)].                (Some borogove chortled at Rafin.)

Nothing else in the premises constrains the various other chortling relations, so whether
• Rafin chortles at herself,  
• Rafin chortles at Bungo,  
• every borogove chortles at Bungo, etc.  
is left completely open.

Now examine the six candidate statements.

1.  Ch(Rafin,Rafin) ↔ ∃x[ Borogove(x) ∧ Ch(x,Bungo) ]  
    Can fail: take a model in which some borogove chortles at Bungo but Rafin does not chortle at herself. (All premises can still be satisfied.)

2.  ∃x[ Borogove(x) ∧ Ch(x,Rafin) ]  ∧  ¬Ch(Rafin,Bungo)  
    The first conjunct is forced (see B) but the second is not; it may be true or false.

3.  ∃x[ Borogove(x) ∧ Ch(x,Rafin) ]  ∧  Ch(Bungo,Bungo)  
    Both conjuncts are guaranteed by B and A, hence the whole statement is forced true.

4.  ∃x[ Borogove(x) ∧ Ch(x,Rafin) ]  ∧  Ch(Rafin,Bungo)  
    First conjunct is forced, second isn’t; the statement need not hold.

5.  Ch(Rafin,Rafin) ↔ ¬∀x( Borogove(x) → Ch(x,Bungo) )  
    Make every borogove chortle at Bungo and let Rafin chortle at herself; left side true, right side false – the biconditional can fail.

6.  ∃x[ Borogove(x) ∧ Ch(x,Rafin) ]  ∧  ¬Ch(Bungo,Bungo)  
    Second conjunct contradicts A, so the whole sentence is always false.

Conclusion

The only statement that must be true in every model satisfying the given information is:

3.  A borogove chortled at Rafin and Bungo chortled at Bungo.

No other statement on the list is guaranteed.

A borogove is uffish if and only if a borogove chortled at Bungo. Rafin isn't beamish and Bungo is a rath. No borogoves will burble but it is not the case that Rafin didn't chortle at Bungo.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Rafin chortled at a rath.
2. A rath chortled at Rafin.
3. Rafin chortled at every rath.
4. No raths chortled at Rafin.
5. Every rath chortled at Rafin.
6. Not all raths chortled at Rafin.
results.model_response:
We look for what is true in every model that satisfies the given sentences.

Facts that are explicitly stated (or are immediate consequences):

F1  Everything is either a tove, a borogove or a rath, and there is at least one of each.

F2  Not all toves chortled at Rafin.  (∃ a tove that did not chortle at Rafin.)

F3  There exists a pair (T,R) with T a tove and R a rath such that  
  T chortled at R and R chortled at T.

F4  A borogove is uffish ⇔ a borogove chortled at Bungo.  
  (This only links two properties of borogoves; it puts no direct
  constraint on chortling to or from Rafin.)

F5  Rafin isn’t beamish.  (Irrelevant to chortling relations.)

F6  Bungo is a rath.

F7  “It is not the case that Rafin didn’t chortle at Bungo’’  
  ⇒ Rafin did chortle at Bungo.

The candidate statements to test:

1. Rafin chortled at a rath.  
2. A rath chortled at Rafin.  
3. Rafin chortled at every rath.  
4. No raths chortled at Rafin.  
5. Every rath chortled at Rafin.  
6. Not all raths chortled at Rafin.

Step 1:  What is forced?

Because of F6 and F7, Bungo is a rath and Rafin chortled at Bungo.  
Therefore Rafin chortled at (at least) one rath.  Statement 1 is true in every model.

Step 2:  Are any of the other statements forced?

• Statement 2 (“a rath chortled at Rafin’’) is not forced.  
  Build a model with a tove T and a rath R such that T ↔ R (satisfying F3).  
  Let Bungo be a different rath that does not chortle at Rafin; let no rath
  chortle at Rafin at all.  All premises are met, yet statement 2 is false.

• Statement 3 (“Rafin chortled at every rath’’) can be false in exactly the
  same model (Rafin chortles only at Bungo).

• Statement 4 (“no raths chortled at Rafin’’) can be made false:   
  take a model in which the rath that pairs with the tove in F3 happens also
  to chortle at Rafin.  All premises are still satisfied.

• Statement 5 (“every rath chortled at Rafin’’) is certainly not forced; the
  first counter-model above already violates it.

• Statement 6 (“not all raths chortled at Rafin’’) is also not forced, because
  we can make a model where every rath does chortle at Rafin (simply let
  every rath—including Bungo and the rath from F3—chortle at Rafin).

Hence only statement 1 holds in every possible situation.

Final answer: 1 only.

results.model_response:

P2  Zindle galumphed over Rafin.   (irrelevant to snicker-snacking)

P3  For every rath x and every rath y, x snicker-snacked y.   
     ( ∀x∀y (Rath(x) ∧ Rath(y) → SnSn(x,y)) )

P4  A rath will burble  OR  not all raths chortled at Bungo.  
     ( ∃x Rath(x)∧Burble(x)  ∨  ¬∀x (Rath(x)→Chortle(x,Bungo)) )

1. ¬∃y (Rath(y) ∧ ∀x (Rath(x)→SnSn(x,y)))
2. ¬∃x (Rath(x) ∧ ∀y (Rath(y)→SnSn(x,y)))
3.  ∃x (Rath(x) ∧ ∀y (Rath(y)→SnSn(x,y)))
4.  ∀x∀y (Rath(x)∧Rath(y) → ¬SnSn(x,y))
5.  ¬∃x∃y (Rath(x)∧Rath(y)∧SnSn(x,y)∧SnSn(y,x))
6.  Burble(Zindle)

Logical consequences of the premises

•  Because there is at least one rath (P1) and every rath snicker-snacks every rath (P3),  
   for ANY rath r and any rath s, SnSn(r,s) is true.  
   Hence for each rath r, r snicker-snacks every rath, and conversely every rath is
   snicker-snacked by every rath.

Result for each candidate statement

1. False – there certainly IS a rath that every rath snicker-snacked.  
2. False – many raths snicker-snacked every rath.  
3. True  – at least one (indeed every) rath snicker-snacked every rath.  
4. False – the premise says the opposite.  
5. False – any two raths snicker-snack each other.  
6. Undetermined – nothing links P4 (which is only a disjunction) to Zindle specifically.

Statement 3.  A rath snicker-snacked every rath.

3. No toves chortled at Bungo.

4. Every borogove chortled at Zindle.

   (Bungo galumped over Zindle → every rath chortled at Bungo).

No premise says anything about whether Bungo or Zindle whiffle; nor does any premise forbid them from doing so.

Example A  
– Bungo (a borogove) whiffles.  
– Zindle (a rath) whiffles.

Example B  
– Bungo (a borogove) does not whiffle.  
– Zindle (a rath) does not whiffle.

Since both examples respect all the stated premises yet disagree about every claim in statements 1–4 and 6, none of those statements is forced to be true.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. A tove snicker-snacked a rath that snicker-snacked a borogove. Not all borogoves will whiffle but it is not the case that Rafin gyred. Only toves are mimsy but it is not the case that Rafin isn't beamish.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. A borogove is mimsy unless not all toves will whiffle.
2. Only toves are mimsy and no borogoves will whiffle.
3. A borogove is mimsy or not all toves will whiffle.
4. Not all borogoves will whiffle and only toves are mimsy.
5. Rafin will whiffle.
6. Rafin won't whiffle.
results.model_response:
The information we are told is (formalised):

P1. Every creature is either a tove, a borogove or a rath, and the three kinds are mutually exclusive.  
P2. ∃ t, r, b ( Tove(t) ∧ Rath(r) ∧ Borogove(b) ∧ Snicker-snack(t,r) ∧ Snicker-snack(r,b) ).  
P3. ¬ (∀ x (Borogove(x) → Whiffle(x)))  (“Not all borogoves will whiffle”).  
P4. ¬ Gyred(Rafin).  
P5. Only toves are mimsy → ∀ x (Mimsy(x) → Tove(x)).  
P6. ¬¬ Beamish(Rafin) → Beamish(Rafin).

We now test each proposed conclusion to see whether it follows from P1–P6.

1. “A borogove is mimsy unless not all toves will whiffle.”  
3. “A borogove is mimsy or not all toves will whiffle.”  
(1) and (3) are logically equivalent (“A unless B” ≡ “A ∨ B”).  
• Because of P5, no borogove can be mimsy, so the left–hand disjunct is always false.  
• Whether all toves whiffle or not is completely unconstrained by the premises.  
  (We can build a model in which every tove whiffles.)  
Hence the whole disjunction can be false, so it is not forced by the premises.

2. “Only toves are mimsy and no borogoves will whiffle.”  
The first half is P5 (true), but the second half is stronger than P3.  
P3 says at least one borogove fails to whiffle; it does ​not​ forbid other
borogoves from whiffling.  So the second half need not be true.

4. “Not all borogoves will whiffle and only toves are mimsy.”  
This is exactly the conjunction of P3 and P5, both of which are given.  
Therefore statement 4 is true in every model of the premises.

5. “Rafin will whiffle.”  
6. “Rafin won’t whiffle.”  
The premises say nothing about Rafin’s whiffling; either choice can be made in a
model, so neither statement is forced.

Since only statement 4 is entailed by the premises, it is the sole answer.

Final answer: 4
