results.model_response:
Let  

T(x)  =  “x is a tove”  
B(x)  =  “x is a borogove”  
R(x)  =  “x is a rath” (the three kinds are mutually exclusive and exhaustive)  

S(x,y) = “x snicker-snacked y”  
C(x,y) = “x chortled at y”  
W(x)   = “x will whiffle”  
Beamish(x) = “x is beamish”

The information given can be formalised (with Z = Zindle, Bn = Bungo) as

1. ∃r ( R(r) ∧ S(r,Bn) )                                    (a rath snicker-snacked Bungo)  
2. ∃r ( R(r) ∧ ∀b( B(b) → C(b,r) ) )                       (some rath was chortled at by every borogove)  
3. ¬S(Bn,Z)  ∧  ∀r( R(r) → C(r,Z) )                        (Bungo didn’t snicker-snack Z; every rath chortled at Z)  
4. ( ¬∃r( R(r) ∧ Beamish(r) ) ) → ¬B(Z)                    (if no rath is beamish then Z isn’t a borogove)  
5. ( ∃b( B(b) ∧ S(b,Rafin) ) ) → ( ¬∀t( T(t) → W(t) ) → ¬W(Rafin) )   (the whiffling clause)

The six candidate conclusions are

1. ∃r ( R(r) ∧ C(Z,r) )                       Zindle chortled at a rath  
2. W(Z)                                       Zindle will whiffle  
3. ∀r ( R(r) → ¬C(r,Z) )                      no rath chortled at Zindle  
4. ¬∀r ( R(r) → C(r,Z) )                      not all raths chortled at Zindle  
5. ∃r ( R(r) ∧ C(r,Z) )                       a rath chortled at Zindle  
6. ∀r ( R(r) → C(Z,r) )                       Zindle chortled at every rath  

What is forced by the premises?

Premise (3) already contains

∀r ( R(r) → C(r,Z) ).                         (every rath chortled at Zindle)

Because we are told that at least one rath exists, we immediately obtain

∃r ( R(r) ∧ C(r,Z) ).                         (some rath chortled at Zindle)

That is exactly statement 5, so 5 must be true.

Do any of the remaining statements have to be true?  
No.  The following model satisfies all premises while making 1, 2, 3, 4 and 6 false.

Individuals:  
r₁, r₂  – raths   b₁ – borogove   t₁ – tove   Z – Zindle (a tove)   Bn – Bungo (the borogove b₁)

Interpret the predicates so that
• S(r₁,Bn) is true (premise 1); no other “snicker-snacked” facts hold.  
• C(b₁,r₁) is true (premise 2); no other borogove-to-rath chortling facts hold.  
• C(r₁,Z) and C(r₂,Z) are true (premise 3); no other rath-to-Zindle chortling facts hold.  
• Beamish(x) is false for every rath (so the antecedent of premise 4 is true and Z is not a borogove).  
• No borogove snicker-snacks Rafin, so premise 5 is vacuously true.  
• W(Z) is false; Z never chortles at anyone.

In this model  
1 is false (Z never chortles),  
2 is false (Z doesn’t whiffle),  
3 is false (some raths chortle at Z),  
4 is false (all raths chortle at Z),  
5 is true (both raths chortle at Z),  
6 is false (Z doesn’t chortle at the raths).

Since it is possible for every premise to hold while 1, 2, 3, 4 and 6 fail, none of those statements is logically forced.  
Statement 5, however, is forced by the premises in every model.

Final answer: Only statement 5 must be true.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Every rath snicker-snacked Rafin. There is a rath that every borogove snicker-snacked. If a borogove snicker-snacked Bungo, then Rafin is a tove only if no borogoves are beamish. A rath is beamish but it is not the case that Bungo is a rath.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. If every rath snicker-snacked Rafin, then not all raths snicker-snacked Rafin.
2. Not all raths snicker-snacked Rafin.
3. A rath snicker-snacked Rafin.
4. Rafin snicker-snacked every rath.
5. Rafin snicker-snacked a rath.
6. No raths snicker-snacked Rafin.
results.model_response:
Let  

S(x,y)  =  “x snicker-snacked y”  
R(x)     =  “x is a rath”  
T(x)     =  “x is a tove”  
B(x)     =  “x is a borogove”  
Bm(x)    =  “x is beamish”  

The premises are

P1.  ∀x( R(x) → S(x,Rafin) )                                         (every rath snicker-snacked Rafin)  
P2.  ∃r( R(r) ∧ ∀y( B(y) → S(y,r) ) )                                 (some rath was snicker-snacked by every borogove)  
P3.  [ ∃b( B(b) ∧ S(b,Bungo) ) ] → [ T(Rafin) → ¬∃y( B(y) ∧ Bm(y) ) ]  
P4.  ∃x( R(x) ∧ Bm(x) )                                              (a rath is beamish)  
P5.  ¬R(Bungo)                                                       (Bungo isn’t a rath)  
P6.  Every object is exactly one of tove, borogove, rath, and there is at least one of each.

The six candidate conclusions are

1.  (∀x( R(x) → S(x,Rafin) )) → ¬∀x( R(x) → S(x,Rafin) )  
2.  ¬∀x( R(x) → S(x,Rafin) )  
3.  ∃x( R(x) ∧ S(x,Rafin) )  
4.  ∀x( R(x) → S(Rafin,x) )  
5.  ∃x( R(x) ∧ S(Rafin,x) )  
6.  ¬∃x( R(x) ∧ S(x,Rafin) )

Analysis of each statement.

•  Statement 1: its antecedent is exactly premise P1, known to be true; its consequent is the negation of P1, hence false. Therefore the whole conditional is false in this situation and cannot be a necessary truth.

•  Statement 2: contradicts P1 directly, so it is false.

•  Statement 3: Because there is at least one rath (P6) and every rath snicker-snacked Rafin (P1), at least one rath snicker-snacked Rafin. Hence Statement 3 is necessarily true.

•  Statement 4: We are told only about raths acting on Rafin, not Rafin acting on raths. Nothing forces this statement to be true, so it is not entailed.

•  Statement 5: Likewise, nothing guarantees that Rafin snicker-snacked any rath, so it is not entailed.

•  Statement 6: The negation of Statement 3; since Statement 3 is necessarily true, Statement 6 is necessarily false.

Therefore the only statement that must be true given the premises is

3. A rath snicker-snacked Rafin.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. A rath snicker-snacked a borogove that snicker-snacked a rath. No toves galumphed over Bungo but it is not the case that Rafin didn't galumph over Zindle. Not all raths galumphed over Rafin and a rath snicker-snacked Zindle.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. A rath galumphed over Bungo unless every tove galumphed over Rafin.
2. No toves galumphed over Bungo and every rath galumphed over Rafin.
3. Rafin is a tove unless a rath galumphed over Bungo.
4. A rath galumphed over Bungo or every tove galumphed over Rafin.
5. Rafin is a tove or a rath galumphed over Bungo.
6. No toves galumphed over Bungo and not all raths galumphed over Rafin.
results.model_response:
Let  

T(x) : x is a tove  
B(x) : x is a borogove  
R(x) : x is a rath  

S(x,y) : x snicker-snacked y  
G(x,y) : x galumphed over y  

and let the individual names Bungo, Rafin and Zindle denote members of the universe, in which every individual is **exclusively** a tove, a borogove or a rath and there is at least one of each.

Facts that are explicitly given:

F1  ∀x [ T(x) → ¬G(x , Bungo) ]                (no tove galumphed over Bungo)  
F2  G(Rafin , Zindle)                          (Rafin galumphed over Zindle)  
F3  ¬∀x [ R(x) → G(x , Rafin) ]               (not all raths galumphed over Rafin)  
      ⇔ ∃x [ R(x) ∧ ¬G(x , Rafin) ]  
F4  ∃x [ R(x) ∧ S(x , Zindle) ]                (a rath snicker-snacked Zindle)  
F5  ∃r₁,b₁,r₂ [ R(r₁) ∧ B(b₁) ∧ R(r₂) ∧ S(r₁ , b₁) ∧ S(b₁ , r₂) ]  
      (a rath snicker-snacked a borogove that snicker-snacked a rath)

The candidate statements are analysed below.

1.  “A rath galumphed over Bungo unless every tove galumphed over Rafin.”  
    ‘Unless’ means “if not … then …”, i.e.  
    ¬∀x [T(x) → G(x , Rafin)]  →  ∃x [ R(x) ∧ G(x , Bungo) ]  
    There is no information that forces either side of this conditional, so it is not guaranteed to be true (a model can be built in which its antecedent is true and its consequent false).  
    Not a necessary truth.

2.  “No toves galumphed over Bungo and every rath galumphed over Rafin.”  
    The first conjunct is F1 (true) but the second contradicts F3 (false).  
    Hence the whole conjunction is false in every admissible model.  
    Not a necessary truth.

3.  “Rafin is a tove unless a rath galumphed over Bungo.”  
    Equivalent to  (∃x [R(x) ∧ G(x , Bungo)]) ∨ T(Rafin).  
    Both disjuncts can be false (take a model with no rath galumphing Bungo and Rafin a borogove).  
    Not a necessary truth.

4.  “A rath galumphed over Bungo or every tove galumphed over Rafin.”  
    This is the disjunction that is logically equivalent to statement 1, and can be false for the same reason.  
    Not a necessary truth.

5.  “Rafin is a tove or a rath galumphed over Bungo.”  
    Again, both disjuncts can simultaneously be false, so the statement is not forced by the data.  
    Not a necessary truth.

6.  “No toves galumphed over Bungo and not all raths galumphed over Rafin.”  
    The first part is exactly F1; the second is exactly F3.  Both are given facts, therefore their conjunction is true in every model satisfying the premises.  
    This statement **must** be true.

Because only statement 6 is entailed by the given information, it is the sole statement that must be true.

Final answer: 6.

results.model_response:
The information can be formalised as follows.

1. Everything is exactly one of: tove, borogove or rath (and there is at least one of each).
2. Mimsy ⇒ borogove                        (only borogoves are mimsy).
3. Tove(x) ⇒ x chortled-at Bungo.          (every tove chortled at Bungo).
4. (¬ Only-toves-gyred) ⇒ (Zindle ¬borogove).   (Zindle isn’t a borogove unless only toves gyred).
5. [Every tove chortled at Bungo] ⇒ ¬(Rafin chortled-at Rafin).
6. Bungo chortled-at Zindle.
7. Tove(x) ⇒ x will-whiffle.               (every tove will whiffle).

First, statement 3 is an outright fact (premise 3), so the antecedent of premise 5 is satisfied.
Hence, by premise 5, Rafin did not chortle at Rafin.

That is the only conclusion that follows unavoidably from the premises:

• Whether Zindle whiffles depends on whether Zindle is a tove; that is not forced by the data, so statements 1, 2, 3 and 4 are not guaranteed true.

• Premise 5 gives ­¬(Rafin chortled at Rafin), so statement 6 (its negation) is certainly false, but statement 5 is certainly true.

Therefore the sole statement that must be true is

5. Rafin didn't chortle at Rafin.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Every borogove snicker-snacked a rath. Bungo didn't snicker-snacked Zindle and a borogove will whiffle. Zindle isn't beamish and Rafin isn't beamish.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. A borogove snicker-snacked a rath.
2. There is a borogove that every rath snicker-snacked.
3. No borogoves snicker-snacked a rath.
4. No borogoves snicker-snacked every rath.
5. A borogove snicker-snacked every rath.
6. Every borogove snicker-snacked every rath.
results.model_response:
Let  

• borogove(x), tove(x), rath(x) be the three exclusive kinds (each non-empty);  
• sn(x,y) mean “x snicker-snacked y”;  
• whiffle(x) mean “x will whiffle”;  
• beamish(x) mean “x is beamish”.

Premises

1. ∀x [borogove(x) → ∃y (rath(y) ∧ sn(x,y))]            (Every borogove snicker-snacked a rath)  
2. ¬sn(Bungo , Zindle) and ∃x [borogove(x) ∧ whiffle(x)]  
3. ¬beamish(Zindle)  
4. ¬beamish(Rafin)

What the premises force

• Because each kind is non-empty, there is at least one borogove b and at least one rath r.  
• From premise 1, for that borogove b there is some rath r with sn(b , r).

Hence the existential statement

(1)  ∃x∃y (borogove(x) ∧ rath(y) ∧ sn(x,y))

must hold.

Nothing else is compelled:

• It is easy to build a model in which no borogove is snicker-snacked by every rath, so statement 2 need not hold.  
• Premise 1 already contradicts statement 3 (“No borogoves snicker-snacked a rath”), so 3 cannot be forced true.  
• One can have a model with only one rath and let a borogove snicker-snack that single rath, making statement 5 true and statement 4 false; or a model with two raths, where no borogove snicker-snacks both, making 4 true and 5 false. So neither 4 nor 5 is necessary.  
• Likewise, statement 6 (“Every borogove snicker-snacked every rath”) can be made true or false at will without violating the premises.

Therefore the only statement that must be true in every situation satisfying the premises is statement 1.

Final answer: Only statement 1 must be true.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. No borogoves chortled at a rath. Bungo chortled at every rath. Bungo snicker-snacked a rath.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. If Bungo isn't a borogove, then Bungo is a borogove.
2. Bungo isn't a borogove.
3. Bungo is a borogove.
4. Bungo is a rath.
5. Bungo isn't a borogove only if Bungo is a borogove.
results.model_response:
Reasoning

1. The three kinds of beings – toves, borogoves and raths – are mutually exclusive and each kind exists.

2. Premise: No borogove ever chortles at a rath.
   Logical form:  (x is a borogove) → (for every y, if y is a rath then x did NOT chortle at y).

3. Premise: Bungo chortled at every rath.
   Since there is at least one rath, Bungo actually did chortle at (at least) one rath.

4. If Bungo were a borogove, point 2 would forbid him from chortling at any rath, contradicting point 3.  
   Therefore Bungo cannot be a borogove.  So ¬(Bungo is a borogove) is forced.

5. Nothing in the premises distinguishes between toves and raths with respect to chortling, so Bungo could be either a tove or a rath.  Hence we cannot deduce that he is (or is not) a rath, and we certainly cannot deduce that he is a borogove.

Evaluation of the five statements

1. If Bungo isn’t a borogove, then Bungo is a borogove.  
   This would be false (we have just shown Bungo isn’t a borogove).  Not forced.

2. Bungo isn’t a borogove.  
   Must be true (see step 4).

3. Bungo is a borogove.  
   False, not forced.

4. Bungo is a rath.  
   Possible but not necessary; not forced.

5. Bungo isn’t a borogove only if Bungo is a borogove.  
   Same conditional as statement 1, therefore false; not forced.

Final answer

The only statement that must be true is:

2. Bungo isn’t a borogove.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Rafin won't whiffle. Zindle and Rafin will burble. Every rath galumphed over Rafin. Rafin snicker-snacked every borogove. Rafin is a tove or Rafin will whiffle.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Zindle won't whiffle.
2. Zindle will whiffle.
3. Rafin is a tove.
4. Rafin will whiffle.
5. Rafin isn't a tove.
6. Zindle and Rafin will whiffle.
results.model_response:
Given information (formalised)  

1. Every creature is exactly one of: tove (T), borogove (B) or rath (R) and each of those kinds exists.  
2. Rafin won’t whiffle:  ¬W(Rafin).  
3. Zindle will burble and Rafin will burble:  Burble(Zindle) ∧ Burble(Rafin).  
4. Every rath galumphed over Rafin:  ∀x (R(x) → Galumphed-over(x , Rafin)).  
5. Rafin snicker-snacked every borogove:  ∀x (B(x) → Snicker-snack(Rafin , x)).  
6. Either Rafin is a tove or Rafin will whiffle:  T(Rafin) ∨ W(Rafin).

Logical consequences

• From 2 we have ¬W(Rafin).  
• Substitute that into 6 (T(Rafin) ∨ W(Rafin)).  
  Because the second disjunct W(Rafin) is false, the first disjunct must be true.  
  Therefore T(Rafin) is forced.

• No premise links “whiffle” to any other property for Zindle, so Zindle might whiffle or might not—both are consistent with all the premises.

• Likewise, since we have proved T(Rafin), the statements “Rafin isn’t a tove”, “Rafin will whiffle”, and “Zindle and Rafin will whiffle” are all false or at least not forced.

Evaluation of the six candidate conclusions

1. Zindle won’t whiffle  —not forced (could be either).  
2. Zindle will whiffle   —not forced (could be either).  
3. Rafin is a tove     must be true (proved above).  
4. Rafin will whiffle    impossible (contradicts premise 2).  
5. Rafin isn’t a tove    false (contradicts the deduction).  
6. Zindle and Rafin will whiffle not forced and in fact impossible for Rafin.

Final answer: Statement 3 alone must be true.

5. A tove galumphed over every rath.
6. Every tove galumphed over every rath.
results.model_response:
Let  

T(x) … “x is a tove”  
R(x) … “x is a rath”  
G(x,y) … “x galumphed over y”  

Facts that are stated unconditionally:

F1  ∀x ( R(x) → ∀y ( T(y) → G(x , y) ) )      (every rath over every tove)  
F2  ∀x ( R(x) → G(x , Rafin) )                 (every rath over Rafin)  
F3  ∃x ( R(x) ∧ G(x , Zindle) )                (a rath over Zindle)

(The long conditional about Bungo mentions neither raths-vs-toves nor the relation G between them, so it cannot force any of the six candidate statements to be true or false.  It can therefore be ignored for our present question.)

    There is at least one rath, and by F1 every rath galumphs every tove.  
    Therefore some rath galumphs every tove.  Statement 1 is compelled.

2.  ¬∃y ( T(y) ∧ ∀x ( R(x) → G(y , x) ) )  
3.  ∀y ( T(y) → ∃x ( R(x) ∧ G(y , x) ) )  
4.  ∃y∃x ( T(y) ∧ R(x) ∧ G(y , x) )  
5.  ∃y ( T(y) ∧ ∀x ( R(x) → G(y , x) ) )  
6.  ∀y∀x ( T(y) ∧ R(x) → G(y , x) )  

Nothing given places any restriction on toves galumphing over raths.  
Hence we can build two consistent models:

• Model A: no tove ever galumphs any rath.  
  (2 true, 3-6 false.)

• Model B: pick one tove t and one rath r and let G(t , r) hold; let t galumph every rath if desired.  
  (2 false, 4 true, others as chosen.)

Because each of statements 2–6 is true in some models and false in others, none of them is forced by the information given.

Therefore the only statement that must be true is:

results.model_response:

2. Zindle is a tove.

4. A tove is uffish   (read in ordinary English as “there exists at least one tove that is uffish’’).

   (Since Zindle is a tove, the first disjunct is true, so this adds no new information.)

Statement 3.  “A tove galumphed over Zindle.”

Statement 4.  “Zindle galumphed over every tove.”

3. A tove galumphed over Zindle.

1. Zindle will whiffle.
2. Zindle won't whiffle.
3. Rafin didn't chortle at Rafin.
4. Rafin will whiffle.
5. Rafin chortled at Rafin.
6. Zindle and Rafin will whiffle.
results.model_response:
Only statement 3 must be true.

Reasoning  
• Rafin is a tove.  
• A tove (call it x) may not chortle at any tove that chortles back at x.  If a tove chortled at itself, then x both chortles at and is chortled at by the same tove (itself), violating the rule.  Hence no tove can chortle at itself.  
• Therefore Rafin, being a tove, did not (and cannot) chortle at Rafin.  
→ Statement 3 is forced to be true.

Nothing in the premises fixes whether Rafin or Zindle whiffle, nor does it force Zindle’s category.  So statements 1, 2, 4, 5, and 6 can all be either true or false in some compliant model and are not logically necessary.

Thus the sole statement that must hold in every situation satisfying the given conditions is:  

3. Rafin didn't chortle at Rafin.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Every rath snicker-snacked every tove. A tove snicker-snacked a borogove that snicker-snacked it. A rath galumphed over Bungo only if no borogoves are mimsy. If no borogoves snicker-snacked Bungo, then Rafin snicker-snacked Rafin.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. A tove snicker-snacked a rath.
2. Every tove snicker-snacked every rath.
3. A tove snicker-snacked every rath.
4. There is a tove that every rath snicker-snacked.
5. No toves snicker-snacked every rath.
6. Every tove snicker-snacked a rath.
results.model_response:
Let  

T(x)  = “x is a tove”  
B(x)  = “x is a borogove”  
R(x)  = “x is a rath”  
S(x,y)= “x snicker-snacked y”  

Premises  

P1.  ∀x( R(x) → ∀y( T(y) → S(x,y) ) )           (every rath snicker-snacks every tove)  
P2.  ∃t∃b( T(t) ∧ B(b) ∧ S(t,b) ∧ S(b,t) )        (a tove and a borogove snicker-snack each other)  
P3.  (other conditions about galumphing, Bungo, Rafin … – they mention neither toves nor raths in the
     snicker-snack relation and are irrelevant to the present questions)  
P4.  There is at least one tove, at least one borogove and at least one rath; the three kinds are mutually
     exclusive and exhaustive.

From P1 we immediately get  

∀y( T(y) → ∀x( R(x) → S(x,y) ) ).            (every rath snicker-snacks that tove)  

Choose any tove y (there is at least one by P4).  
For that y we have ∀x( R(x) → S(x,y) ).  
Thus

∃y( T(y) ∧ ∀x( R(x) → S(x,y) ) ).           (some tove is snicker-snacked by every rath)

That is exactly statement 4.

-------------------------------------------------
To see that none of the other statements is forced, build a model that satisfies all premises but falsifies each of them.

Universe: {t1 (a tove), b1 (a borogove), r1 (a rath), Bungo, Rafin}.  
Snicker-snack relation:

• S(r1 , t1)       (required by P1)  
• S(t1 , b1) and S(b1 , t1)  (required by P2)  
• S(Rafin , Rafin) (to meet P3 if desired)  
• No other snicker-snacking pairs.

This model fulfils P1–P4, yet:

1. There is no pair (tove,rath) with the tove snick-snacking the rath.  
2. Hence not every tove snick-snacks every rath.  
3. Nor does any tove snick-snack every rath.  
5. Indeed, in this model no tove snick-snacks every rath—but a different model (add S(t1,r1)) would make
   statement 5 false, so 5 is not necessary.  
6. The (only) tove snick-snacks no rath here, so 6 is false.

Since we can satisfy all premises while making each of 1, 2, 3, 5 and 6 false, none of them is logically required.

-------------------------------------------------
Therefore the only statement that must be true in every situation satisfying the premises is

Statement 4:  “There is a tove that every rath snicker-snacked.”

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Rafin is a tove unless Bungo isn't a borogove. A rath is beamish but it is not the case that Rafin is a tove. Zindle isn't beamish but it is not the case that Rafin isn't beamish.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. If Bungo isn't a borogove, then Bungo is a borogove.
2. Bungo isn't a borogove.
3. Zindle is beamish.
4. Zindle and Rafin are beamish.
5. Bungo isn't a borogove only if Bungo is a borogove.
6. Bungo is a borogove.
results.model_response:
Step 1 – Translate the information into symbolic form  

T = “…is a tove”,  
B = “…is a borogove”,  
R = “…is a rath”,  
M = “…is beamish”.  

Every creature is exactly one of T, B, or R and there is at least one of each.

Named individuals: Rafin (Ra), Bungo (Bu), Zindle (Zi).

1. “Rafin is a tove unless Bungo isn’t a borogove.”  
   “A unless B” = “if not B then A”.  
   ¬(Bu ¬B) → (Ra T)           (1)  
   i.e. If Bungo is a borogove then Rafin is a tove.  

2. “A rath is beamish but it is not the case that Rafin is a tove.”  
   ∀x( x R → x M )            (2a)  
   ¬(Ra T)                    (2b)

3. “Zindle isn’t beamish but it is not the case that Rafin isn’t beamish.”  
   ¬(Zi M)                    (3a)  
   ¬¬(Ra M)  ⇒  Ra M          (3b)

Step 2 – Deduce further facts  

From (1):   (Bu B) → (Ra T).  
Contrapositive: ¬(Ra T) → ¬(Bu B).  
Using (2b) (¬Ra T) we obtain             (4)  ¬(Bu B).  
Therefore Bungo is NOT a borogove.

So we now have:  
• Bungo is not a borogove.  
• Rafin is beamish (3b).  
• Zindle is not beamish (3a).

Nothing forces any other classification of the three individuals, and no other determinate facts follow.

Step 3 – Test each candidate statement

1. If Bungo isn’t a borogove, then Bungo is a borogove.  
   Antecedent is true (from (4)); consequent is false, so the whole conditional is false.  
   Not necessarily true.

2. Bungo isn’t a borogove.  
   This is exactly what (4) gives us.  
   MUST be true.

3. Zindle is beamish.  
   Premise (3a) says Zindle is NOT beamish.  
   Not true.

4. Zindle and Rafin are beamish.  
   Zindle isn’t beamish → conjunction false.  
   Not true.

5. Bungo isn’t a borogove only if Bungo is a borogove.  
   “P only if Q” = P → Q.  
   Same form as statement 1, so likewise false.

6. Bungo is a borogove.  
   Directly contradicted by (4).  
   Not true.

Final answer  

The only statement that must be true is:

2. Bungo isn’t a borogove.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Rafin chortled at Rafin. Every tove snicker-snacked every tove that snicker-snacked it. Only borogoves are beamish and every borogove is uffish. Not all toves snicker-snacked Zindle just in case no toves snicker-snacked Zindle. If a borogove snicker-snacked Rafin, then Bungo didn't snicker-snacked Bungo only if every rath chortled at Bungo.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. A tove snicker-snacked a tove.
2. A tove snicker-snacked every tove.
3. No toves snicker-snacked every tove.
4. Every tove snicker-snacked a tove.
5. A tove snicker-snacked every tove that snicker-snacked it.
6. Every tove snicker-snacked every tove.
results.model_response:
The information we are given includes the universal rule

Every tove snicker-snacked every tove that snicker-snacked it  

together with the fact that there is at least one tove.

Because that rule is stated universally (“every tove …”), it is certainly satisfied by every individual tove that exists.  
Since at least one tove does exist, there is (in fact there are many) an individual tove that snicker-snacks every tove that snicker-snacks it.

Hence statement 5

A tove snicker-snacked every tove that snicker-snacked it

is guaranteed to be true.

For the other five statements one can construct models of the situation in which they are false (for example, a model in which no creature ever snicker-snacks any other creature satisfies all the given conditions but makes statements 1, 2, 4 and 6 false, while a slightly different model can make statement 3 false). Therefore none of those statements is forced.

Thus, the only statement that must be true is:

5. A tove snicker-snacked every tove that snicker-snacked it.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Bungo didn't chortle at Bungo and no toves chortled at Bungo. Rafin isn't a tove and no toves chortled at Bungo. Not all toves will burble or every borogove chortled at Bungo.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Not all toves chortled at Bungo but it is not the case that Rafin isn't a tove.
2. Not all toves chortled at Bungo but it is not the case that Rafin didn't chortle at Bungo.
3. Not all toves chortled at Bungo and Rafin didn't chortle at Bungo.
4. Not all toves chortled at Bungo and no toves chortled at Rafin.
5. Bungo didn't chortle at Bungo and Rafin isn't a tove.
6. Every borogove is mimsy just in case not all borogoves are mimsy.
results.model_response:
Let  

T(x)  = “x is a tove”  
B(x)  = “x is a borogove”  
R(x)  = “x is a rath”  
Ch(x , y) = “x chortled at y”  
Bu(x) = “x burbles”  
M(x)  = “x is mimsy”  

and let the constants b and r stand for Bungo and Rafin.

Facts supplied in the statement translate to the following sentences.

F1  ∀x [T(x) ∨ B(x) ∨ R(x)]  ∧  exclusiveness  (and there is at least one of each sort).  
F2  ¬Ch(b , b).  
F3  ∀x (T(x) → ¬Ch(x , b)).  
F4  ¬T(r).  
F5  (¬∀x (T(x) → Bu(x))) ∨ (∀x (B(x) → Ch(x , b))).        (the final disjunction).

Now test each candidate statement S₁–S₆ against these facts.

S1  (¬∀x (T(x) → Ch(x , b))) ∧ T(r)  
 • The first conjunct is true by F3 together with the existence of at least one tove.  
 • The second conjunct contradicts F4.  
 So S1 is impossible ⇒ not forced.

S2  (¬∀x (T(x) → Ch(x , b))) ∧ Ch(r , b)  
 • First conjunct again true.  
 • Ch(r , b) is completely unconstrained by the facts (Rafin might chortle or not),   
   so S2 can be false in a legitimate model ⇒ not forced.

S3  (¬∀x (T(x) → Ch(x , b))) ∧ ¬Ch(r , b)  
 • First conjunct true.  
 • But nothing obliges ¬Ch(r , b); we can make Rafin chortle at Bungo in some model,  
   making S3 false ⇒ not forced.

S4  (¬∀x (T(x) → Ch(x , b))) ∧ ∀x (T(x) → ¬Ch(x , r))  
 • Second conjunct is not implied by the facts (a tove could chortle at Rafin),  
   so S4 is not forced.

S5  ¬Ch(b , b) ∧ ¬T(r)  
 • These are exactly the facts F2 and F4.  
 • Therefore S5 is true in every model that satisfies the given information.

S6  (∀x (B(x) → M(x))) ↔ ¬∀x (B(x) → M(x))  
 • This is the biconditional P ↔ ¬P, which is always false in classical logic  
   (since at least one borogove exists, P and ¬P cannot both hold).  
 • Hence S6 is false in every model ⇒ not forced.

Conclusion

The only statement that must be true in every situation described is statement 5.

Final answer: 5.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Zindle didn't snicker-snacked Zindle. No toves chortled at Bungo and Rafin didn't snicker-snacked Bungo. If Zindle gimbled, then no borogoves are beamish only if every borogove chortled at Bungo.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. A borogove will burble and no borogoves will burble.
2. Not all toves chortled at Bungo but it is not the case that Zindle didn't snicker-snacked Zindle.
3. Bungo didn't snicker-snacked Zindle and no toves chortled at Bungo.
4. Zindle didn't snicker-snacked Zindle and no toves chortled at Bungo.
5. Not all toves chortled at Bungo but it is not the case that Bungo didn't snicker-snacked Zindle.
6. Not all toves chortled at Bungo and Bungo didn't snicker-snacked Zindle.
results.model_response:
We translate the information into first-order notation (any equivalent formalisation suffices).

Predicates  
T(x): x is a tove  
B(x): x is a borogove  
R(x): x is a rath                        (Every object is exactly one of these three and each set is non-empty.)  
S(x,y): x snicker-snacked y  
C(x,y): x chortled at y  
G(x): x gimbled                            
M(x): x is beamish  

Premises  

1. ¬S(Zindle , Zindle)                                           (Zindle didn’t snicker-snack Zindle.)  
2. ∀x (T(x) → ¬C(x , Bungo))                                     (No tove chortled at Bungo.)  
3. ¬S(Rafin , Bungo)                                             (Rafin didn’t snicker-snack Bungo.)  
4. G(Zindle) → [ (¬∃x (B(x) ∧ M(x))) → ∀x (B(x) → C(x , Bungo)) ]  
   (If Zindle gimbled, then “no borogoves are beamish” only if “every borogove chortled at Bungo”.)

Candidate statements  

1. ∃x (B(x) ∧ burble(x)) ∧ ¬∃x (B(x) ∧ burble(x))  
   – A contradiction (A ∧ ¬A); cannot be true in any consistent model, therefore not forced.

2. ¬∀x (T(x) → C(x , Bungo)) ∧ ¬¬S(Zindle , Zindle)  
   – First conjunct is true because premise 2 gives the stronger ∀x (T(x) → ¬C(x , Bungo)).  
     Second conjunct simplifies to S(Zindle , Zindle), contradicting premise 1.  
     Whole conjunction is false, hence not forced.

3. ¬S(Bungo , Zindle) ∧ ∀x (T(x) → ¬C(x , Bungo))  
   – Second conjunct is premise 2 and is true.  
     First conjunct is not entailed (we have no information about Bungo snicker-snacking Zindle).  
     Conjunction therefore not guaranteed.

4. ¬S(Zindle , Zindle) ∧ ∀x (T(x) → ¬C(x , Bungo))  
   – Both conjuncts are exactly premises 1 and 2.  
     Hence this statement is unconditionally true.

5. ¬∀x (T(x) → C(x , Bungo)) ∧ ¬¬S(Bungo , Zindle) (= … ∧ S(Bungo , Zindle))  
   – First conjunct is true (as in 2).  
     Second conjunct is not entailed.  
     Conjunction not guaranteed.

6. ¬∀x (T(x) → C(x , Bungo)) ∧ ¬S(Bungo , Zindle)  
   – Again, first conjunct true; second not entailed.  
     Conjunction not guaranteed.

Because only statement 4 is a direct restatement of the given premises, it—and only it—must hold in every model that satisfies the premises.

Final answer: Statement 4 is the one that must be true; the other five need not be true.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. No borogoves galumphed over Rafin. A rath galumphed over a borogove that galumphed over a rath. Not all raths galumphed over Rafin but it is not the case that Rafin didn't galumph over Zindle.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. A borogove galumphed over Rafin.
2. Rafin galumphed over every borogove.
3. A borogove galumphed over Rafin or Rafin galumphed over Rafin.
4. Rafin galumphed over a borogove.
5. Every borogove galumphed over Rafin.
6. Not all borogoves galumphed over Rafin.
results.model_response:
Premises translated into predicate logic  

Let Bx, Rx, Tx be “x is a borogove / rath / tove” (the three kinds are exclusive and all non-empty).  
Let Gxy be “x galumphed over y”.  
Let the constant symbols Rafin and Zindle name the two individuals that appear in the story.

1.  ¬∃x (Bx ∧ Gx Rafin) ⇔ ∀x (Bx → ¬Gx Rafin)          (No borogove galumphed over Rafin)  
2.  ∃a∃b∃c (Ra ∧ Bb ∧ Rc ∧ Ga b ∧ Gb c)               (Some rath→borogove→rath chain)  
3.  ¬∀x (Rx → Gx Rafin)                               (Not all raths galumphed over Rafin)  
    ⇒ ∃x (Rx ∧ ¬Gx Rafin)                             (there is a rath that didn’t)  
4.  ¬¬G Rafin Zindle ⇒ G Rafin Zindle                 (Rafin did galumph over Zindle)

What the six candidate sentences say

1.  ∃x (Bx ∧ Gx Rafin)                                (a borogove galumphed over Rafin)  
2.  ∀x (Bx → G Rafin x)                               (Rafin galumphed over every borogove)  
3.  ∃x (Bx ∧ Gx Rafin) ∨ G Rafin Rafin               (1st disjunct = statement 1)  
4.  ∃x (Bx ∧ G Rafin x)                               (Rafin galumphed over a borogove)  
5.  ∀x (Bx → Gx Rafin)                                (every borogove galumphed over Rafin)  
6.  ¬∀x (Bx → Gx Rafin)                               (not all borogoves galumphed over Rafin)

Checking each against the premises

• Premise 1 directly says that there is no borogove that galumphed over Rafin.  
  Therefore sentence 1 is false in every model that satisfies the premises.  
  Sentences 3 and 4 both contain the same existential clause, so the first disjunct of 3
  and all of 4 are always false as well (though 3 might still be true if G Rafin Rafin
  happened to hold; it is not forced to).

• Premise 1 also makes the universal claim in sentence 5 impossible (indeed, false), and
  makes the universal claim in sentence 2 entirely unsupported (it can be true in some
  models, false in others).

• Because there is at least one borogove (given in the statement of the problem) and
  premise 1 tells us that none of them galumphed over Rafin, it follows that it is
  necessarily the case that not all borogoves galumphed over Rafin.  That is exactly what
  sentence 6 asserts.

Conclusion

The only statement that is forced (must be true) by the given information is

6. Not all borogoves galumphed over Rafin.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. If Rafin didn't chortle at Rafin, then a tove chortled at Rafin. Zindle isn't beamish but it is not the case that Rafin isn't beamish. Every rath gyred but it is not the case that Rafin isn't beamish.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. A rath is beamish but it is not the case that Zindle gyred.
2. Zindle and Rafin gyred.
3. A rath is beamish and Zindle gyred.
4. Zindle isn't beamish and every rath gyred.
5. Zindle didn't gyre.
6. Zindle gyred.
results.model_response:
Must-true statements are those that follow from the information that is explicitly given (or that is an uncontroversial logical consequence of it).

Premises we are told:

1. If Rafin didn’t chortle at Rafin, then a tove chortled at Rafin.
2. Zindle isn’t beamish.
3. It is not the case that Rafin isn’t beamish   →   Rafin is beamish.
4. Every rath gyred.
5. (Again) It is not the case that Rafin isn’t beamish   →   Rafin is beamish.

From this we can assert with certainty only

• Zindle isn’t beamish.  
• Every rath gyred.

Nothing in the premises forces Zindle to gyre or not to gyre, and nothing guarantees (or rules out) that some rath is beamish, or that Rafin (or anyone else) gyred, chortled, etc.

Now examine the six candidate conclusions:

1. A rath is beamish ∧ ¬(Zindle gyred) —not forced (there needn’t be a beamish rath, and Zindle might gyre).
2. Zindle gyred ∧ Rafin gyred —neither part is forced.
3. A rath is beamish ∧ Zindle gyred —again, neither conjunct is forced.
4. Zindle isn’t beamish ∧ every rath gyred —both conjuncts are exactly what the premises assert, so this is certain.
5. ¬(Zindle gyred) —not forced.
6. Zindle gyred —not forced.

Therefore the only statement that must be true is statement 4.

Final answer: 4 only.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. No toves chortled at Rafin. Rafin chortled at Zindle and a borogove will whiffle. Zindle didn't galumph over Bungo only if Rafin snicker-snacked Rafin.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Rafin is a tove unless Zindle didn't chortle at Rafin.
2. Rafin is a tove or Zindle didn't chortle at Rafin.
3. No toves chortled at Rafin and Rafin chortled at Zindle.
4. No toves chortled at Rafin but it is not the case that Rafin chortled at Zindle.
5. Rafin is a tove or it is not the case that Zindle didn't chortle at Rafin.
6. If Zindle didn't chortle at Zindle, then a tove chortled at Rafin.
results.model_response:
The information we are given can be formalised as follows.

1. ∀x ( Tove(x) → ¬Chortled(x , Rafin) )        (No tove chortled at Rafin)
2. Chortled(Rafin , Zindle)                     (Rafin chortled at Zindle)
3. (¬Galumph(Zindle , Bungo)) → Snicker-snack(Rafin , Rafin)
4. Partition axiom: every individual is exactly one of Tove, Borogove, Rath and each class is non-empty.

Only (1) and (2) involve the predicates that appear in the six candidate statements, so those two premises are all that matter for this question.

Test each candidate.

1. (Chortled(Zindle , Rafin) → Tove(Rafin))
   Counter-example: let Rafin be a Rath and let Zindle (a Borogove) chortle at Rafin.  
   All premises are satisfied, the implication is false.  Not necessary.

2. Tove(Rafin) ∨ ¬Chortled(Zindle , Rafin)
   The same counter-example (Rafin a Rath, Zindle chortles at Rafin) makes this false.  
   Not necessary.

3. ¬∃x ( Tove(x) ∧ Chortled(x , Rafin) )  ∧  Chortled(Rafin , Zindle)
   This is exactly the conjunction of premises (1) and (2).  
   Therefore it must be true.

4. ¬∃x ( Tove(x) ∧ Chortled(x , Rafin) )  ∧  ¬Chortled(Rafin , Zindle)
   Second conjunct contradicts premise (2).  Impossible, so not necessary.

5. Tove(Rafin) ∨ Chortled(Zindle , Rafin)
   Counter-example: Rafin is a Borogove (or Rath) and Zindle does not chortle at Rafin.  
   Premises satisfied, statement false.  Not necessary.

6. (¬Chortled(Zindle , Zindle)) → ∃x ( Tove(x) ∧ Chortled(x , Rafin) )
   Take a model where nobody chortles at herself at all.  Antecedent true, but the
   consequent is false by premise (1).  Statement false, so not necessary.

Hence the only statement that must hold in every situation satisfying the premises is statement 3.

Final answer: 3 only.

Every borogove snicker-snacked every tove that snicker-snacked every borogove. Bungo didn't chortle at Bungo and Rafin didn't galumph over Zindle. Rafin isn't a tove but it is not the case that Rafin didn't chortle at Bungo. If every rath snicker-snacked Bungo, then Bungo didn't snicker-snacked Bungo only if every rath galumphed over Rafin.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

2. Bungo didn't chortle at Bungo and Rafin isn't a tove.

a) Bungo didn’t chortle at Bungo.  
b) Rafin isn’t a tove.  

(The first is stated outright; the second is stated together with the fact that Rafin did chortle at Bungo.)

Nothing else we are told forces any further fact about which (or how many) toves chortle at Bungo, nor does it make Rafin a tove or stop Rafin from chortling at Bungo.  
Indeed, it is easy to build a model in which every single tove chortles at Bungo and all the other premisses are still satisfied.

That leaves only statement 2:

2. Bungo didn't chortle at Bungo and Rafin isn't a tove.

Both conjuncts are direct consequences of the premises, so statement 2 is true in every possible situation that satisfies the givens, whereas none of the other statements is forced to be true.

results.model_response:
Must-true statements are those that follow from the information given in every possible situation that satisfies the premises.

Premises restated logically

2. ∃x ( x is a tove and x galumphed over Rafin ).

4. ¬ chortle( Bungo , Bungo ).  
5. Everything is exactly one of: tove, borogove, rath, and each class is non-empty.

Now test each candidate statement.

1. “A tove chortled at Zindle.”  
   This contradicts premise 1, so it is impossible.  Not must-true.

2. “Every tove chortled at Zindle.”  
   Also contradicts premise 1.  Not must-true.

3. “Not all toves chortled at Zindle.”

4. “Zindle is a tove.”

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. There is not a tove that every tove snicker-snacked. Every tove snicker-snacked every tove that snicker-snacked every borogove. Not all raths will whiffle but it is not the case that Bungo didn't snicker-snacked Zindle.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. No toves snicker-snacked every borogove.
2. No toves snicker-snacked a borogove.
3. A tove snicker-snacked a borogove.
4. A tove snicker-snacked every borogove.
5. Every tove snicker-snacked every borogove.
6. Every tove snicker-snacked a borogove.
results.model_response:
Let

• T(x), B(x), R(x) mean “x is a tove / borogove / rath”  
• S(x , y) mean “x snicker-snacked y”.

The premisses are

(1) ¬∃t [ T(t) ∧ ∀y (T(y) → S(y , t)) ]  
  “There is no tove that every tove snicker-snacked.”

(2) ∀s [ T(s) → ∀t ( T(t) ∧ ∀b (B(b) → S(t , b))  →  S(s , t) ) ]  
  “Every tove snicker-snacked every tove that snicker-snacked every borogove.”

There are at least one tove, one borogove and one rath, but those facts play no direct role in what follows.  
(The additional information about whiffling and about Bungo and Zindle is irrelevant to the question.)

----------------------------------------------------------------
Key deduction
----------------------------------------------------------------
Suppose, for contradiction, that some tove t₀ snicker-snacked every borogove:

  T(t₀)  and  ∀b (B(b) → S(t₀ , b)).

Then t₀ satisfies the antecedent inside (2), so (2) gives

  ∀s (T(s) → S(s , t₀)).                       (3)

In particular, with s = t₀ itself (t₀ is a tove), S(t₀ , t₀) holds; hence

  ∀y (T(y) → S(y , t₀)).                       (4)

Thus t₀ is a tove that every tove snicker-snacked, contradicting (1).  
Therefore the supposition is impossible, and we have

  No tove snicker-snacked every borogove.

So statement 1 must be true.

----------------------------------------------------------------
Checking the other candidate conclusions
----------------------------------------------------------------
We now show that none of the other statements is forced by the premisses; i.e. each of them can be true in some model and false in another model that still satisfies (1) and (2).

Model A  
Objects: two toves t₁, t₂; one borogove b; one rath r.  
Snicker-snacked relation: empty (no one snicker-snacks anyone).  
Then (1) holds (there is certainly no universal target), and (2) is satisfied vacuously (because no tove snicker-snacks every borogove).  

In this model  
• Statement 2 (“No toves snicker-snacked a borogove”) is true,  
• Statements 3, 4, 5, 6 are all false.  

Model B  
Same objects as before.  
Snicker-snacked relation: t₁ S b (t₁ snicker-snacks the single borogove), nothing else.  

Again (1) and (2) hold, but now  
• Statement 2 is false and Statement 3 is true,  
• Statements 4, 5, 6 are false.

Because each of statements 2, 3, 4, 5 and 6 is true in one admissible model and false in another, none of them is forced by the premisses.

----------------------------------------------------------------
Final answer
----------------------------------------------------------------
Exactly statement 1 must be true; statements 2-6 need not be true.

1. No toves snicker-snacked every borogove.   (must be true)  
2.-6.   Not forced by the information given.

results.model_response:
Only statement 3 is forced by the information given; the other five statements may be either true or false in different models that still satisfy all the premises.

Reasoning (sketch)

1.  We are told that
    a.  Every borogove whiffles.  
    b.  Every borogove chortled at Zindle.  
    c.  Not all toves snicker-snacked Zindle (so ∃ tove that did not).  
    d.  Not all raths snicker-snacked Rafin (equivalent to the odd conditional that was supplied).  
    e.  There is at least one tove, one borogove and one rath.

2.  From (a) and the existence of a borogove, “A borogove chortled at Zindle” is always true (because every borogove chortled at Zindle, and there is at least one).

3.  From (c), “Not all toves snicker-snacked Zindle” is always true.

4.  Thus the two sides of statement 3
        A borogove chortled at Zindle   ↔   Not all toves snicker-snacked Zindle
    are each necessarily true, so the biconditional itself is necessarily true.  
    Hence statement 3 must hold in every model of the premises.

5.  The other statements are not forced:

    •  1, 5, 6 (about Zindle and/or Rafin whiffling) depend on what categories
       Zindle and Rafin belong to; the premises leave this open.

    •  2 equates “A borogove chortled at Zindle” with “No toves snicker-snacked Zindle”.
       The left-hand side is always true, but the right-hand side need not be;
       it may be that some (but not all) toves did snicker-snack Zindle.

    •  4 (“Zindle won’t whiffle”) can be made false simply by letting Zindle be,
       say, a borogove (all of which whiffle).

6.  Hence the only statement that must be true given the information is:

Statement 3.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Not all toves chortled at Bungo and a rath galumphed over Zindle. A rath galumphed over Bungo only if every tove chortled at Bungo. If Zindle gimbled, then no borogoves are beamish only if every rath galumphed over Rafin. If not all toves chortled at Bungo, then not all toves galumphed over Zindle only if Bungo galumphed over Zindle. If Bungo is beamish, then not all toves galumphed over Zindle only if every borogove chortled at Bungo.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Zindle gimbled.
2. Bungo galumphed over every rath.
3. No raths galumphed over Bungo.
4. Every rath galumphed over Bungo.
5. A rath galumphed over Bungo.
6. Bungo galumphed over a rath.
results.model_response:
First translate the five given facts into precise logical form.

Let  

T(x)  = “x is a tove”  
Bv(x) = “x is a borogove”  
R(x)  = “x is a rath”  

C(x)        = “x chortled at Bungo”  
G(x,y)      = “x galumphed over y”  
Be(x)       = “x is beamish”  
Gi(x)       = “x gimbled”

Facts supplied

F1  ¬∀x (T(x) → C(x))  and  ∃x (R(x) ∧ G(x,Zindle))

F2  ( ∃x (R(x) ∧ G(x,Bungo)) ) → ∀y ( T(y) → C(y) )

F3  Gi(Zindle) → [ (¬∃x (Bv(x) ∧ Be(x))) → ∀y ( R(y) → G(y,Rafin) ) ]

F4  ¬∀x (T(x) → C(x)) → [ (¬∀x (T(x) → G(x,Zindle))) → G(Bungo,Zindle) ]

F5  Be(Bungo) → [ (¬∀x (T(x) → G(x,Zindle))) → ∀y ( Bv(y) → C(y) ) ]

The crucial point is Fact 2 together with the first half of Fact 1.

1. From F1 we know ¬∀x (T(x) → C(x))   (i.e. not all toves chortled at Bungo).

2. Take the contrapositive of F2:

¬∀y (T(y) → C(y))  →  ¬∃x (R(x) ∧ G(x,Bungo))

Because the antecedent of this contrapositive is exactly what we have in step 1, we can conclude

¬∃x (R(x) ∧ G(x,Bungo))

Which is the statement

No rath galumphed over Bungo.

Nothing else in the premises forces any of the other listed statements to be true (models can be built in which each of them is false while all premises are satisfied).

Therefore the only statement that must be true is:

3. No raths galumphed over Bungo.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Zindle gimbled. Rafin chortled at Rafin if and only if no raths chortled at Rafin. Rafin chortled at Rafin just in case not all borogoves chortled at Bungo.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Bungo is a borogove only if every rath chortled at Rafin.
2. Bungo isn't a borogove just in case no raths chortled at Rafin.
3. No raths chortled at Rafin just in case not all borogoves chortled at Bungo.
4. If Bungo is a borogove, then every rath chortled at Rafin.
5. A borogove chortled at Rafin and Bungo is a rath.
6. No raths chortled at Rafin if and only if a borogove chortled at Bungo.
results.model_response:
Let  

P = “No raths chortled at Rafin”.  
Q = “Not all borogoves chortled at Bungo”.  
R = “Rafin chortled at Rafin”.

The two given biconditionals are

1.  R ↔ P  (“Rafin chortled at Rafin iff no raths chortled at Rafin”)  
2.  R ↔ Q  (“Rafin chortled at Rafin iff not all borogoves chortled at Bungo”)

From these we get an unavoidable consequence:

P ↔ Q  (“No raths chortled at Rafin iff not all borogoves chortled at Bungo”).

That is exactly statement 3, so statement 3 must be true in every situation satisfying the premises.

For the other items we can build models that satisfy all the premises while making the item false; hence none of them is forced.

Example counter-models (all meet the stated premises):

•  Statements 1 and 4 (they are logically the same conditional).  
  Let Bungo be a borogove.  
  Have two raths, only one of which chortles at Rafin.  
  Then “every rath chortled at Rafin” is false, so statements 1/4 are false.

•  Statement 2.  
  Keep the above model but let no rath chortle at Rafin.  
  Now “no raths chortled at Rafin” is true while “Bungo isn’t a borogove” is false, so the biconditional in 2 is false.

•  Statement 5.  
  Let no borogove chortle at Rafin and let Bungo be a borogove (not a rath).  
  Then statement 5 is false.

•  Statement 6.  
  In the first model (some rath chortles at Rafin, all borogoves chortle at Bungo) the left-hand side is false and the right-hand side is true, so the biconditional in 6 is false.

Since only statement 3 is inevitable, the answer is:

Statement 3 – and no others – must be true.

     (∀r₁)(Rath(r₁) → ∃r₂( Rath(r₂) ∧ r₁ galumphed-over r₂))

P4  Bungo is a rath.

P5  No borogoves will whiffle.  
     (∀x)(Borogove(x) → ¬Will-whiffle(x))

Nothing at all is said about whether toves or raths will whiffle.

Evaluating the six candidate statements

1. Only borogoves will whiffle and no borogoves will whiffle.  
   The second conjunct is guaranteed by P5, but the first (If something
   whiffles then it is a borogove) may or may not be true, depending on
   whether any rath or tove happens to whiffle.  Not forced.

2. Every borogove will whiffle.  
   Directly contradicted by P5.  Not forced (indeed impossible).

3. A borogove will whiffle.  
   Directly contradicted by P5.  Not forced (impossible).

4. Not all borogoves will whiffle.  
   P5 says none of them will, so certainly at least one borogove will
   fail to whiffle.  This statement is necessarily true.

5. Only borogoves will whiffle.  
   Could be true (vacuously, if nothing whiffles at all) or false (if a
   rath or tove whiffles).  Not forced.

6. Only borogoves will whiffle and not all borogoves will whiffle.  
   First part not forced, so the conjunction is not forced.

Conclusion

The one and only statement that must be true, given the premises, is:

4. Not all borogoves will whiffle.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. If Zindle didn't galumph over Bungo, then Zindle is mimsy. Bungo isn't a borogove if and only if Zindle didn't snicker-snacked Bungo. Zindle didn't snicker-snacked Bungo if and only if no toves snicker-snacked Zindle.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Bungo isn't a borogove if and only if no toves snicker-snacked Zindle.
2. No toves snicker-snacked Zindle if and only if every borogove snicker-snacked Bungo.
3. Zindle and Bungo are mimsy.
4. Not all toves snicker-snacked Zindle if and only if every borogove snicker-snacked Bungo.
5. Bungo isn't a borogove just in case not all toves snicker-snacked Zindle.
6. Zindle is mimsy.
results.model_response:
From the premisses we have  

P2 Bungo is not a borogove  ↔  Zindle didn’t snicker-snack Bungo  
P3 Zindle didn’t snicker-snack Bungo  ↔  no tove snicker-snacked Zindle  

Because equivalence is transitive,

 Bungo is not a borogove  ↔  no tove snicker-snacked Zindle.   (★)

Statement 1 is exactly (★), so it must be true.

None of the other proposed statements is forced:

• 2–5 add new, unrelated clauses about who snicker-snacked whom; models can be built in which they are true or false while still satisfying all premisses and (★).  
• 3 and 6 require Zindle (and Bungo) to be mimsy, yet the only rule concerning “mimsy” is “If Zindle didn’t galumph over Bungo then Zindle is mimsy”; if Zindle does galumph over Bungo, Zindle need not be mimsy at all.

Hence the sole statement that must hold in every situation described is:

1. Bungo isn't a borogove if and only if no toves snicker-snacked Zindle.

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. A borogove galumphed over every rath that galumphed over it. Only borogoves are beamish and no toves chortled at Rafin. If every tove chortled at Bungo, then not all borogoves galumphed over Bungo. If every rath chortled at Rafin, then Rafin is beamish only if every rath galumphed over Rafin. Zindle isn't beamish but it is not the case that Rafin isn't beamish.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Rafin is a borogove.
2. Zindle is beamish.
3. Rafin isn't a borogove.
4. Zindle and Rafin are beamish.
5. Rafin and Zindle are beamish.
6. Zindle and Bungo are beamish.
results.model_response:
First collect the explicit information.

1. Classification  
   – Everything is either a tove, a borogove, or a rath (mutually exclusive, jointly exhaustive; at least one of each exists).

2. Beamish  
   – Only borogoves are beamish.  
     (beamish → borogove)

3. Chortling  
   – No tove chortled at Rafin.  
     (tove(x) → ¬chortled-at(x , Rafin))

4. Galumphing  
   – A borogove galumphed over every rath that galumphed over it.  
     (borogove(b) ∧ rath(r) ∧ galumphed-over(r , b) → galumphed-over(b , r))

5. Two conditional statements (ordinary “if … then …” sentences)  
   a. If every tove chortled at Bungo, then not all borogoves galumphed over Bungo.  
   b. If every rath chortled at Rafin, then (Rafin is beamish → every rath galumphed over Rafin).

6. Explicit facts about Zindle and Rafin  
   – Zindle isn’t beamish.   (¬beamish(Zindle))  
   – It is not the case that Rafin isn’t beamish ⇒ Rafin is beamish.   (beamish(Rafin))

-------------------------------------------------
Logical consequences
-------------------------------------------------

A. From “beamish(Rafin)” and “beamish → borogove” we get  
   borogove(Rafin).

   So Rafin is definitely a borogove.

B. Nothing else forces Zindle’s classification; the only thing fixed about Zindle is ¬beamish(Zindle).

C. The two long conditional sentences need not be applied at all—their antecedents may simply be false—so they add no unconditional obligations.

D. It is easy to construct a model satisfying all the given sentences in which  
   – Rafin is a (beamish) borogove,  
   – Zindle is a non-beamish tove (or rath),  
   – none of the other listed statements besides “Rafin is a borogove” is true.

Hence we have found that

• Statement 1 “Rafin is a borogove” must hold in every situation compatible with the premises.  
• Statements 2–6 can all fail (they are not forced by the premises).

-------------------------------------------------
Answer
-------------------------------------------------
Only statement 1 must be true; none of the others is guaranteed.

A borogove chortled at Zindle just in case not all borogoves galumphed over Rafin.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. No borogoves chortled at a borogove.
2. No borogoves chortled at every borogove.
3. No borogoves chortled at a borogove that chortled at it.
4. A borogove chortled at every borogove.
5. Zindle is a tove.
6. There is not a borogove that every borogove chortled at.
results.model_response:
First translate the information into first–order logic.

Let  
T(x) ≡ “x is a tove”,  
B(x) ≡ “x is a borogove”,  
R(x) ≡ “x is a rath”,  
C(x,y) ≡ “x chortled at y”,  
G(x) ≡ “x galumphed over Rafin”,  
Z ≡ Zindle.

Premises

1. Every borogove chortled at every borogove  
   ∀x∀y (B(x) ∧ B(y) → C(x,y)).

2. A tove chortled at every rath that chortled at it  
   ∀x (T(x) → ∀y (R(y) ∧ C(y,x) → C(x,y))).

3. A tove chortled at a borogove that chortled at a borogove  
   ∀x (T(x) → ∃y (B(y) ∧ ∃z (B(z) ∧ C(y,z)) ∧ C(x,y))).

4. Every borogove chortled at every tove that chortled at every borogove  
   ∀x (B(x) → ∀y ((T(y) ∧ ∀z (B(z) → C(y,z))) → C(x,y))).

5. A borogove chortled at Zindle ⇔ not all borogoves galumphed over Rafin  
   (∃x (B(x) ∧ C(x,Z))) ↔ ¬∀x (B(x) → G(x)).

There is at least one tove, one borogove and one rath, and each creature is exactly one of those kinds.

––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
What the premises force

Premise 1 already gives: for every borogove b, and every borogove b′,
C(b,b′).  
In particular, for any fixed borogove b, every borogove chortles at b.

Hence these two facts are unavoidable in every model satisfying the premises:

• There exists a borogove that chortled at every borogove (indeed all borogoves do).  
• Therefore it is false that “no borogoves chortled at every borogove”, “no borogoves chortled at a borogove”, “no borogoves chortled at a borogove that chortled at it”, or “there is not a borogove that every borogove chortled at”.

Nothing in the premises settles anything about Zindle’s species or about galumphing; they can be arranged either way, so statement 5 is not forced.

––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Checking each candidate conclusion

1. No borogoves chortled at a borogove. Contradicted by premise 1 ⇒ NOT forced.  
2. No borogoves chortled at every borogove. Contradicted by premise 1 ⇒ NOT forced.  
3. No borogoves chortled at a borogove that chortled at it.  
   From premise 1 any two borogoves chortle at each other, so this is false ⇒ NOT forced.  
4. A borogove chortled at every borogove.  
   Premise 1 guarantees this (take any borogove) ⇒ FORCED.  
5. Zindle is a tove.  
   Zindle can consistently be a tove, a borogove, or a rath ⇒ NOT forced.  
6. There is not a borogove that every borogove chortled at.  
   Premise 1 ensures there is such a borogove ⇒ statement is false ⇒ NOT forced.

––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Final answer

The only statement that must be true in every situation satisfying the given information is:

4. A borogove chortled at every borogove.

Not all toves will whiffle but it is not the case that Bungo didn't snicker-snacked Bungo. Not all borogoves will whiffle and only toves are mimsy.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Only borogoves will whiffle and not all toves will whiffle.
2. Not all toves will whiffle and no borogoves will whiffle.
3. Not all toves will whiffle and not all borogoves will whiffle.
4. Not all borogoves will whiffle and every tove will whiffle.
5. Every tove will whiffle and no borogoves will whiffle.
6. Rafin will whiffle.
results.model_response:
What we can read directly from the premisses.

A.  “Not all toves will whiffle.”  
   So ∃x (Tove(x) ∧ ¬Whiffle(x)).

B.  “Not all borogoves will whiffle.”  
   So ∃x (Borogove(x) ∧ ¬Whiffle(x)).

C.  “Only toves are mimsy” together with “everything is either a tove, a borogove or a rath (exclusively)” gives  
   No borogove is mimsy.  

D.  From C and the conditional  
      If no borogoves are mimsy → Bungo didn’t snicker-snack Rafin  
   we get that Bungo didn’t snicker-snack Rafin.  
   (This has no bearing on whiffling, but shows the
   premisses are self-consistent.)

Now test each candidate statement.

1.  “Only borogoves will whiffle …”  
    Not forced; we are told only that some borogoves do not whiffle, not that every whiffler must be a borogove.  
    So 1 is not guaranteed.

2.  “… no borogoves will whiffle.”  
    We know only that some borogoves don’t whiffle, so 2 need not hold.

3.  “Not all toves will whiffle and not all borogoves will whiffle.”  
    Both parts are exactly A and B, which are premisses.  
    Therefore 3 must be true.

4.  “Every tove will whiffle.”  
    Contradicts A, so 4 cannot be true.

5.  Same fatal clash with A, so 5 cannot be true.

6.  Nothing in the premisses settles whether Rafin whiffles, so 6 is not forced.

Conclusion

The only statement that must be true in every model satisfying the premisses is statement 3.

Final answer: 3

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. A borogove chortled at Zindle. A tove galumphed over every tove. Bungo chortled at Bungo and no borogoves will burble.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. A borogove will burble.
2. Zindle and Bungo will burble.
3. Zindle will burble.
4. Every borogove will burble.
5. Only borogoves will burble.
6. Not all borogoves will burble.
results.model_response:
Premises (formalised)

1. Every creature is exactly one of: tove (T), borogove (B) or rath (R); each class is non-empty.  
2. Some borogove chortled at Zindle.  
3. ∃t [T(t) ∧ ∀u (T(u) → GalumphedOver(t,u))].  
4. C(Bungo,Bungo).  
5. ∀x [B(x) → ¬Burble(x)]    (“No borogoves will burble”)

Required: which listed sentences are logically forced by 1–5.

The six candidates

1. ∃x [B(x) ∧ Burble(x)]                     “A borogove will burble.”  
2. Burble(Zindle) ∧ Burble(Bungo).  
3. Burble(Zindle).  
4. ∀x [B(x) → Burble(x)].  
5. ∀x [Burble(x) → B(x)]                     “Only borogoves will burble.”  
6. ¬∀x [B(x) → Burble(x)] ≡ ∃x [B(x) ∧ ¬Burble(x)].

Analysis

Premise 5 already tells us that every borogove fails to burble.  
Because (Premise 1) there exists at least one borogove, we have

∃x [B(x) ∧ ¬Burble(x)].

That statement is exactly item 6, so 6 is true in every model of the premises.

• Item 1 contradicts premise 5, so it cannot be true.  
• Item 4 also contradicts premise 5, so it cannot be true.  
• Items 2 and 3 talk about Zindle and Bungo; the premises say nothing about whether either of them burbles, so these items can be either true or false in different models—they are not forced.  
• Item 5 is not forced either: if nobody at all burbles it is true, but we can also build a model where a tove burbles (premises permit this), making item 5 false.

Conclusion

The only statement that must be true is

6. Not all borogoves will burble.

All other listed statements are either false or undetermined.

results.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Every rath galumphed over every rath. No borogoves gimbled just in case not all toves gyred. Not all toves gyred if and only if not all borogoves galumphed over Rafin.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. No borogoves gimbled if and only if not all borogoves galumphed over Rafin.
2. Rafin gyred.
3. Rafin didn't gimble.
4. Rafin is a tove.
5. Rafin gimbled.
6. Rafin didn't gyre.
results.model_response:
Let  

A :  No borogoves gimbled.  
B :  Not all toves gyred.  
C :  Not all borogoves galumphed over Rafin.

The information that is given contains the two biconditionals  

(1) No borogoves gimbled just in case not all toves gyred.      A ↔ B  
(2) Not all toves gyred iff not all borogoves galumphed over Rafin.  B ↔ C  

Because the biconditional relation is transitive, from A ↔ B and B ↔ C we immediately obtain  

A ↔ C :  No borogoves gimbled iff not all borogoves galumphed over Rafin.  

That sentence is exactly statement 1, so statement 1 must be true in every situation that satisfies the premises.

To see that none of the remaining statements (2 – 6) is forced, build two concrete models of the information.

Model I  
• Rafin is a tove that gyred and gimbled.  
• There is a second tove that did not gyre (so B is true).  
• Hence, by the biconditionals, no borogove gimbled and some borogove failed to galumphe over Rafin.  
• All raths galumphed over every rath, as required.  

In this model  
2 true, 3 false, 4 true, 5 true, 6 false.

Model II  
• Rafin is a borogove that did not gyre and did not gimble.  
• All toves gyred (so B is false).  
• Therefore some borogove (a different one) gimbled and every borogove galumphed over Rafin.  

Here  
2 false, 3 true, 4 false, 5 false, 6 true.

Because each of statements 2 – 6 is true in one model and false in the other, none of them is logically forced by the premises.

Hence the only statement that must be true is:

Statement 1.

   (¬∃x∃y [B(x) ∧ B(y) ∧ S(x,y)]).

6. A borogove snicker-snacked every borogove.

   Both conjuncts are true in every model of the premises, so the whole statement is forced.  
   Entailed.

5. “If Zindle didn’t chortle at Rafin, then (every rath snicker-snacked Bungo only if every rath chortled at Rafin).”  
   Build a model where  
     – Zindle does not chortle at Rafin (making the antecedent true),  
     – every rath snicker-snacks Bungo,  
     – some rath fails to chortle at Rafin.  
   All premises are still satisfied, yet the consequent is false, so the whole conditional is false in that model.  
   Not entailed.

6. “Zindle didn’t snicker-snack Bungo just in case (i.e. iff) no raths chortled at Rafin.”  
   The premises impose no link between those two facts; one can construct models where one side holds and the other doesn’t.  
   Not entailed.

Result

The only statement that must be true given the premises is statement 4.

Final answer: 4.

Consider the following situation:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Rafin is a tove. Rafin galumphed over Bungo. Every rath galumphed over Zindle.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Not all toves galumphed over Bungo.
2. Bungo galumphed over every tove.
3. Every tove galumphed over Bungo.
4. No toves galumphed over Bungo.
5. A tove galumphed over Bungo.
6. Bungo galumphed over a tove.
results.model_response:
Premises  

P1. Everything is exactly one of: tove, borogove or rath, and there is at least one of each.  
P2. Rafin is a tove.  
P3. Rafin galumphed-over Bungo.  
P4. Every rath galumphed-over Zindle.  

Let us test each candidate statement against every interpretation that satisfies the premises.

1. “Not all toves galumphed over Bungo.”  
   – The premises give no information about what the other toves (besides Rafin) do.  
   – There are models in which every tove galumphs over Bungo and models in which some don’t.  
   – Therefore this statement is not forced.

2. “Bungo galumphed over every tove.”  
3. “Every tove galumphed over Bungo.”  
6. “Bungo galumphed over a tove.”  
   – None of the premises says anything about Bungo acting as the subject of ‘galumphed over’; they can be true or false at will.  
   – Hence 2, 3 and 6 are not forced.

4. “No toves galumphed over Bungo.”  
   – Directly contradicted by P2 + P3 (Rafin is a tove who did).  
   – So 4 cannot be true in any model of the premises.

5. “A tove galumphed over Bungo.”  
   – Rafin is a tove (P2) and Rafin galumphed over Bungo (P3).  
   – Therefore this statement is true in every model of the premises.

Conclusion  

The only statement that must be true is:

5. A tove galumphed over Bungo.

3. Every borogove is mimsy just in case not all borogoves are mimsy.
4. A tove galumphed over every rath.
5. Every tove galumphed over a rath.
6. A tove galumphed over a rath.
results.model_response:
Premises

1. Every object is (exclusively) either a tove, a borogove, or a rath, and there is at least one of each.
2. Bungo is a tove.
3. No tove galumphed over a rath. (∀x)(Tove(x) → (∀y)(Rath(y) → ¬Galumphed(x,y)))
4. If some borogove snicker-snacks Bungo, then every tove chortles at Bungo.

Needed: which of the six candidate statements is forced (must be true) by those premises.

Analysis of each candidate

1. Every tove galumphed over every rath.
   Premise 3 directly says the opposite (no such galumping ever occurs).  Statement 1 is therefore impossible, hence not forced.

2. There is not a rath that every tove galumphed over.
   Suppose, for contradiction, there were a rath R such that every tove galumphed over R.  Then in particular some tove would galump over a rath, contradicting Premise 3.  Therefore such a rath cannot exist; statement 2 is necessarily true.

3. Every borogove is mimsy just in case not all borogoves are mimsy.
   “Just in case” means “iff”.  This is the biconditional
        (∀x)(Borogove(x) → Mimsy(x))  ↔  ¬(∀x)(Borogove(x) → Mimsy(x))
   which is logically equivalent to P ↔ ¬P, always false no matter what the premises say.  It is certainly not forced to be true.

4. A tove galumphed over every rath. (∃t)(Tove(t) ∧ (∀y)(Rath(y) → Galumphed(t,y)))
   Premise 3 rules out any tove galumping over any rath, so this is impossible; not forced.

5. Every tove galumphed over a rath. (∀x)(Tove(x) → (∃y)(Rath(y) ∧ Galumphed(x,y)))
   Again excluded by Premise 3; not forced.

6. A tove galumphed over a rath. (∃x)(Tove(x) ∧ (∃y)(Rath(y) ∧ Galumphed(x,y)))
   Also ruled out by Premise 3; not forced.

Conclusion

The only statement that must be true given the premises is:

2. There is not a rath that every tove galumphed over.

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Rafin chortled at Zindle and a rath galumphed over Zindle. A borogove galumphed over Rafin or every borogove chortled at Bungo. Bungo chortled at Zindle and not all toves galumphed over Zindle.

3. A rath galumphed over Bungo only if a rath chortled at Zindle.
4. Bungo chortled at Bungo and a rath galumphed over Zindle.
5. If Zindle didn't galumph over Bungo, then every rath chortled at Bungo only if no raths galumphed over Bungo.
6. Bungo didn't chortle at Bungo and a rath galumphed over Zindle.
results.model_response:
Premises (paraphrased)

P1  Every creature is exactly one of: tove, borogove or rath; at least one of each exists.  
P2  Rafin chortled at Zindle.  
P3  A rath galumphed over Zindle.  
P4  Either (i) a borogove galumphed over Rafin, or (ii) every borogove chortled at Bungo.  
P5  Bungo chortled at Zindle.  
P6  Not all toves galumphed over Zindle.

What the six candidate statements say

1. (∃ rath x)(x galumphs Bungo) → (∃ rath y)(y chortles Zindle)  
3. is the same statement, expressed with “only if”.

2. Bungo chortled at Zindle ∧ a rath galumphed over Zindle.

4. Bungo chortled at Bungo ∧ a rath galumped over Zindle.

5. ¬(Zindle galumphs Bungo) → [ (∀ rath x)(x chortles Bungo) → ¬(∃ rath y)(y galumphs Bungo) ].

6. ¬(Bungo chortles Bungo) ∧ a rath galumphed over Zindle.

Which statements are forced by the premises?

Statement 2  
The two conjuncts of (2) are exactly P5 and P3, both given as premises, so (2) is unconditionally true in every model of the premises.

Statements 1 and 3  
They can fail.  Build a model satisfying all premises in which  
– there is a rath r1 that galumphs over Bungo,  
– no rath chortles at Zindle.  
(For example: let Rafin be a borogove, Bungo a tove, r1 the only rath; let r1 galumph Bungo and Zindle, Rafin chortle Zindle and Bungo, Bungo chortle Zindle; the other conditions are easily met.)  
In that model the antecedent of 1/3 is true while the consequent is false, so 1 and 3 are not forced.

Statement 4  
Whether Bungo chortles at himself is completely unconstrained, so the conjunction in (4) need not hold.

Statement 5  
The statement is false whenever  
¬(Zindle galumphs Bungo)   (allowed by the premises),  
all raths chortle at Bungo, and  
some rath galumphs over Bungo.  
Such a model can be built alongside all the premises, so (5) is not forced.

Statement 6  
The second conjunct is guaranteed (P3) but the first conjunct is undecided; hence the whole conjunction is not forced.

Conclusion

The only statement that must be true given the premises is:

2. Bungo chortled at Zindle and a rath galumphed over Zindle.

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Every borogove is mimsy just in case no toves will burble. A borogove will burble but it is not the case that Zindle isn't mimsy. Every rath gyred and not all toves galumphed over Zindle.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Zindle gyred.
2. Zindle will burble.
3. Zindle won't burble.
4. Every rath gyred and no borogoves will burble.
5. A borogove will burble and every rath gyred.
6. Zindle didn't gyre.
results.model_response:
Premises (restated)

1. Every creature is exactly one of: tove, borogove, rath (and there is at least one of each).

2. (All borogoves are mimsy)   ↔   (No toves will burble).

3. A borogove will burble, and Zindle is mimsy.

4. Every rath gyred, and at least one tove failed to galumph over Zindle.

What can be inferred

• From 3 we have:  (∃x)(borogove(x) ∧ will-burble(x)).

• From 4 we have:  (∀x)(rath(x) → gyred(x)).

Nothing in the premises fixes
– which of the three kinds Zindle is,
– whether Zindle gyred,
– whether Zindle will burble,
– whether any toves will burble,
– whether all borogoves are mimsy.

Hence:

1. “Zindle gyred.”  
   Not forced: true if Zindle is a rath, false otherwise.

2. “Zindle will burble.”  
3. “Zindle won’t burble.”  
   Neither is forced; Zindle’s burbling status is undetermined.

4. “Every rath gyred and no borogoves will burble.”  
   First conjunct is true, second is false (since a borogove will burble), so the whole sentence is false.

5. “A borogove will burble and every rath gyred.”  
   Both conjuncts are exactly the two facts given in premises 3 and 4, so the whole sentence is unavoidably true.

6. “Zindle didn’t gyre.”  
   Also not forced; opposite of statement 1.

Therefore the only statement that must be true in every model of the premises is:

Statement 5.

If every rath chortled at Rafin, then not all toves galumphed over Zindle only if Zindle didn't galumph over Bungo.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

No toves snicker-snacked a rath.  
(formally ∀x∀y [Tove(x) ∧ Rath(y) → ¬SS(x , y)])

Nothing else in the premises adds any further requirement about who does or does not snicker-snack whom (the other premises concern galumphing and chortling, or give only conditional information about snicker-snacking by raths, not by toves).

• Because SS(t , r) is always false:
 – A tove cannot have snicker-snacked even one rath, let alone all raths.

results.question:

Everything is a tove, or a borogove, or a rath (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. A borogove is uffish but it is not the case that Rafin didn't chortle at Bungo. A rath snicker-snacked Zindle and every borogove is uffish. If Zindle didn't chortle at Rafin, then every borogove chortled at Bungo only if no raths galumphed over Bungo.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. A borogove is uffish and a rath snicker-snacked Zindle.
2. A rath is uffish and every borogove snicker-snacked Zindle.
3. Zindle is uffish.
4. Zindle and Rafin are uffish.
5. Zindle and Bungo are uffish.
6. Zindle isn't uffish.
results.model_response:
From the information given we can extract the following explicit facts.

1. There is at least one borogove and at least one rath.  
2. Every borogove is uffish (it is stated twice).  
3. There exists a rath that snicker-snacked Zindle (“A rath snicker-snacked Zindle”).  
4. Rafin did chortle at Bungo.  
5. The long conditional concerning Zindle, Rafin, borogoves and raths puts no extra unconditional requirement on who is or is not uffish.

Now check each candidate statement.

1. “A borogove is uffish and a rath snicker-snacked Zindle.”  
   • There is at least one borogove and every borogove is uffish, so such a borogove exists.  
   • Premise (3) guarantees the existence of a rath that snicker-snacked Zindle.  
   Both parts are therefore unconditionally true, so their conjunction is unconditionally true.

2. “A rath is uffish and every borogove snicker-snacked Zindle.”  
   – We are never told that any rath is uffish, nor that every borogove snicker-snacked Zindle.  
   – Either clause can be false in a model satisfying the premises.  
   Not forced to be true.

3. “Zindle is uffish.”  
4. “Zindle and Rafin are uffish.”  
5. “Zindle and Bungo are uffish.”  
6. “Zindle isn’t uffish.”  
   – The premises never determine whether Zindle (or Rafin, or Bungo) is a borogove, a rath or a tove, nor whether any of them is uffish.  
   – You can build one model in which Zindle is a borogove (hence uffish) and another in which Zindle is a tove (hence not uffish).  
   – Therefore none of statements 3-6 is forced, and 6 is no more compelled than 3-5.

Hence the only statement that must be true under every interpretation satisfying the premises is:

Statement 1.
